William Thomson, by contrast, championed precision measurement of well-defined quantities — resistance, current, electromotive force — using standardized instruments and Ohm's law. When the cable failed after just a few weeks of operation (partly due to damage from Whitehouse's use of massive induction coils that broke down the cable's insulation), Whitehouse was publicly cast out of both the scientific and engineering communities. The telegraph engineer W. H. Preece, writing as "A Telegraph Engineer and Practical Electrician," attacked Whitehouse in the *Engineer*, declaring that the profession must publicly disavow him to protect its own reputation. Preece noted pointedly that in Whitehouse's papers, "those most essential and important terms, 'quantity,' 'intensity,' and 'resistance,' are almost . . . lost sight of." Hunt uses the Whitehouse affair to show how the convergence of scientists' and engineers' measurement practices around Ohm's law and standardized units created a new regime in which those who refused to adopt shared standards were squeezed out entirely. Thomson, who presided over the actual death of the cable, emerged with his reputation enhanced because his measurement practices aligned with those being developed by the broader community.

**The British Association Committee on Electrical Standards and the Making of the Ohm (Chs. 4–5).** Beginning in 1861, a committee of the British Association for the Advancement of Science — featuring Thomson, Maxwell, Jenkin, and other leading figures — undertook to establish a universal system of electrical units grounded in Weber's "absolute" system of measurement. Hunt traces how this committee's work, driven by the cable industry's commercial need for reliable electrical specifications, became the vehicle through which Maxwell was drawn into practical measurement and led to formulate his field theory in new ways. Maxwell and Jenkin's 1863 paper "On the Elementary Relations Between Electrical Measurements" — largely overlooked by historians because it was omitted from Maxwell's collected papers — served as a crucial transition piece between his speculative "Physical Lines of Force" (1861–62) and his more measured "Dynamical Theory of the Electromagnetic Field" (1864). Working with Jenkin exposed Maxwell to engineering-style thinking focused on measurable macroscopic quantities rather than hypothetical microstructures, leading him to step back from his vortex model and formulate the general field equations in terms of relations among measurable quantities. The committee also faced off against Werner Siemens's competing mercury-based resistance standard, and the eventual triumph of the British Association unit (the ohm) was secured largely by persuading the cable industry to adopt it in contracts — beginning with the Malta–Alexandria cable contract of 1868\. Hunt shows how the ohm was not simply "discovered" but was a material and institutional achievement that required coordinated effort across scientific, engineering, and commercial communities, with the cable industry serving as the crucial market that gave the new standards their authority.

**Oliver Heaviside and the Making of "Maxwell's Equations" (Ch. 6).** Hunt's final major case traces how Oliver Heaviside, a former telegraph operator living in near-total isolation in Camden Town, reformulated Maxwell's theory into the compact vector equations that passed into the textbooks as "Maxwell's equations." Heaviside's path into electromagnetic theory came directly through cable telegraphy: his brother Arthur worked for the Great Northern Telegraph Company (a subsidiary of the Eastern Telegraph Company, owned by cable magnate John Pender), and Oliver's earliest papers addressed problems of telegraphic transmission. His 1876 paper "On the Extra Current" added a self-induction term to Thomson's cable equation, producing what became "the telegrapher's equation" — his first step toward conceiving of telegraph signals as electromagnetic waves rather than as diffusing currents. His breakthrough came in June 1884 when, working through Maxwell's expressions for electromagnetic energy and tracing how energy enters a conducting core, he derived the result that energy flow at any point equals the vector product of electric and magnetic force (S \= E × H). This implied that electrical energy flows through the insulating dielectric, not along the conducting wire — a "counterintuitive" result that was nonetheless natural for someone who, as Heaviside said, had "never swallowed the electric fluids." Hunt emphasizes that Heaviside published most of his work in *The Electrician*, a trade journal owned by Pender and Anderson of the Eastern Telegraph Company, illustrating how deeply the cable industry was embedded in the very venues through which Maxwell's theory was disseminated and reformulated.

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"The important applications of electromagnetism to telegraphy have . . . reacted on pure science by giving a commercial value to accurate electrical measurements, and by affording to electricians the use of apparatus on a scale which greatly transcends that of any ordinary laboratory." — James Clerk Maxwell, *Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism* (1873), quoted p. 4

"To put the point simply and a bit too baldly, the British did field theory because they had submarine cables, and the French and Germans did not because they had none." (p. 4\)

"Scientific knowledge is inescapably contingent: the picture scientists form of the world depends on the evidence they encounter and the weight they give to it. This evidence is typically mediated through various technologies — whether scientific instruments or technologies devised for more practical ends — that consequently shape the theories scientists (and others) form to account for what they see." (p. 274\)

"Whenever many persons are to act together, it is necessary that they should have a common understanding of the measures to be employed." — Maxwell and Jenkin, "On the Elementary Relations between Electrical Measurements" (1863), quoted p. 199

"In the perusal of Mr. Whitehouse's papers, those most essential and important terms, 'quantity,' 'intensity,' and 'resistance,' are almost . . . lost sight of." — W. H. Preece, *Engineer* (1858), quoted p. 108

"There was a time in my life when I was something like old Teufelsdröckh in his garret, and was in some measure satisfied or contented with a mere subsistence. But that was when I was making discoveries. It matters not what others may think of their significance. They were meat and drink and company to me." — Oliver Heaviside, quoted p. 264

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

Hunt's argument pairs naturally with several works across Matthew's reading lists. Galison's *Image and Logic* shares Hunt's attention to how material instruments and measurement practices shape theoretical knowledge, and the two books together demonstrate that the science-technology interaction operates at the level of instruments, not just ideas. Shapin and Schaffer's *Leviathan and the Air-Pump* offers a complementary argument about how experimental practices and their social organization determine what counts as knowledge — Hunt effectively extends this framework into the industrial-imperial context of Victorian telegraphy. Starosielski's *The Undersea Network* provides a twentieth-century counterpart, examining the material and geopolitical dimensions of undersea cable infrastructure. MacKenzie's *Inventing Accuracy* shares Hunt's interest in how measurement practices and standards are socially constructed rather than simply "discovered," though MacKenzie focuses on Cold War missile guidance rather than Victorian electromagnetism. Hughes's *Networks of Power* offers the large technical systems framework that Hunt implicitly engages but does not fully adopt, preferring a more granular focus on measurement practices and individual trajectories.

One limitation is that Hunt's focus on the science-technology nexus means he devotes relatively little attention to the labor conditions of cable laying, the ecological consequences of gutta-percha harvesting (mentioned briefly but not developed), or the experiences of colonized peoples at the receiving end of the imperial cable network. A second is that the book's deep immersion in the technical details of electromagnetic theory, while its great strength, may limit its accessibility to historians without some background in physics.

# **David Hochfelder, *The Telegraph in America, 1832–1920* (Johns Hopkins University Press, 2012\)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Hochfelder argues that the telegraph was revolutionary in two senses—as a paradigm shift in technological practice (from mechanical to electrical technology) and as a socially transformative medium—but that its actual social impact was shaped and constrained by the institutional, economic, and political contexts in which it operated. The telegraph's heyday lasted roughly from 1860 to 1920, a period when users actively adapted themselves and their institutions to the technology; its social effects were differential, affecting Americans unevenly by class, occupation, and region, and its ultimate fate depended as much on corporate strategy, regulatory politics, and social preferences as on technical capabilities.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Hochfelder positions his work against two prevalent tendencies in telegraph historiography: the technologically determinist narrative that treats the telegraph as an autonomous agent of social transformation, and the social constructivist tendency to dissolve technological agency entirely into social interests. He argues instead for a middle path that takes seriously the material attributes and capabilities of the technology while insisting that its social effects were mediated through institutions, corporate strategies, and political choices. The book engages directly with Richard R. John's *Network Nation* (2010), which Hochfelder acknowledges as the definitive account of American wire communications, and with Robert Luther Thompson's classic *Wiring a Continent* (1947), which covered the pre-1866 period. Hochfelder explicitly engages the technological determinism debate articulated in Merritt Roe Smith and Leo Marx's *Does Technology Drive History?* (1994), arguing that historians of technology must acknowledge that technology shapes society without treating it as the sole or inevitable cause of change. He also intervenes in the historiography of Gilded Age political economy by recovering the postal telegraph movement as a major but forgotten episode in American reform, one that illuminates the broader trajectory of the public ownership movement. The book pushes against James Carey's influential "Technology and Ideology" thesis about the telegraph's separation of communication from transportation by grounding that claim in detailed institutional and economic analysis, showing that the gap between the telegraph's theoretical promise and actual performance was shaped by Western Union's narrow vision of whom the technology should serve.

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Hochfelder employs a broadly contextual approach to the history of technology that draws on large technical systems (LTS) theory, network economics (particularly the concept of natural monopoly and network externalities), and the sociology of technological change. Key concepts mobilized include "industrial succession" (the process by which one technology supplants another), the distinction between communication and information media, and the psychological dimensions of technological change.

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

The book draws primarily on archival sources from the Western Union Telegraph Company Records at the Smithsonian's Lemelson Center (over 800 boxes), the New York Stock Exchange Archives, AT\&T Archives, and the personal papers of telegraph operators, managers, and reformers. It also uses congressional hearings and reports, company annual reports, trade publications, the Thomas Edison Papers, and contemporary periodicals. The geographic scope is primarily national (United States) with significant transatlantic comparison, and the temporal scope extends from the 1830s through the mid-twentieth century, with the densest treatment covering 1860–1920.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**The Civil War Telegraph and Civilian Control of the Military.** The conflict between the U.S. Military Telegraph Corps (USMT) under civilian superintendent Anson Stager and the Army Signal Corps under Albert Myer provides a richly detailed case study of how technology mediates power relationships. The USMT was a unique hybrid organization—its operators were civilians employed by commercial telegraph companies, yet they served under nominal military authority. Secretary of War Stanton used his control of the telegraph as what one biographer called "a perfect autocracy," sending telegrams so frequent and peremptory that generals formed a mental picture of Stanton watching their every move. This civilian control checked military power at a moment when General McClellan speculated he could "become Dictator." Yet the same hybrid structure produced endemic conflicts of interest: USMT superintendent Stager simultaneously ran Western Union's Cleveland office, speculated in gold using advance knowledge of war news, and steered the best operators to Western Union while shunting inferior ones to the military. When the USMT disbanded in 1866, it turned over 14,000 miles of line to commercial companies at bargain prices, with division superintendents like William Gross receiving gifts of stock from company president Norvin Green in exchange for favorable treatment. This case demonstrates Hochfelder's broader argument that the telegraph's centralizing and decentralizing tendencies operated simultaneously.

**The Stock Ticker, Bucket Shops, and Popular Participation in Financial Markets.** The invention of the stock ticker in 1866–1867 and its subsequent diffusion provides Hochfelder's most compelling case for how technology reshapes not only institutions but also the psychology of market participants. The ticker decoupled traders from exchange floors, allowing them to monitor markets from a distance—but this spatial liberation created a powerful psychological dependence. Daniel Drew, the famed speculator, was so addicted to the ticker that even after losing $2 million in 1869, he could not stop watching the tape; when he retired upstate, his son leased him a personal ticker so he could speculate with modest sums until his death. The ticker also spawned an entirely new institution: the bucket shop, a quasi-legal betting parlor where ordinary Americans wagered on ticker quotations without actually purchasing securities. By 1900, Western Union supplied quotations to approximately 1,600 offices nationwide, the majority of which were bucket shops. The resulting three-decade war between exchanges, telegraph companies, and bucket shops demonstrates how technological capabilities interact with legal and institutional frameworks. Exchanges oscillated between cutting off quotations entirely (which harmed their own members) and pressuring Western Union to police its customers (which the telegraph company was unwilling to do because bucket shops generated roughly a quarter of its ticker revenue). Only when the Chicago Board of Trade credibly threatened to build its own independent telegraph network in 1900–1901 did Western Union relent. This case powerfully illustrates how the same technology simultaneously modernized legitimate financial markets and democratized speculation through illegitimate channels.

**The Postal Telegraph Movement and the Failure of Public Ownership.** The fifty-year campaign (1866–1918) to place the telegraph under government control serves as a case study in the political economy of communication technology. Hochfelder recovers this nearly forgotten movement as one of the most heavily debated issues in Gilded Age and Progressive Era politics—nearly one hundred bills were introduced, forty-eight committee reports issued, and a petition signed by over two million Americans was presented to Congress in 1888\. Reformers argued that Western Union's monopoly distorted the flow of news, exploited workers, and denied ordinary Americans access to communication. Yet the movement failed for reasons that illuminate the structural barriers to utility reform: Western Union's exclusive railroad right-of-way contracts (upheld by the Supreme Court in 1904), its symbiotic relationship with the Associated Press, the different marginal cost structures of telegraphy and postal service (each telegram required two operators and monopolized a circuit, unlike mail), and the chronic deficits of the British postal telegraph. The movement's final defeat came during World War I when Postmaster General Albert Burleson's disastrous government administration of the wires—rates rose 20 percent, unions remained hostile, management became autocratic—killed the public ownership movement permanently. As Wilson adviser Joseph Tumulty wrote, government control "produced nothing but criticism and discontent."

**Western Union, Bell, and Industrial Succession.** The chapter on the telegraph-telephone relationship provides a detailed case of how one technology supplants another through a combination of technical capability, corporate strategy, and regulatory politics. The pivotal moment was the 1879 contract in which Western Union exited the telephone business in exchange for Bell's commitment not to enter telegraphy—a boundary that proved impossible to maintain. Western Union's technological conservatism proved self-fulfilling: its presidents between 1878 and 1909 regarded telegraphy as a mature business, adopting no significant innovation between Edison's quadruplex (1870s) and the multiplex printer (c. 1920). When AT\&T acquired working control of Western Union in 1909, Bell managers found the company's headquarters "looked like a page from Dickens"—important correspondence filed in an old wardrobe cabinet, financial reports piled on the floor. Theodore Vail's brief tenure modernizing Western Union (1909–1914) demonstrated what was possible: revenues increased 75 percent, wages rose 50 percent, and modern accounting was introduced. But AT\&T's forced divestiture under antitrust pressure in 1914 left Western Union fatally weakened. By 1930, Western Union was buying both its terminal equipment and its circuit capacity from AT\&T—its biggest competitor. The FCC then spent decades propping up the telegraph company as a token counterweight to Bell's monopoly, a regulatory dynamic that persisted until deregulation in the 1970s–1980s.

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"The electric telegraph forever liberated communication from transportation. All the social effects of telegraphy ultimately derive from that simple fact." (p. 3\)

"Individuals and institutions did not simply adopt the telegraph—they adapted themselves to it. Telegraphy did not fulfill its potential as a driver of social change until its users reshaped their actions, organizations, perceptions, and expectations around it." (p. 4\)

"The footsteps of all lead into the Western Union cave; not one has ever returned." —Bell Telephone president William H. Forbes on Western Union's absorption of competitors (p. 39\)

"The very life of the Street seems to depend upon accurate, immediate, and continuous quotations from the Stock Exchange." —Sereno S. Pratt, editor of the Wall Street Journal, 1903 (p. 117\)

"The ticker is always a treacherous servant. In the end it proved itself the master." —George Rutledge Gibson on the ticker's addictive hold over speculators (p. 118\)

"Federal abandonment of electrical communication to the private sector might well be the origin of today's cheapened political debate and impoverishment of the public sphere." (p. 72\)

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

Hochfelder's analysis of Western Union as natural monopoly and the postal telegraph debate pairs productively with Richard R. John's *Network Nation* (2010), which provides the definitive political history of American telecommunications, and with Thomas Hughes's *Networks of Power* (1983), whose large technical systems framework illuminates how network technologies tend toward consolidation. The ticker and financial markets chapter connects to Donald MacKenzie's *An Engine, Not a Camera* (2008) on how technical instruments actively shape the markets they purport merely to observe. The analysis of the telegraph's effects on journalism and written language complements Stephen Kern's *The Culture of Time and Space* (1983) on how communication technologies restructured temporal and spatial experience.

The book's primary limitation is its relatively thin engagement with the social history of telegraph operators and users beyond the elite level—the labor history of telegraphers, women in telegraphy, and the experiences of ordinary Americans who encountered the telegraph primarily through newspapers receive less sustained attention than corporate strategy and regulatory politics. Additionally, while Hochfelder gestures toward the telegraph's significance as an information medium (as distinct from a communication medium), this important distinction could have been developed more fully as an analytical framework throughout the book.

# **Susan J. Douglas, *Inventing American Broadcasting, 1899–1922* (Johns Hopkins University Press, 1987\)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Douglas argues that radio broadcasting did not suddenly emerge in the 1920s as a revolutionary technology but was instead the product of a twenty-three-year process (1899–1922) of social construction in which individuals, institutions, and press representations interacted to determine how the technology would be used, by whom, and to what ends. The "basic technological, managerial, and cultural template of American broadcasting" was cast during this supposed "pre-history," as inventors, corporations, the U.S. Navy, amateur operators, and the popular press all competed to define what wireless meant and who had legitimate claims to the electromagnetic spectrum.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Douglas intervenes against a historiography of broadcasting that begins in the 1920s with KDKA and the radio boom, treating the preceding decades as mere background. She challenges standard accounts—particularly Gleason Archer's *History of Radio to 1926* (1938), which she argues uncritically reproduced RCA propaganda—that present broadcasting as the inevitable product of corporate foresight and technological momentum. Against both economic determinism (which makes communications corporations appear "more prescient than they actually were") and technological determinism (which "grants the technology a life of its own"), Douglas insists on a social constructionist approach that foregrounds the contingency of radio's development. She draws on the emerging social construction of technology (SCOT) framework associated with Bijker, Hughes, and Pinch, but gives it a distinctive cultural-studies inflection by insisting that press representations and ideological frameworks were not passive reflections of technological change but active forces shaping it. Her most original contribution is treating journalistic coverage as a constitutive "arena" alongside technology and business strategy, arguing that how wireless was talked about in public directly shaped what it became. She positions herself as bridging the history of technology (citing Merritt Roe Smith, Thomas Hughes, David Noble) and American studies/media studies (Berger and Luckmann, Todd Gitlin, Herbert Gans), noting that "too few historians of technology have studied the mass media, and too few media scholars have concentrated on media portrayals of technology."

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Social construction of technology combined with cultural history and ideology critique. Douglas identifies three interconnected "arenas" that together shaped radio's development: technology (patents, apparatus design), business strategy (corporate organization, market positioning), and the press (journalistic conventions, public representations, ideological legitimation). She draws on concepts of cultural hegemony and legitimation to analyze how romantic press narratives about inventor-heroes masked processes of corporate consolidation, and how myths of democratic access obscured the corporate acquisition of cultural authority over the airwaves.

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

Archival research in major radio history collections, including the George H. Clark "Radioana" Collection at the Smithsonian, the Reginald Fessenden Papers at the North Carolina State Archives, the Marconi Company Archives in Chelmsford, England, and private letters held by Marconi's daughter. Douglas also conducts extensive content analysis of popular press coverage in newspapers (*New York Times*, *New York Herald*), magazines (*Scientific American*, *McClure's*, *Outlook*, *Literary Digest*), children's publications (*St. Nicholas*), and technical journals (*Electrical World*, *Electrician and Mechanic*). Geographic scope is primarily the United States with significant attention to British-American-German competition; temporal scope is 1899–1922.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**Marconi and the America's Cup, 1899\.** Douglas's opening case study examines Marconi's first major American appearance, when he demonstrated wireless telegraphy during the October 1899 yacht races in New York harbor. Douglas shows how the press constructed Marconi as an "inventor-hero," arriving simultaneously with the war hero Admiral Dewey, and wrapped the new technology in romantic, nationalist language that made it seem both revolutionary and reassuringly familiar. Crucially, Douglas demonstrates that the press coverage did not merely report on Marconi's demonstration—it actively shaped public understanding of wireless by casting it within existing narrative conventions (the lone inventor-genius, the conquest of nature, democratic progress through technology). The press legitimated Marconi's particular vision of wireless as point-to-point commercial communication controlled by private enterprise, while alternative possibilities were never articulated. The episode reveals Douglas's core analytical method: showing how journalistic conventions mediated between technological possibility and social outcome, and how the "romantic rhetoric" applied to new inventions helped "legitimate the private, corporate control of machines as the only equitable and progressive method of management."

**The Amateur Operators and Populist Technology, 1906–1912.** Douglas devotes her most culturally rich chapter to the amateur wireless operators—primarily young, white, middle-class boys and men who built homemade wireless stations from scavenged materials (Quaker Oats containers as tuning coils, Model T ignition coils as transmitters, brass bedstead spheres as spark gaps, stolen public telephone handsets as headphones). By 1912, the *New York Times* estimated several hundred thousand active amateurs in America. Douglas treats this as a profoundly significant social phenomenon: the amateurs represented a "populist" vision of technology in which access to the electromagnetic spectrum was democratized and decentralized, standing in direct opposition to both corporate and military claims. She connects the amateur movement to anxieties about masculinity in the Progressive Era, showing how wireless tinkering offered young men a way to reconcile competing definitions of manhood—physical mastery and technical expertise—in an increasingly bureaucratized world. The amateurs' story is also one of resistance: they fought the Navy in the airwaves, told naval operators to "beat it," and relayed messages the military couldn't. The press celebrated them as "boy inventor-heroes" in the Horatio Alger tradition. Yet their ultimate significance was paradoxical: by establishing a mass audience of listeners, they laid the groundwork for commercial broadcasting, which would co-opt and extinguish the very decentralized, participatory vision they embodied.

**The Titanic Disaster and Radio Regulation, 1912\.** The sinking of the Titanic in April 1912 serves as Douglas's pivotal case study for understanding how catastrophe catalyzed regulatory action. The disaster exposed every inadequacy of the existing wireless system: the Californian, only ten miles away, had shut down its wireless for the night; the Carpathia had limited range; amateur interference and garbled messages produced the cruel false report that the Titanic was being towed safely to Halifax. Douglas shows how the disaster transformed the terms of the public debate over the electromagnetic spectrum as a "common property resource." Before the Titanic, Congress had been unable to resolve competing claims from the military, commercial operators, and amateurs; afterward, the 1912 Radio Act was passed swiftly, restricting amateurs to 200 meters and below (wavelengths then believed to be commercially useless) while giving preferred spectrum to the military and corporations. Douglas frames this as a case study in how "the tragedy of the commons" was resolved in ways that systematically favored institutional power over individual access, establishing the regulatory template that would persist through the Radio Act of 1927 and the Communications Act of 1934\.

**The Formation of RCA and the Corporate Co-optation of Broadcasting, 1919–1922.** Douglas traces how the U.S. Navy orchestrated the creation of the Radio Corporation of America in 1919 to prevent the British Marconi Company from acquiring GE's Alexanderson alternator and establishing an international monopoly. The Navy's Admiral Bullard and Commander Hooper persuaded GE to terminate its negotiations with Marconi, and within months RCA was incorporated, absorbing American Marconi's entire infrastructure. Douglas emphasizes the striking absence of press attention: "not one article on the subject listed in the Reader's Guide for 1919 or 1920." This institutional watershed was invisible to the public even as the Frank Conrad/KDKA story unfolded: a Westinghouse employee's amateur broadcasts from his garage station 8XK evolved into scheduled programming, a department store advertised ten-dollar receiving sets, and Westinghouse vice-president Harry P. Davis grasped that radio's "field was really one of wide publicity." Douglas argues that by the time the 1922 "radio boom" swept America, the critical precedents—corporate control of technology, regulatory marginalization of non-commercial users, and an ideological framework equating consumption with democratic participation—were already locked in place.

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"This book, then, is about the social construction of radio. . . . just as individuals and institutions, and the interactions between them, influenced the course of technical adaptation, so did journalistic portrayals of radio's promise and significance." (p. xvi)

"Technology is as much a process as a thing; it is an evolving relationship between people and their environment which is affected by more than one factor." (pp. xxviii–xxix)

"It is important to point out that this was primarily a white, middle-class, male construction, a process from which most women and minorities were excluded." (p. xxix)

"The ramifications of the introduction of the crystal detectors cannot be overemphasized. The crystals contributed more than any other component to the democratization of wireless, the concomitant wireless boom, and the radio boom of the 1920s." (p. 196\)

"In this case, although the concept of social construction has been quite valuable, the word *construction* itself is misleading. It suggests a more cooperative process than occurred with radio. Radio broadcasting . . . was the result of battles over technological control and corporate hegemony, and of visions about who should have access to America's newly discovered frontier environment, the electromagnetic spectrum." (p. 318\)

"References to democracy and to audience participation equated consumption with power. The early 1920s may have been the historical period when the myth of audience power, which rested on the myth of consumer choice, became reified and held up as evidence that Americans possessed unprecedented political and economic freedom." (p. 321\)

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

Douglas's work pairs productively with Thomas Hughes's *Networks of Power* (1983), which provides the large technical systems framework for understanding how corporate actors build and extend technological networks—though Douglas's cultural analysis of press representation goes well beyond Hughes's institutional focus. Raymond Williams's *Television: Technology and Cultural Form* (1974) offers a complementary theoretical framework for thinking about how broadcasting technologies are shaped by social forces rather than determining social outcomes, and Williams's concept of "mobile privatization" resonates with Douglas's analysis of how radio moved entertainment into the home. Fred Turner's *From Counterculture to Cyberculture* (2006) extends Douglas's story of amateur enthusiasm being co-opted by corporate interests into the digital age, making a similar argument about how countercultural visions of decentralized, participatory media were absorbed by Silicon Valley capitalism.

The book's primary limitation is its relatively thin treatment of race: Douglas acknowledges this was a "white, middle-class, male construction" but does not substantially explore how racial exclusion shaped the technology or its meanings. Additionally, the cultural-studies analysis of press representation, while innovative for 1987, sometimes treats "the press" as more monolithic than it was, underplaying regional variation and the diversity of journalistic voices.

# **Simone M. Müller, *Wiring the World: The Social and Cultural Creation of Global Telegraph Networks* (Columbia University Press, 2016\)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Müller argues that the global submarine telegraph network was not simply a product of imperial strategy or technological determinism but was shaped by the social and cultural visions of a specific Euro-American actor network—the "Class of 1866"—whose competing ideas about commerce, civilization, peace, and global connectivity determined the structure, scope, and exclusivity of the world's first instantaneous communication system. By centering these actors and their diverse, often contradictory visions of *Weltcommunication*, Müller demonstrates that globalization processes in the nineteenth century were driven as much by social and cultural practices as by economic imperialism and geopolitics.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Müller intervenes directly against two dominant frameworks in the history of submarine telegraphy. The first is the imperial-nationalist paradigm, exemplified by Daniel Headrick's *Tools of Empire* (1981) and Harold Innis's *Empire and Communications* (1950), which treats cables as instruments of British imperial control and assigns them distinct national identities and implicit nationalist agendas. Müller charges this tradition with "methodological nationalism" that underplays the transnational financing, working agreements, and cosmopolitan identities of the cable actors themselves. The second target is the "Victorian Internet" thesis, popularized by Tom Standage, which analogizes the telegraph to modern mass communication. Müller dismantles this analogy by showing that submarine telegraphy was never a means of mass or social communication but remained an exclusive medium for an elite clientele, with exorbitant tariffs enforcing sharp divisions of class, race, and gender. In place of these narratives, Müller proposes a global history approach informed by postcolonial theory, actor-network analysis, and the concept of "technology-in-use." She argues that the micro-level social and cultural practices of the cable actors—their professional identities, competing market ideologies, visions of civilization, and strategic deployment of nationalism—must be analyzed alongside macrostructural processes to understand how the global communication system actually took shape. Her framework of "strategic nationalism" is particularly original: she demonstrates that cable companies adopted national identities instrumentally and situationally, presenting themselves as British, American, or German depending on context, while operating fundamentally transnational enterprises.

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Müller combines global history methodology (drawing on Sebastian Conrad and Jürgen Osterhammel) with an actor-network approach adapted from science and technology studies, emphasizing "technology-in-use" over technological determinism. Key concepts include *Weltcommunication* (Ernst Kapp's term for the imagined global communicative body), the "Class of 1866" (the gatekeeper network formed by the Atlantic cable project), "strategic nationalism" (the instrumental adoption of national identity by transnational actors), *l'esprit d'internationalité* (the legal-diplomatic framework for governing a global system), and "cable diplomacy" (the informal mediation performed by cable operators between nation-states).

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

Müller draws on archives in over twenty institutions across six countries, including the Cable and Wireless Archives at Porthcurno, the BT Archives, the National Archives in London, Paris, Washington, and Ottawa, the Siemens Archives Munich, and the ITU Archives. She makes particularly inventive use of shareholder lists (Annual Lists of Members required by the Companies Act), personal diaries (Emma Pender's correspondence, Daniel Gooch's diary), cable station letter books (Heart's Content), operator correspondence (George Kennan's letters from Siberia), and material culture such as lithographs, frescoes, and commemorative objects. The temporal scope runs from 1854 to 1914, with primary geographic focus on the North Atlantic world and secondary attention to Caribbean, South Asian, and Pacific cable routes.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**The Pender-Siemens Cable War (1870s).** The rivalry between John Pender's Eastern and Associated Companies and William Siemens's Direct United States Cable Company (DUSC) constitutes one of the book's richest case studies. Pender, a Manchester cotton merchant, represented a system of monopoly aiming at capital accumulation; Siemens, a German émigré engineer, stood for a system of competition allowing for technological progress. Their conflict was not merely a business rivalry but a "drama of progress" over the fundamental structure of the global communication system—and by extension, a contest between the businessman and the engineer-scientist for authority over the telegraph enterprise. Pender's 1877 victory installed a monopolistic market system and, crucially, disconnected the engineer from the enterprise of ocean telegraphy. As Pender put it, scientists were "all very well in their place, but their place was in the laboratory, or at any rate not in the directory of big business." The consequence was technological stagnation: submarine telegraphy changed very little over the following decades, and when it was finally supplanted, it was by an entirely different technology (wireless) rather than by a more innovative competitor. Müller reads this outcome against the broader historiographic debate about British industrial "decline," showing how the case simultaneously supports and complicates claims of "entrepreneurial failure."

**Women as Shareholders and Actors of Globalization.** Through a systematic analysis of the DUSC's complete shareholder lists from 1873 to 1909, Müller reveals a dramatic and previously invisible transformation: women went from 3.7 percent of shareholders in 1873 to 44 percent by 1909\. The shift correlates with the Married Women's Property Act of 1882, which allowed women to hold property independently. By the early twentieth century, Comtesse Isabelle Gontran de la Baume-Pluvinel was the largest individual shareholder, man or woman. Spinsters consistently made up the majority among female investors, suggesting that stock investment functioned as a means of economic independence for women denied access to professions. Müller argues that submarine telegraphy's unusual public visibility—cable breakages and layings were covered extensively in the daily press—made it uniquely accessible to women investors who were excluded from the clubs and fraternal lodges where most market intelligence circulated. This case study challenges the "separate spheres" thesis and inserts women into the history of globalization not as "patient, faithful wives" but as active financial agents.

**George Kennan in Siberia and the Telegraph as Civilizing Instrument.** Müller uses the letters of George Kennan, who led Western Union's telegraph surveying crew in Russian America and Siberia in the 1860s, to illuminate how the technology functioned as an instrument of Euro-American "civilizing" ideology. Kennan performed telegraph "tricks" for indigenous Siberians, who—utterly unfamiliar with the technology—named the machine "Ivan Machina" (Machine John) and regarded Kennan as a "shaman" or magician. Kennan's letters reveal a common pattern: telegraph operators' mastery of electricity established their perceived superiority over "uncivilized" populations, making them unwilling agents of Euro-America's civilizing mission. The case simultaneously shows how the boundary of "civilization" was unstable—contemporaries also used the telegraph to argue for American superiority over "old" Europe, as in Constantino Brumidi's 1862 Capitol fresco *The Telegraph*, which reversed the actual cable-laying route to depict America as the generous benefactor bestowing technology upon a suppliant Europa.

**Heart's Content and the Newfoundland Preemption Scandal (1873).** The cable station at Heart's Content, Newfoundland—landing place of the Atlantic cable—served for decades as a British microcosm on colonial soil, where British operators were imported while Newfoundlanders were restricted to the landlines. Yet Newfoundlanders were not merely passive subjects of globalization. In 1873, Newfoundland politicians, merchants, and even ordinary fishermen successfully manipulated London stock prices by orchestrating a preemption scandal that caused Anglo-American Telegraph Company shares to plummet, generating large revenues for the Newfoundlanders and significant losses for the London investors. This case powerfully illustrates Müller's argument that even those formally excluded from the telegraph system could act as agents within the globalization processes it enabled—and that the relationship between "connected" and "unconnected" was far more complex than simple binary models suggest. Cable station superintendent Ezra Weedon's correspondence reveals the ongoing diplomatic work required to maintain friendly local relations, including managing Newfoundland elections and trade disputes that directly affected cable operations.

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"The promise of an all-inclusive *Weltcommunication* was as empty as the analogy of a Victorian Internet is misleading. Submarine telegraphs never were a means of social or mass communication but remained a specialty service for an exclusive clientele until they were displaced by wireless and radio telegraphy as the dominant means of global communication in the 1920s." (p. 119–120)

"Although the actors strongly benefited from imperial and national structures and a global coloniality, i.e., a world shaped through the experience and logic of centuries of colonialism, they did not necessarily embrace imperial interests." (p. 7\)

"This methodological nationalism underplays the international financing structure of the cable companies and the transnational working agreements between the companies, the nation-states, and international governance." (p. 7\)

"Scientists were 'all very well in their place, but their place was in the laboratory, or at any rate not in the directory of big business.'" —John Pender (p. 236\)

"Striving to challenge a male-dominated society, \[women\] saw the stock market as a means for their new freedom and apparently took a particular liking to the ocean cables." (p. 244\)

"Although the cable agents may have been privately patriotic, they were certainly not nationalistic." (p. 248\)

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

Müller's work pairs productively with several texts across the reading lists. Nicole Starosielski's *The Undersea Network* (2015) offers a complementary contemporary analysis of submarine cable infrastructure, sharing Müller's attention to materiality and the politics of connection/disconnection while focusing on the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. David Hochfelder's *The Telegraph in America* (2012) and Richard John's *Network Nation* (2010) provide the American domestic context that Müller's transatlantic frame touches only partially. Stephen Kern's *The Culture of Time and Space* (1880–1918) engages many of the same temporal and spatial transformations from a cultural history rather than actor-network perspective, and Müller's account of competing visions of "electric union" enriches Kern's claims about the annihilation of distance. David Edgerton's emphasis on technology-in-use over innovation in *The Shock of the Old* (2007) resonates strongly with Müller's critique of heroic invention narratives.

One limitation is the book's relatively thin engagement with non-Euro-American actors: despite invoking global history methodology and postcolonial theory, the study focuses overwhelmingly on British, American, and German cable agents, with indigenous populations, colonial subjects, and Asian actors appearing primarily as objects of Euro-American discourse. Additionally, while the actor-network approach yields rich social and cultural detail, it sometimes fragments into biographical vignettes at the expense of sustained structural analysis of how the telegraph system shaped, for instance, commodity markets, financial flows, or diplomatic practice at a systemic level.

# Week 6 — Environment

# **Jeffrey K. Stine and Joel A. Tarr, "At the Intersection of Histories: Technology and the Environment" (1998)**

**Source:** *Technology and Culture* 39, no. 4 (October 1998): 601–640.

---

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Stine and Tarr argue that the history of technology and environmental history have developed as largely separate fields despite sharing an obvious and fundamental object of inquiry: the reciprocal relationship between human technological systems and the natural world. While scattered works have addressed this intersection since the 1930s, sustained, systematic scholarship that gives equal analytical weight to both technology and the environment has only recently begun to emerge. The authors survey the existing literature across six thematic domains—textbooks, cities, public health, industry, natural resources, and environmental policy—to map where productive work has been done, identify persistent blind spots, and make the case that historians of technology have a special obligation to incorporate environmental dimensions into their analyses.

---

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

This essay functions as a comprehensive state-of-the-field review aimed at two audiences that have mostly talked past one another. On one side, historians of technology had long focused on technological "progress" and innovation, treating environmental consequences as incidental or invisible. On the other, environmental historians frequently treated technology as a black box—an undifferentiated force of destruction—without attending to design choices, alternative technological paths, or the internal logic of engineering systems. Stine and Tarr argue that both fields suffer from this mutual neglect. They trace the genealogy of missed connections: Mumford wrote about technology and the urban environment in the 1930s; Lynn White Jr. raised ecological questions in 1967; Hunter Dupree called for an ecological perspective in 1969; Nathan Rosenberg outlined the economics of environmental externalities in 1971; Thomas Parke Hughes noted shifting public attitudes—yet none of these interventions generated sustained research programs within the history of technology. The essay is explicitly programmatic, identifying what John Staudenmaier called in 1990 "virtually no serious study of the historical relationship between technology and the environment" and arguing that this gap was finally beginning to close. They position their review against earlier historiographic assessments by Staudenmaier and others, while pointing to emerging institutional developments—new book series, jointly sponsored conference sessions between SHOT and ASEH, special journal issues—as signs of an incipient subfield.

---

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

The essay does not advance a single theoretical framework but rather calls for an integrated analytical approach that treats technology and the environment as mutually constitutive agents of historical change, drawing on Arthur McEvoy's formulation of technology as "the point of interaction between the human and the natural." The authors implicitly favor approaches that open the black box of technology (attending to design choices, alternative paths, and engineering ideology) while simultaneously taking environmental consequences seriously as outcomes, not mere backdrop.

---

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

This is a bibliographic essay and historiographic review. The authors survey approximately twenty-five years of published scholarship (monographs, journal articles, edited volumes, dissertations) across the history of technology, environmental history, public health history, and related fields, restricted to works dealing with the United States. They supplement this with institutional evidence: conference programs, book series launches, university program formations, and the trajectory of *Technology and Culture* itself.

---

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

Because this is a review essay rather than a monograph, the "case studies" are really clusters of scholarship that Stine and Tarr highlight as especially productive or especially neglected intersections. Four stand out as the most exam-useful:

**Cities and urban infrastructure as environmental systems.** The richest vein of existing scholarship, Stine and Tarr argue, sits at the intersection of urban technology and environmental degradation. They trace how the NSF-funded "retrospective technology assessment" of wastewater technology in the late 1970s opened a research corridor into sewerage systems, water supply, and their downstream ecological effects. Martin Melosi's work on garbage, solid waste, and sanitation in American cities exemplifies the best of this literature, as does William Cronon's *Nature's Metropolis*, which traced how railroads, grain elevators, refrigerated boxcars, and slaughterhouses transformed "first nature" into "second nature" by linking Chicago's commodity markets to the ecological exploitation of the western hinterland. The key point is that cities are themselves built environments whose technological systems—water supply, sewerage, streets, energy grids—have massive environmental footprints that historians of technology have been slow to examine on their own terms. Stine and Tarr note that Carroll Pursell's *The Machine in America* (1995) stands as the only textbook to date that weaves technology-environment interactions throughout rather than isolating them in a single chapter.

**Heavy industry's environmental blind spot.** Stine and Tarr identify a striking omission: historians of technology and business historians have systematically ignored the environmental devastation wrought by heavy industry, particularly the iron and steel and chemical industries. Thomas Misa's prize-winning *A Nation of Steel* "completely sidesteps the steel industry's major impacts on air and water quality and land use." Histories of chemical firms ignore their pre-1960s pollution despite the industry's "starring role in polluting the nation's air, land, and water since the late nineteenth century." The authors contrast this silence with the work of environmental, labor, and social historians—S. J. Kleinberg on Pittsburgh working-class families, Andrew Hurley on environmental inequalities in Gary, Indiana—who have been far more sensitive to how industrial technology degrades the environments where workers and communities actually live. The implication is that the history of technology's traditional focus on innovation and systems-building has produced a systematic blindness to ecological harm.

**Water resources development in the American West.** The damming, diverting, and engineering of western rivers represents perhaps the most dramatic intersection of technology and environment, and it has generated a large literature—yet Stine and Tarr note that most of these works give unequal weight to the two variables. They single out Richard White's *The Organic Machine* on the Columbia River as a "brilliant little book" that uses the themes of energy and work to explore the connections between technology and nature. Philip Scarpino on the Upper Mississippi and Peter Coates on the trans-Alaska pipeline also receive praise for giving genuine analytical parity to technological systems and ecological consequences. The contrast is with works like Todd Shallat's *Structures in the Stream* and Donald Jackson's *Building the Ultimate Dam*, which are "deeply researched examinations of engineering choices" that nevertheless leave the "spectrum of environmental questions" unanswered.

**Occupational health as environmental history.** Stine and Tarr make a compelling case that workplace health and safety should be understood as an environmental problem. Drawing on Arthur McEvoy's argument that industrial damage to workers' bodies is structurally analogous to industrial damage to the natural environment—both are "social costs, so broadly diffused over large numbers of politically inarticulate victims that employers are rarely called to account for them"—they trace how mining histories, the silicosis crisis of the 1930s, and Christopher Sellers's work on industrial hygiene all reveal technology as an agent of environmental harm at the most intimate scale: the worker's body. This reframing usefully extends the concept of "environment" beyond landscapes and watersheds to include the immediate material conditions of labor.

---

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

* "technology is what distinguishes human activity in nature from that of other animals; because technology is a means of interacting with nature, however, it should be amenable to ecological analysis." — Arthur McEvoy, quoted by Stine and Tarr (p. 606\)

* "there has been virtually no serious study of the historical relationship between technology and the environment." — John Staudenmaier (1990), quoted on p. 607

* Cronon's *Nature's Metropolis* showed how people "transformed 'first nature' into 'second nature,' mastered by technology and subjected to the whims of the consumer market." (p. 617\)

* McEvoy on occupational health: "the damage that industrial accidents and disease do to workers' bodies is very much like the damage that industry does to the natural environment: both pollution and work injuries are social costs, so broadly diffused over large numbers of politically inarticulate victims that employers are rarely called to account for them." (p. 619–20)

* Schama's formulation: "Even the landscapes that are supposed to be most free of our culture may turn out, on closer inspection, to be its product." (quoted on p. 634\)

* "historians of technology have a special obligation to write about environmental problems because their particular skills and experience offer invaluable insights into this area of global concern." (p. 639\)

---

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

This essay pairs naturally with several works on Matthew's reading lists. Leo Marx's *The Machine in the Garden* (HoT \#101) is one of the founding texts Stine and Tarr identify as establishing the technology-nature framework in American studies, and this essay effectively historicizes Marx's intervention and its reception. Theodore Steinberg's *Nature Incorporated* (HoT \#102) is cited as an exemplary study of how industrialists used waterpowered mills and the legal system to control and transform a New England watershed—precisely the kind of work Stine and Tarr want more of. William Cronon's *Nature's Metropolis* (Urban \#15) receives extensive discussion as a model for understanding city-hinterland relationships through the lens of technology and environment. The essay also anticipates the concerns of Jennifer Light's *The Nature of Cities* (Urban \#31) in its attention to how ecological thinking intersected with urban professional expertise. For the HoS list, the essay's discussion of technology assessment and environmental expertise connects to Jasanoff's concerns about science advising and co-production (*States of Knowledge*, HoS \#37).

**Limitations:** The essay restricts itself to the United States, which the authors acknowledge as a significant constraint given excellent international scholarship. More fundamentally, the piece is a literature review rather than an argument—it maps terrain rather than advancing a new interpretive framework. By 1998, many of the "gaps" it identifies were already being filled by the dissertations it cites in its footnotes, making portions of the essay feel more like a snapshot of a transitional moment than a durable analytical contribution.

# **Leo Marx, *The Machine in the Garden: Technology and the Pastoral Ideal in America* (1964; 2nd ed. 2000\)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

American culture has been shaped by a recurring collision between the pastoral ideal — the dream of a simple, harmonious life in a green landscape — and the forces of industrialization. Marx identifies a "metaphor of contradiction" that runs through American literature from the 1840s onward: the sudden, violent intrusion of the machine (typically the locomotive) into an idyllic natural scene. This pattern, which he calls the "interrupted idyll," reveals a deep structural tension in American consciousness between inherited agrarian hopes and technological reality, and the most important American writers have used this tension not as a retreat into nostalgia but as a vehicle for complex, often tragic, insight.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Marx is writing against two tendencies at once. On one hand, he challenges the approach of liberal historians like Richard Hofstadter and Henry Nash Smith, who treated the "agrarian myth" or "myth of the garden" as essentially a sentimental illusion, an obstacle to clear political thinking. Marx agrees that popular pastoralism is often sentimental and reactionary, but he insists that the same impulse, when taken up by serious literary artists, becomes something far more sophisticated and valuable — what he calls "complex pastoralism." On the other hand, Marx pushes back against literary critics who would treat imaginative responses to industrialization as autonomous aesthetic objects disconnected from history. His method is to hold literature and social change in sustained tension, showing that writers like Thoreau, Melville, Twain, and Fitzgerald used pastoral conventions to register and explore the lived experience of industrial transformation — sometimes more sensitively than any historian or social scientist could. The book helped establish the field of American Studies as an interdisciplinary enterprise, and it became a foundational text for what would later develop into the environmental humanities and the cultural history of technology. Marx is in explicit dialogue with Smith's *Virgin Land* (1950) and with Perry Miller's intellectual history of New England, while drawing on classical pastoral theory (Virgil, Empson) and Freudian ideas about the psychology of regression and wish-fulfillment.

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Cultural history of ideas and symbols, grounded in close literary analysis and the American Studies tradition of reading canonical literature as a window onto national consciousness. The key conceptual apparatus is Marx's distinction between "sentimental pastoralism" (uncritical nostalgia for rural life) and "complex pastoralism" (literary works that set the pastoral ideal against a "counterforce" — typically industrial technology — and derive their power from the unresolved tension between the two). He also introduces the concept of the "middle landscape," a symbolic terrain located between untouched wilderness and urban civilization, which he traces from Virgil through Jefferson to Thoreau.

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

Close reading of canonical American literature — Hawthorne, Emerson, Thoreau, Melville, Twain, Henry Adams, Fitzgerald — supplemented by readings of Elizabethan travel narratives, Virgil's Eclogues, Jefferson's *Notes on Virginia*, Crèvecoeur, and visual art (George Inness, Charles Sheeler). The temporal scope runs from the European discovery of America to the early twentieth century, with the heart of the analysis focused on the 1840–1860 period of America's industrial "take-off." Geographically, it is a study of the American literary imagination, but with deep roots in European (especially English) romantic and pastoral traditions.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**Hawthorne's Sleepy Hollow notebook entry (1844).** This is the book's founding scene and recurring touchstone. On a July morning, Hawthorne sits in a wooded hollow near Concord, recording impressions of perfect rural harmony — birdsong, insects, the distant sound of farmers. Then, suddenly: "But, hark\! there is the whistle of the locomotive — the long shriek, harsh, above all other harshness." The train shatters the pastoral mood and forces Hawthorne to acknowledge a reality alien to the green retreat. Marx reads this as a "paradigm" for a design that recurs across all of American literature: the interrupted idyll, where an intrusion of industrial power exposes the fragility of the pastoral dream. Crucially, Marx insists this is not primarily "about" the transition from agrarian to industrial society — it is about the clash between two states of consciousness. The Sleepy Hollow episode acquires its power precisely because the railroad, associated with fire, smoke, iron, and noise, does not simply represent economic change; it represents the incursion of history itself into a timeless retreat. Marx traces this pattern back to Virgil's first eclogue, where the dispossessed herdsman Meliboeus disrupts the peaceful scene of Tityrus playing his pipe, revealing that the idyll is an oasis surrounded by chaos.

**Jefferson's *Notes on Virginia* and the "middle landscape."** Marx devotes sustained attention to Jefferson's attempt to articulate a pastoral vision grounded in actual political economy rather than literary fantasy. Jefferson's famous declaration that "those who labour in the earth are the chosen people of God" is usually read as simple agrarian piety. Marx shows that it occurs not in a discussion of agriculture but in a chapter on manufactures — it is a response to the threat of industrialization, not a celebration of farming for its own sake. More importantly, Marx identifies what he calls Jefferson's "syntax of the middle landscape": conditional statements ("were it made a question, whether no law, as among the savage Americans, or too much law, as among the civilized Europeans...") that deliberately hold open a range of social possibilities between primitivism and industrial civilization. Jefferson knew he could not literally keep workshops in Europe, but he used this rhetorical structure to insist that America's abundance of land created genuinely different conditions from those of the Old World. Marx shows that Jefferson was simultaneously a devotee of technological improvement (he was thrilled by the steam mill at Blackfriar's Bridge) and a critic of the factory system — a position that only seems contradictory if you assume that technology necessarily entails factories. For Jefferson, the machine was a liberating tool; the factory was feudal oppression in new clothing.

**Thoreau's railroad in *Walden*.** Marx's reading of the "Sounds" chapter is the book's most sustained and intricate literary analysis. Thoreau introduces the sound of the locomotive twice, first as a partridge (blending peacefully into the landscape), then as a hawk (rapacious, discordant). This double image captures the genuine ambiguity of the machine: it is both exhilarating in its power and threatening in its implications. The passage culminates in Thoreau's devastating image of the cattle train: "A carload of drovers, too, in the midst, on a level with their droves now, their vocation gone, but still clinging to their useless sticks as their badge of office. So is your pastoral life whirled past and away." Unlike Emerson, who cheerfully reconciled machinery and transcendentalism, Thoreau acknowledges that the agrarian pastoral way of life is doomed, and he refuses nostalgic consolation. His answer comes not through politics or economics but through art: in the "Spring" chapter, the thawing sand and clay of the railroad embankment flow into organic forms, and the "Deep Cut" — the wound the railroad inflicted on the landscape — becomes a site of natural regeneration. The resolution is aesthetic, not social: meaning must be wrested from the facts by the parable-maker's art.

**Fitzgerald's *The Great Gatsby* and the "garden of ashes."** In his Epilogue, Marx reads Gatsby as the terminal case of the pastoral ideal in America. Nick Carraway's vision of "the old island here that flowered once for Dutch sailors' eyes — a fresh, green breast of the new world" connects Gatsby's romantic hope back to the original European encounter with the continent. But between West Egg and Manhattan lies the valley of ashes — "a fantastic farm where ashes grow like wheat" — which is the true landscape of an industrial civilization. Gatsby's cream-colored car, "swollen here and there in its monstrous length," is simultaneously a symbol of pastoral aspiration (sitting inside it is like being in "a sort of green leather conservatory") and a murder weapon. Nick ultimately distinguishes himself from Gatsby by recognizing what Gatsby could not: that the dream "was already behind him, somewhere back in that vast obscurity beyond the city, where the dark fields of the republic rolled on under the night." Marx uses this to crystallize his distinction between sentimental and complex pastoralism: Gatsby cannot distinguish dreams from facts, while Nick — like Thoreau, like Melville's Ishmael — learns to hold both in view simultaneously.

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"The pastoral ideal has been used to define the meaning of America ever since the age of discovery, and it has not yet lost its hold upon the native imagination." (p. 3\)

"Those who labour in the earth are the chosen people of God, if ever he had a chosen people, whose breasts he has made his peculiar deposit for substantial and genuine virtue." (Jefferson, quoted p. 124 — Marx's central exhibit for the pastoral ideal as political theory)

"So is your pastoral life whirled past and away. But the bell rings, and I must get off the track and let the cars go by." (Thoreau, quoted p. 254\)

"It is industrialization, represented by images of machine technology, that provides the counterforce in the American archetype of the pastoral design." (p. 26\)

"The machine's sudden entrance into the garden presents a problem that ultimately belongs not to art but to politics." (p. 365\)

"In the end the American hero is either dead or totally alienated from society, alone and powerless, like the evicted shepherd of Virgil's eclogue." (p. 364\)

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

*The Machine in the Garden* pairs naturally with David Nye's *American Technological Sublime* (1994), which picks up the affirmative, awe-struck dimension of American responses to technology that Marx largely sets aside in favor of the critical literary tradition. It also works powerfully alongside Raymond Williams's *The Country and the City* (1973), which performs a parallel analysis of English literature's rural-urban tension but with a more explicitly Marxist political economy and less focus on technology per se. Within the reading lists, it connects directly to Eric Schatzberg's *Technology: Critical History of a Concept* — Marx's own later essay "Technology: The Emergence of a Hazardous Concept" (1997), also on the list, is a self-revision that argues the word "technology" has itself become a vehicle for the same kind of deterministic thinking he once critiqued.

The book's main limitation is its near-total restriction to a white, male, canonical literary tradition. The "American consciousness" it describes is really a New England intellectual consciousness; the pastoral ideal looks very different if you consider the perspectives of enslaved people, Indigenous nations, or immigrant laborers for whom the "green landscape" was a site of dispossession, not of dreamy retreat. Second, the argument's power depends on a sharp binary between "sentimental" and "complex" pastoralism that can feel more evaluative than analytical — it sometimes reads as if the whole point is to rescue the canonical writers from the charge of naivety, rather than to investigate how the pastoral ideal actually operated across the full range of American culture.

# 

# 

# 

# 

# **Leo Marx, "The Idea of Nature in America" (2008)**

*Daedalus* 137, no. 2 (Spring 2008): 8–21.

---

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

The idea of nature as a separate, independent entity—distinct from human society—has been one of the most fundamental organizing concepts in American thought since the colonial era, but it has become intellectually incoherent and culturally obsolete. Marx traces how successive American deployments of "nature" (Puritan theology, Jeffersonian Enlightenment, Emersonian Romanticism, Darwinian biology, postmodern critique) each relied on the assumption of nature's separateness from humanity, even when that assumption contradicted the available science. He proposes that the first nature/second nature distinction offers the most promising way to salvage a workable concept of nature in the post-ecological-crisis era.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

This essay is Marx's late-career attempt to synthesize a lifetime of thinking about how Americans have used "nature" as an ideological tool—work that stretches back to *The Machine in the Garden* (1964) and *Does Technology Drive History?* (1994). He is intervening in a debate that was intensely live in the 1990s and early 2000s: the question of whether the concept of "nature" was dead or recoverable. He engages directly with Bill McKibben's *The End of Nature* (1989), Carolyn Merchant's *The Death of Nature* (1980), Fredric Jameson's postmodern declaration that nature is "gone for good," and especially William Cronon's provocative 1995 essay "The Trouble with Wilderness." Marx positions himself as sympathetic to all these critics while arguing they each fall short. McKibben, he contends, exposes the real theological stakes of nature's independence but cannot escape the very framework he mourns. Cronon correctly identifies "wilderness" as a colonial fiction but remains torn between his scholarly skepticism and his environmentalist reverence—ultimately unable to resolve the contradiction. Marx's own intervention is to argue that the concept's fatal flaw has always been the assumption of separateness, an assumption that Darwin's theory of evolution should have destroyed in 1859 but that American culture—driven by both Christian theology and the ideology of "Progress"—stubbornly refused to abandon.

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Intellectual history and cultural criticism, drawing on Raymond Williams's keyword analysis and the Hegelian/Marxist tradition of first nature/second nature. Marx treats "nature" as a keyword whose shifting meanings reveal deep structures of American ideology, particularly the entanglement of theology, capitalism, and nationalism.

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

Close reading of canonical American texts (Bradford, Winthrop, Jefferson, Emerson, Melville, Darwin, Cronon, McKibben) organized as a series of historical "stages" rather than a continuous narrative. No archival work—this is a synthetic intellectual history essay spanning roughly 1620 to 2000\.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**Jefferson's Natural Bridge and the politics of the sublime.** Marx uses Jefferson's description of Virginia's Natural Bridge in *Notes on Virginia* (1785) to show how Enlightenment Americans fused Newtonian rationalism with Romantic aesthetics. Jefferson begins his account with precise measurements—the detached observer cataloguing dimensions—then abruptly shifts into the second person, describing how "you involuntarily fall on your hands and feet, creep to the parapet and peep over it." The rapture is "really indescribable." Marx reads this shift as the moment when Jefferson's secular faith in nature displaces his rationalism: nature becomes a source of sublime experience that has the emotional force of religious conversion. This is useful for showing how the idea of nature as separate-but-magnificent served the political purposes of the new republic. Jefferson used "the Laws of Nature and of Nature's God" to justify armed rebellion—nature authorized the political order.

**Winthrop's two liberties and the Puritan weaponization of "nature."** In 1645, when accused of infringing on colonists' liberty, the Massachusetts Bay Colony's lieutenant governor John Winthrop defended himself by distinguishing between "natural" liberty (shared with beasts, the freedom to do evil as well as good) and "civil" liberty (available only to regenerate Christians). Marx shows how the existence of an actual, vast, unexplored continent made this theological argument spatially concrete: the unregenerate were consigned to the realm of wild nature outside the colony's boundaries. The indigenous peoples were collapsed into nature itself—Bradford's "wild beasts and wild men." The separateness of nature was not just an abstract theological idea but a tool of colonial power, reinforcing the barrier between the elect and everyone else.

**Ishmael's chapter on whiteness in *Moby-Dick*.** Marx treats Melville's meditation on the whiteness of the whale as America's deepest literary reckoning with the ambiguity of "nature." Ishmael exhausts every possible meaning of whiteness—terror, beauty, death, purity—and concludes that the whale's power lies precisely in its resistance to fixed meaning: "all deified nature paints like a harlot, whose allurements cover nothing but the charnel-house within." Ahab's monomaniacal rage is driven not by any particular meaning of nature but by nature's maddening blankness, its refusal to yield stable significance. Marx reads this as Melville dramatizing the fatal incoherence of treating nature as a separate entity that can be known, mastered, or conquered. The apocalyptic outcome reveals the "incalculable cost—and futility—of the human effort to grasp the ultimate meaning of nature."

**Cronon's wilderness impasse.** Marx carefully dissects Cronon's 1995 "Trouble with Wilderness" essay as a case study of intellectual paralysis. Cronon demonstrated in *Changes in the Land* (1983) that indigenous peoples had transformed the New England landscape long before Europeans arrived—there was nothing "pristine" about it. He then argued that the myth of "virgin wilderness" was an ideological weapon of white conquest, "especially cruel when seen from the perspective of the Indians who had once called that land home." Yet Cronon cannot bring himself to abandon the idea. He concedes that belief in nature's separateness "is likely to reinforce environmentally irresponsible behavior," but then says the "autonomy of nonhuman nature" may be "an indispensable corrective to human arrogance." His proposed solution—replacing "wilderness" with Thoreau's "wildness" (which can be found in a Manhattan sidewalk crack as easily as in the Rockies)—strikes Marx as "tenuous, even quixotic." The episode demonstrates Marx's larger point: the idea of nature's separateness is intellectually indefensible but emotionally indispensable to those who depend on it for meaning.

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"Why should not we also enjoy an original relation to the universe?" —Emerson, *Nature* (1836), as cited by Marx to frame the American longing for direct access to nature that drives the entire intellectual tradition. (p. 12\)

"Nothing is easier than to admit in words the truth of the universal struggle for life, or more difficult—at least I have found it so—than constantly to bear this conclusion in mind. Yet unless it be thoroughly engrained in the mind, the whole economy of nature . . . will be dimly seen or quite misunderstood." —Darwin, *Origin of Species*, as cited by Marx. (p. 14\)

"Despite Darwin, we are not, in our hearts, part of the natural process." —Lynn White Jr., cited by Marx as the pithiest summary of America's refusal to accept the implications of evolutionary biology. (p. 13\)

"We have deprived nature of its independence, and that is fatal to its meaning . . . nature's independence is its meaning, without it there is nothing but us." —McKibben, *The End of Nature*, as cited by Marx. Marx calls this "an astute observation and a poignant confession." (p. 19\)

"Any way of looking at nature that encourages us to believe that we are separate from nature—as wilderness tends to do—is likely to reinforce environmentally irresponsible behavior." —Cronon, "The Trouble with Wilderness," as cited by Marx. (p. 20\)

"Postmodernism is what you have when the modernization process is complete and nature is gone for good." —Jameson, cited by Marx as the most extreme version of the death-of-nature thesis, representing the pure-constructivist position that nature is nothing but discourse. (p. 17\)

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

This essay pairs naturally with Cronon's *Nature's Metropolis* (which Marx cites as one of the works endorsing first nature/second nature), Carolyn Merchant's *The Death of Nature* (which Marx engages at length), and Leo Marx's own *The Machine in the Garden*—this essay is in many ways a retrospective on the same territory forty-four years later. For exam purposes, it connects powerfully to the history of technology reading list through its analysis of "Progress" as an ideology that required nature to remain separate and exploitable, and to Schatzberg's *Technology: Critical History of a Concept* in its attention to how keywords carry hidden ideological freight. The first nature/second nature framework is directly relevant to environmental-urban connections on the urban history list (Cronon, Rome, Light's *Nature of Cities*).

The essay's main limitation is its extreme canonicity: the trajectory runs Bradford–Jefferson–Emerson–Melville–Darwin, with no indigenous, Black, or non-elite voices.
