Marx acknowledges this by noting that the "wilderness" idea erased indigenous experience, but he does not incorporate those perspectives into his own intellectual history. The essay also remains at the level of ideas—there is no attention to the material practices, technologies, or economic structures through which Americans actually transformed the landscapes Marx discusses.

# **Ronald R. Kline, *Consumers in the Country: Technology and Social Change in Rural America* (2000)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Kline argues that farm people were not passive recipients of supposedly "urbanizing" technologies—the telephone, automobile, radio, and electricity—but active agents who resisted, modified, and selectively adopted these technologies to create their own forms of rural modernity. The transformation of rural America in the first sixty years of the twentieth century was a fractured, contested process in which promoters' utopian predictions about technological progress went largely unfulfilled: these technologies neither stopped migration to the city, reduced farm women's working hours, nor erased urban-rural cultural differences.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Kline writes against two deeply entrenched narratives. The first is technological determinism—the belief, held by reformers from the Country Life Commission (1908) through the Rural Electrification Administration and beyond, that new technologies autonomously drive social change, that each innovation would inevitably modernize farm life and keep people on the land. The second is diffusion theory, which treats consumers as passive endpoints in a one-way transfer of technology from producer to user. He engages directly with Ruth Schwartz Cowan's concept of the "consumption junction" (from her essay in Bijker, Hughes, and Pinch's *Social Construction of Technological Systems*) to center his analysis on the mediated space between producers and consumers, and he extends the SCOT framework he co-developed with Trevor Pinch by incorporating gender power relations and consumer resistance into the model. He is also in dialogue with Lizabeth Cohen's work on interwar Chicago workers (*Making a New Deal*), arguing that farm communities, like ethnic urban communities, drew on local cultural resources to resist the homogenizing pressures of mass consumer culture. Kline pushes against Paul Johnstone's influential 1940 claim that the countryside was undergoing "accelerated urbanization," arguing instead that farm people created individual, plural modernities rather than converging on a single urban norm. The book also implicitly critiques modernization theory—the postwar social-science framework that treated technological adoption as an inevitable, monolithic force—by showing it to be a contingent, historically situated process that began as a domestic reform effort before being exported internationally.

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Kline uses a modified version of the social construction of technology (SCOT) framework, extended to include the consumption junction, gender relations, and consumer resistance. He traces the "mutual construction" of technology and society: how farm people reshaped artifacts (e.g., turning the Model T into a farm power source) and how manufacturers responded by producing new or modified technologies (e.g., pickup trucks, gas-engine washers). Raymond Williams's concept of "keywords" structures his analysis of contested terms like *urbanization*, *modernization*, *technology*, and *consumer*.

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

Kline draws on an impressive range of archival sources: AT\&T corporate records, REA and USDA archives, General Electric internal documents, farm journals (*Wallaces' Farmer*, *Rural New Yorker*, *Country Life*), independent telephone industry trade publications (*Telephony*), oral histories with New York farm families, Census Bureau reports, and USDA social surveys. The geographic scope is national with particular attention to the Midwest (where cooperative traditions were strongest), the South (where poverty and sharecropping created different patterns), and New York State. The temporal scope runs from approximately 1900 to 1965\.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**Farmer Telephone Cooperatives and "Access Competition" (Ch. 1).** When the Bell system and independent companies found it unprofitable to serve sparsely populated areas, farm people organized their own telephone cooperatives, building lines out of barbed-wire fences, cutting their own poles, and stringing wire in their spare time. In southeastern Iowa before World War I, nearly 90 percent of the 251 telephone companies were mutuals, with more than 90 percent of their presidents being farmers. These cooperatives exercised real economic power: merchants in small towns depended on farm trade, so farmers could threaten to take their business to a neighboring town if they didn't get favorable connection rates. Bell engineer W. O. Pennell wrote in 1906 that "the power of the mutual company lies chiefly in the fact that the small agricultural town owes its existence and prosperity to the surrounding country. In other words the farmers make the town." Farm people also used the party line in unauthorized ways—broadcasting music, playing games, eavesdropping on neighbors—recreating the rural custom of "visiting" through a new medium. AT\&T ultimately acquiesced, designing systems that permitted eavesdropping rather than fighting the practice.

**The Crusade Against the "Devil Wagon" (Ch. 2).** When automobiles first appeared on rural roads in the early 1900s, driven by wealthy city people on pleasure tours, farm people mounted a fierce resistance campaign. They sabotaged their own roads with rakes, nails, glass, barbed wire stretched across the road, and plowed-up surfaces designed to be impassable by car but passable by horse and wagon. Connecticut farmers spread tire-cutting slag on roads; Minnesota farmers plowed up roads near Rochester; Indiana farmers weakened bridges and barricaded roads. Groups like the Farmers' Anti-Automobile League near Evanston, Illinois formed vigilante committees. Farmers shot at motorists in Minnesota and South Carolina, stoned them in Indiana, and assaulted them in Wisconsin. This resistance faded around 1910 as manufacturers developed affordable cars for rural roads (especially the Model T) and farm prosperity enabled purchases. Crucially, when farm people adopted the car, they modified it to suit rural needs: removing the back seat to haul produce, jacking up the rear axle to use the engine as a power source for washing clothes, sawing wood, grinding feed, and pumping water. Manufacturers responded by developing pickup trucks and tractors that incorporated these consumer innovations.

**The REA "Circus" and Consumer Resistance to Electrical Modernization (Chs. 5–7).** The Rural Electrification Administration, founded in 1935, discovered that simply stringing power lines to farms did not produce the electrical consumption it needed to make cooperatives financially viable. Most farm families joined co-ops mainly to get lights, buying only a radio, an iron, and perhaps one large appliance. The REA responded with elaborate traveling shows—the "Farm Equipment Tour," informally called the "REA Circus"—that set up circus tents in farm fields near newly energized co-ops, drawing crowds of up to ten thousand. Male agricultural engineers demonstrated milking machines and a rotating drum with rubber "fingers" that plucked chickens (a crowd favorite); female home economists demonstrated kitchen appliances and ran the all-electric lunch tent. "Cooking duels" between local male celebrities exploited rural humor traditions. The ceremonies for turning on power had an even more ritualistic quality: in Kentucky in 1938, a 4-H girl read "solemn funeral rites" for an "old-fashioned coal oil lamp" buried in a white casket as a choir sang hymns at Daniel Boone's Cave. Despite all this, farm people resisted buying the full complement of appliances the REA wanted them to have. They valued lights, irons, radios, and washing machines but saw vacuum cleaners, coffeemakers, and electric ranges as "foolish luxuries." They were especially slow to install indoor bathrooms—owing to high costs, poor water supplies, and entrenched cultural habits among older farm men. Surveys showed that well into the 1960s, electrified farm women worked just as many hours as they had before, using appliances to do more work in the same time rather than to gain leisure.

**Radio as Counter-Urbanizer (Ch. 4).** Unlike the telephone and automobile, radio met almost no resistance from farm people, partly because their experience with telephone "broadcasting" (party-line music, market reports relayed over phone lines) had prepared them for it. Agricultural reformers seized on radio as the solution to the Country Life paradox: it could bring the city's culture to the farm without requiring people to leave. But farm listeners tuned in the country, not the city. They overwhelmingly preferred country music, barn-dance programs (like the *National Barn Dance* on WLS Chicago), and regional entertainment over the "highbrow" music and educational programming that reformers envisioned. Broadcasters responded to listener demand rather than reformer wishes. The USDA's "Aunt Sammy" cooking program, played by fifty different women at fifty stations in 1926, was popular, but its success owed to practical recipe advice rather than uplift ideology. Farm people also improvised access: connecting car batteries to radios through windows, building barbed-wire antennas, and using small windcharger units to power battery sets off the grid.

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"In large measure, this book is a commentary on the scissors picture, a recounting of what is left out of the drawing and a critique of the belief in technological determinism that continues to shape how we think about technology and society." (p. 6\)

"Farm men, women, and youth were much more than passive, simple-minded, and grateful recipients of the transfer of technology from the city to the country. . . . They contested efforts to urbanize the farm by resisting each new technology and then weaving it into existing cultural patterns in their own way." (p. 6\)

"The power of the mutual company lies chiefly in the fact that the small agricultural town owes its existence and prosperity to the surrounding country. In other words the farmers make the town." —Bell engineer W. O. Pennell, 1906 (p. 33\)

"Our big job is to build up the psychology of the generous use of electricity—a few lights in a home is not rural electrification." —Morris Cooke, REA administrator (p. 178\)

"You can't go to town in a bathtub\!" —farm woman explaining why the family had a car but no indoor plumbing (p. 93\)

"What social scientists in the 1960s retrospectively called modernization and viewed as a historical force was actually a contingent, mutual shaping of technology and culture." (p. 8\)

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

Kline pairs naturally with Ruth Schwartz Cowan's *More Work for Mother* (which he draws on explicitly for the consumption junction concept and which shares his interest in how domestic technologies don't necessarily reduce women's labor), with Bijker, Hughes, and Pinch's *Social Construction of Technological Systems* (whose SCOT framework he extends to include consumers and gender), and with David Nye's *Electrifying America* (which covers much of the same chronological territory for electrification but from a more cultural-history angle and with less attention to rural resistance). There's also a strong pairing with Raymond Williams's *Television: Technology and Cultural Form*, since Kline explicitly borrows Williams's "keywords" method and his analysis of radio anticipates Williams's arguments about how broadcasting technologies are shaped by existing social structures.

One limitation is geographic unevenness: the book is richest on the Midwest, where cooperative traditions provided the clearest examples of consumer agency, and thinnest on the South, where sharecropping, racial inequality, and deep poverty created very different dynamics that receive less sustained attention. A second limitation is that Kline's emphasis on resistance and agency, while a valuable corrective to technological determinism, can understate the degree to which corporate and government power ultimately did reshape rural life in ways farm people could not control—particularly the postwar agricultural consolidation that made the family farm economically unviable regardless of how cleverly its occupants wielded consumer technologies.

# **Joy Parr, *Sensing Changes: Technologies, Environments, and the Everyday, 1953-2003* (UBC Press, 2010\)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Parr argues that people come to know their environments and technologies not primarily through language, concepts, or symbolic systems but through their sensing bodies — through the accumulated habits, reflexes, and perceptual tunings that arise from physical dwelling in a place. When large-scale technological and environmental changes (megaprojects, contaminations) radically alter those habitats, the resulting crisis is not only economic or political but deeply corporeal: people's bodies, trained by decades of sensory interaction with a now-vanished world, become unreliable guides to the new one, producing a bewildering loss of competence and confidence that standard frameworks of property loss and health impact cannot capture.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Parr is writing against the dominance of the "linguistic turn" in environmental history, the history of technology, and the social sciences more broadly. She directly challenges the assumption — central to social construction scholarship inspired by Foucault, Joan Scott, and Bruno Latour — that meaning precedes experience, that humans know the world primarily through symbolic, discursive, and textual representations. She finds this framework profoundly limiting for understanding how people actually live with technologies and environments, because it sets aside what she calls "experiential excess": the vast store of bodily knowledge that resists being captured in language. She builds on Katherine Hayles's 1995 call for more "embodied" historical practice, which went largely unheeded during the heyday of discourse analysis. She also draws on Pierre Bourdieu's concept of habitus, Marcel Mauss's "techniques of the body," Humberto Maturana and Francisco Varela's cognitive psychology of "embodied minds," and phenomenologists like Merleau-Ponty. Against environmental historians who track only property loss, health impacts, and employment disruption when studying displaced populations, she insists on a richer accounting that includes the sensory and perceptual retraining — the remaking of the body's relationship to its habitat — that such upheavals demand. William Cronon's 1992 warning about the "rhetorical razor" of narrative is an important touchstone: Parr shares his view that ecological relationships are synthetic, non-linear, and poorly served by the strict sequence of written language.

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Phenomenological and sensory history, combined with what Parr distinguishes as "corporeal embodiment" (as opposed to "epistemological embodiment," which uses bodily metaphors as figures of speech). She mobilizes Bourdieu's habitus, Maturana's relational/operational spaces, Hayles's concept of "flux," Csordas's "somatic attention," and Polanyi's "tacit knowledge" to argue that humans make sense of the world directly through their sensing bodies, storing consistencies as habit and reflex before — and often without — conscious linguistic articulation.

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

Oral histories conducted over a decade with residents of six Canadian communities affected by Cold War-era megaprojects and a water contamination; government and corporate archives; judicial inquiry transcripts (the Walkerton Inquiry); local and metropolitan newspaper coverage; agricultural census data; land-use records; GIS reconstructions; and a parallel "new media" project (audio walks, sound compositions, video, photography) designed to convey the sensory dimensions that text alone cannot communicate. Geographic scope: Canada (New Brunswick, Ontario, British Columbia), 1953-2003.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**Iroquois and the St. Lawrence Seaway (Chapter 4: Movement and Sound).** This is Parr's most vivid illustration of embodied loss. Iroquois was a "walking village" — a pedestrian settlement where the Caldwell Linen Mill was the main employer and people moved entirely on foot through a compact, twelve-block-long, two-block-wide grid between the railway tracks and the St. Lawrence River. Residents' daily routes brought them into intimate sensory contact with the roar of the International Rapids (the village's defining "sound mark" in all seasons), the sight of ships on the river, the textures of old masonry buildings and mature hardwood trees, and the micro-encounters of village life — mill workers passing on sidewalks at shift change, children gathering at the canal locks and powerhouse. In 1958, to make way for the Seaway, the village was relocated 1.6 km north. Wooden houses were moved but stripped of their porches; stone and brick structures were burned and demolished. The new village was designed as a 1950s suburban plan — curved streets, cul-de-sacs, a plaza with a parking lot facing the highway, treeless and oriented toward the automobile. The rapids were silenced, replaced by a placid shipping channel. Residents reported that, despite knowing the same people, they felt they "didn't know anybody" in the new town. Joe Roberts stopped walking. Erma Stover, who had always known exactly where to find friends without a telephone, lost that intuitive spatial knowledge. Ron Fader described the dislocation as "quite a kick in the pants." Parr uses Marcel Mauss's insight that "there is technique" even without instruments — walking itself is a technology, a "technique of the body" — to argue that the shift from walking to driving didn't just change transportation; it severed the sensory exchange through which residents had known their place. The "peripatetic sense of place" was destroyed, and with it the bodily archive that had organized social life.

**Base Gagetown, New Brunswick (Chapter 2: Place and Citizenship).** In 1952, at the height of the Cold War, 834 families were displaced from 958 square kilometres of farms and woodlots in south-central New Brunswick to build a NATO training base. Parr traces how the displaced communities — descendants of Loyalists, Irish Catholic famine refugees, and New England planters — had, over 125 to 200 years, built an intricate agro-forestry economy. The chapter's richest material concerns the sensory life of the lumber camps and the woodlots. Men went into the woods in winter with their sons and horses, worked with axes and cross-cut saws in near-silence, ate communal meals of roast meat and molasses cakes, played fiddle music and danced in the evenings. The Scotts and Clarkes had managed their timber stands as multi-generational investments, producing 50,000 board feet per hundred acres, carefully culled rather than stripped. When government estimators offered $10 per forested acre, the community was outraged — not just at the price, but because compensation frameworks could not account for what was being destroyed: the embodied knowledge of sustainable forestry practices refined over generations, the sensory pleasure of the woods, the social rituals of "getting the wood in." Lydia Scott's brother Willard was confident the army had received more from selling the standing timber than it had paid for the land. Many displaced farmer-lumberers were later hired to spray Agent Orange on the base lands — using their intimate knowledge of the terrain to help destroy it, with grave health consequences that emerged decades later.

**Nuclear Radiation Protection in CANDU Plants (Chapter 3: Safety and Sight).** Workers at Canadian nuclear generating stations (NPD, Douglas Point, Bruce, Point Lepreau) came to nuclear work from farming, forestry, fishing, and shipyards — occupations where danger was physically perceptible. Nuclear radiation, by contrast, is entirely "insensible": it cannot be seen, heard, smelled, tasted, or felt. The distinctive Canadian approach, pioneered by Lorne McConnell at NPD in the early 1960s, made every worker (not just specialized health surveyors) responsible for their own safety. Each person, from janitor to supervisor, was trained to understand the theory of ionizing radiation well enough to interpret instrument readings and adjust their bodily practices accordingly — modifying gait, posture, and movement to stay within the registering range of their dosimeters. Parr frames this as a new form of embodiment: workers learned to "embody the insensible" by developing reflexes keyed not to somatic signals but to the proxy readings of instruments. This Canadian model contrasted sharply with the hierarchical "shepherd" systems in France and the United States, where specialized surveyors directed workers who had little understanding of the hazards. The chapter's founding horror stories — the 1952 NRX meltdown caused by a cascade of misheard instructions in a rigid hierarchy, and Alex Sandula losing his hand after picking up an irradiated fuel rod he couldn't sense was dangerous — dramatize the stakes of competing approaches to embodied nuclear knowledge.

**Walkerton Water Contamination (Chapter 7: Taste and Expertise).** In May 2000, heavy rains washed cattle manure containing E. coli O157:H7 through permeable limestone karst into Walkerton, Ontario's shallow Well 5\. Seven people died; half the town became ill. Parr, who grew up in the surrounding townships and was nearby doing research when the contamination occurred, treats this as the book's twenty-first-century case: a crisis not of displacement but of the collision between "internal" and "external" ways of knowing water. Walkerton residents had long known their water by tasting it, by trusting the PUC manager Stan Koebel (a local man whose father had held the job before him), and by a communal suspicion of chlorination common in rural Ontario. The contamination forced a wholesale reckoning with the limits of sensory and communal knowledge. The gap between local and expert knowledge was stark: urban journalists wanted villains and angry mobs, but Walkerton residents initially rallied around Koebel, seeing him as a decent man who had made an honest mistake. The Concerned Walkerton Citizens group, formed in the Davidsons' living room on 26 May, became the vehicle through which townspeople insisted on both systemic explanations (provincial deregulation, neoliberal cost-cutting in environmental monitoring) and the importance of local voices in rebuilding water governance. Parr frames the episode as the moment when established bodily habits of trusting local water — habits tested by time but tragically inadequate — had to yield to the authority of science-based regulation.

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"Our bodies are the instruments through which we become aware of the world beyond our skin, the archives in which we store that knowledge and the laboratories in which we retool our senses and practices to changing circumstances." (p. 1\)

"The balance between universalizing postulates and gritty specificity must be reset if we are to bring research about environments, technologies, and the everyday ... back towards the materiality and sensuality of direct encounters with the world beyond our skin." (p. 2\)

"The people in the way" had "no place to put" the disorganization they experienced "in their sensuous judgments, habitats, and workplaces. The grief for which they could not find words is often dismissed as nostalgia." (p. 8\)

"When the land is gone, then ... you can't go back. You just have to keep going ahead, regardless of what happens in your mind." — Joyce Fader, displaced from Iroquois (p. 193\)

"What price do they pay for contentment?" — Elizabeth McCarthy, age 93, of the Gagetown expropriation (p. 28\)

"Son, they're changing the face of the earth." — Cooke Sisty's father, watching the demolition of Iroquois Point (p. 80\)

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

Parr's work pairs well with several texts across all three reading lists. Leo Marx's *The Machine in the Garden* shares her concern with how technological intrusion transforms the experience of place, though Marx works through literary and symbolic analysis rather than the corporeal register Parr insists upon — the comparison highlights exactly the "linguistic turn" limitations Parr is challenging. Jennifer Light's *From Warfare to Welfare* offers a parallel Cold War story of expert planning overriding local knowledge, but from the perspective of defense intellectuals applying military techniques to urban problems rather than from the perspective of bodies being remade. Theodore Steinberg's *Nature Incorporated* similarly traces how large-scale engineering (New England's dammed rivers) destroyed place-based economies, though Steinberg emphasizes capital and law where Parr emphasizes perception and habit.

The book's main limitation is that its theoretical claims about the priority of corporeal over discursive knowledge sometimes outrun the evidence available: Parr is honest that much of what she describes is by definition "held beyond speech," which means the oral histories on which she relies are necessarily partial windows into the very tacit knowledge she argues is most important. The exclusively Canadian focus, while providing rich particularity, limits the book's engagement with the vast global literature on displacement — especially from the developing world — where megaprojects remain central instruments of statecraft.

# **Theodore Steinberg, *Nature Incorporated: Industrialization and the Waters of New England* (1991)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Steinberg argues that the rise of industrial capitalism in New England was fundamentally an environmental event — a systematic effort to control and master water for productive purposes. Focusing on the Waltham-Lowell textile mills along the Charles and Merrimack rivers, he shows that industrialization involved a profound restructuring of human-nature relations: water was progressively commodified, privatized, and subjected to minute corporate control, with dramatic consequences for river ecology, public health, and legal doctrine. Industrial capitalism, in his framing, is not only an economic system but "a system of ecological relations as well."

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Steinberg intervenes in a rich historiography on the Waltham-Lowell mills that had, by 1991, covered technological change, labor relations, gender, immigration, business history, and intellectual responses to industrialization — yet had almost entirely ignored the natural world. Historians like Thomas Dublin (*Women at Work*), Robert Dalzell (*Enterprising Elite*), and John Kasson (*Civilizing the Machine*) had treated Lowell as a story about people, machines, and social relations, but nature had been "there all along" and "historians have largely neglected it." Steinberg proposes environmental history as the missing counterpart to social history: where social historians see industrialization as a transformation in human relations with one another, Steinberg insists it equally transformed relations with the natural world. He draws explicitly on William Cronon's *Changes in the Land* (1983) and Carolyn Merchant's *Ecological Revolutions* (1989) for colonial New England's ecological history, but pushes their story forward into the industrial nineteenth century. His legal analysis engages Morton Horwitz's *The Transformation of American Law* (1977), extending Horwitz's argument about the shift from static to instrumental property doctrines specifically to water law. The book also builds on Louis Hunter's work on waterpower and positions itself within the broader "Studies in Environment and History" series edited by Donald Worster and Alfred Crosby, anchoring environmental history as a field with a central task: "to examine how the environment has shaped human cultures over time and to understand the effects of human activity on nature itself."

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Steinberg works within environmental history and political economy, drawing on economic and ecological anthropology (Polanyi, Godelier, Bennett). His core concept is the progressive **commodification of nature** — the process by which water was "conceived of, acted upon, and valued primarily for its capacity to be exchanged at market for profit." He treats industrial capitalism as involving the ever-more-thoroughgoing "incorporation of nature into the human agenda," with law, technology, and corporate organization all serving as instruments of that incorporation.

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

Steinberg relies on an eclectic mix of corporate archives (Proprietors of Locks and Canals, Essex Company papers, Amoskeag Manufacturing Company records, Boston Manufacturing Company records), legal case files from the New Hampshire Supreme Judicial Court, legislative records, government surveys (including George F. Swain's waterpower census), fish commission reports, public health board documents, and contemporary accounts including Thoreau's *A Week on the Concord and Merrimack Rivers*. Geographic scope is the Charles River valley and the Merrimack River valley from southeastern New Hampshire to the Massachusetts coast; temporal scope runs roughly from colonial settlement through the 1890s, with the heaviest concentration on 1813–1880.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**The Boston Manufacturing Company and the Charles River (1813–1820s).** The BMC's founding at Waltham in 1813 is Steinberg's origin story. Patrick Tracy Jackson purchased land and a mill privilege on the Charles, where the river dropped about twenty feet. The BMC erected a brick mill to produce cotton sheeting, becoming the first American factory to use waterpower for all major steps in making cotton cloth. Steinberg traces how this modest waterway — supporting only twenty-three small mills in 1812 — could barely sustain the BMC's demands. The Charles's limited power (mean discharge of only 368 cubic feet per second at Waltham) drove the Boston Associates to seek far more substantial water resources on the Merrimack. Steinberg uses this case to show how the industrial appetite for water quickly outstripped any single river's capacity, creating a logic of expansion that would reshape the entire region's waterscape.

**The Lake Company and the 1859 Dam Riot at Lake Village, New Hampshire.** This is Steinberg's most vivid case study and the dramatic center of the book. In 1845, the waterpower companies at Lowell and Lawrence purchased the Lake Company, gaining control over New Hampshire's four largest freshwater lakes — over 50,000 acres of surface water — to store water for mills downstream in Massachusetts. Local residents, whose land was flooded and whose livelihoods were disrupted, fought back. In September 1859, George W. Young and a group of perhaps fifty men attacked the Lake Company's dam at Lake Village. Young had first tried to provoke a legal proceeding — offering to let the company arrest him, offering arbitration, offering to submit the dispute to judges — but the company refused every avenue, knowing the law favored it. The mob pulled planks off the dam with axes and iron bars before being dispersed. A month later, someone planted ten pounds of gunpowder at the Amoskeag Company's dam in Manchester. Four legal cases followed. Steinberg uses the episode to argue that "not only the conflict over the workplace, over wages and hours, but the struggle to control and dominate nature is central to industrialization." The parallel between factory strikes and dam riots is the book's most powerful analytical move.

**The Evolution of New Hampshire Water Law (1820–1870s).** Steinberg traces how water law shifted from the old common-law natural-flow rule (*aqua currit et debet currere, ut currere solebat* — water flows and ought to flow as it has customarily flowed) to an instrumental doctrine favoring productive use. Early doctrine protected downstream owners from any diminution of flow; the prescriptive standard in *Bullen v. Runnels* (1820) required only twenty years of occupation to establish a water right. But by midcentury, courts increasingly favored "reasonable use" doctrines that privileged manufacturing. The *Gilford v. Winnipiseogee Lake Co.* case (1872) tested the Lake Company's prescriptive claims to flood surrounding land, and the jury rejected them — a rare loss for the company. Still, the overall trajectory of the law moved "firmly toward an instrumental conception of water use, toward a vision of water and law that sanctioned the maximization of economic growth." Steinberg shows how legal change and environmental change were deeply intertwined: the law didn't just reflect economic interests but actively restructured how water could be owned, sold, and controlled.

**Depleted Fisheries and the Fish Culture Movement (1860s–1870s).** Dams on the Merrimack blocked the migration of salmon, shad, and alewives — species that had once been so abundant that fishing rocks at Amoskeag Falls were individually claimed by fishermen. By the 1860s, salmon had virtually vanished from the river. Massachusetts appointed commissioners in 1866 to oversee fish restoration, and both states launched artificial propagation programs, importing salmon eggs from New Brunswick and Maine's Penobscot River. But the restoration effort was deeply shaped by industrial priorities: no one proposed leveling any dam to restore the ecosystem. Instead, the solution was fish culture — treating fish as another form of private property and another crop to be produced. In 1869, Massachusetts passed legislation extending private property rights to fish, doubling the size of ponds that could be privately controlled. Steinberg shows the cruel irony: the decline of fisheries was caused by the privatization of water for industry, and the proposed remedy was further privatization — "making fishes, under certain conditions, PROPERTY."

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"The burden of this study is to show that industrial capitalism is not only an economic system, but a system of ecological relations as well." (p. 11\)

"Human history is defined by the transformation and control of nature. The progressive reworking of the natural world to suit human needs and desires has been a constant throughout our history on this planet." (p. 12\)

"Industrial capitalism is as much a battle over nature as it is over work, as likely to result in strife involving water or land as wages or hours." (p. 16\)

"Instead of the scream of a fish-hawk scaring the fishes, is heard the whistle of the steam-engine arousing a country to its progress." (Thoreau, quoted by Steinberg in the Introduction, p. 8\)

"A fair stream is a mechanical power and a lavatory, but it is not a common sewer." (Massachusetts Report on Obstructions, quoted p. 192 — capturing the nineteenth-century view that rivers existed for production)

"This unquestioned attitude of dominance toward nature is among the most powerful legacies of industrialization. It is also an attitude we may rightly suspect to be little more than an illusion." (p. 271\)

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

Steinberg pairs naturally with William Cronon's *Nature's Metropolis* (1991), published the same year, which similarly examines how capitalist development reorganized nature across vast hinterlands — Cronon through Chicago's commodity markets, Steinberg through New England's waterways. The book also connects powerfully to Thomas Hughes's *Networks of Power* (1983) as a study of how large technical systems (here, hydraulic infrastructure rather than electrical grids) extend corporate control across regions and reshape both landscapes and legal frameworks. For the legal dimension, Morton Horwitz's *Transformation of American Law* is the essential companion: Steinberg essentially writes the environmental chapter that Horwitz's book lacked.

The book's geographic and temporal scope is somewhat narrow — limited to the Merrimack and Charles valleys and largely silent on how other New England industries or other regions experienced comparable environmental transformations. Steinberg also tends to treat nature primarily as something acted upon by humans rather than as an active agent in the historical process — a limitation that later environmental historians (like Richard White in *The Organic Machine*) would push past.

# **Daniel Yergin, *The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money, and Power* (1991; updated epilogue 2008\)**

---

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Oil has been the defining commodity of the twentieth century, shaping the rise of modern capitalism, the strategies and fates of nations, and the very texture of everyday life. Yergin argues that the story of oil is best understood through three interlocking themes: the development of corporate capitalism and modern business; the entanglement of petroleum with national strategy, geopolitics, and military power; and the emergence of a "Hydrocarbon Society" in which oil became the invisible infrastructure of daily existence—from suburbanization and automobiles to plastics and fast food. The common thread binding all three is the pursuit of "mastery"—Churchill's word—over the resource that underwrites modern civilization.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

*The Prize* intervened in a moment (1991) when oil had become so naturalized as a feature of modern life that few non-specialists understood the political and strategic dramas behind it. Yergin's major contribution was to synthesize what had been separate literatures—diplomatic history, business history, Middle Eastern studies, energy policy—into a single sweeping narrative that demonstrated how oil was not merely a technical or economic commodity but a force that actively reorganized international politics, military strategy, and social life. The book pushes back against two common simplifications: first, the idea that oil is "just another commodity" governed by simple market mechanisms, and second, the conspiracy-theory version in which shadowy oil companies pull all the strings. Instead, Yergin presents oil as a field of contest where companies, states, geologists, engineers, nationalists, and consumers all exercise agency under conditions of radical uncertainty. The Prize builds on the tradition of business history associated with Alfred Chandler (corporate strategy and organizational structure), while integrating the geopolitical perspective of diplomatic historians and the commodity-centered approach of scholars like Anthony Sampson (*The Seven Sisters*, 1975). But where Sampson focused primarily on the corporate cartel, Yergin widens the lens to include producing states, consuming societies, and the physical infrastructure that connects them. The result is a work that doubles as a history of twentieth-century geopolitics told from the vantage point of a single resource.

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Yergin works within a broadly narrative political-economic framework, treating oil as a strategic commodity whose control confers geopolitical power. There is no explicit social-science theory, but the book tacitly draws on concepts from business history (vertical integration, corporate strategy), international relations (resource competition, balance of power), and what we might now call infrastructure studies (the physical networks—pipelines, tankers, refineries, highways—through which oil flows). His key conceptual contribution is the figure of "Hydrocarbon Man," a phrase that functions similarly to what historians of technology call a sociotechnical system: the idea that oil doesn't just fuel machines but creates and sustains an entire way of life.

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

The book rests on an enormous base of archival research (corporate records, government documents, diplomatic cables), interviews with major figures in the oil world (from Sheikh Yamani to George H.W. Bush), memoirs, trade press, and secondary literature. It spans from the 1850s oil strikes in Pennsylvania to the 1991 Gulf War (and, in the 2008 epilogue, through the Iraq War and the oil price spike of 2004–2008). Geographic scope is global: the United States, the Middle East, Europe, Russia/the Soviet Union, Japan, Venezuela, Libya, and elsewhere.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**Marcus Samuel and the Murex's Voyage Through the Suez Canal (1892).** One of the book's most vivid episodes traces how Marcus Samuel—a Jewish merchant from London's East End whose father had sold decorative seashells—launched an audacious plan to break Standard Oil's monopoly in the Asian kerosene market. The scheme required commissioning a new type of tanker with advanced safety features, winning passage rights through the Suez Canal (which Standard Oil's lawyers lobbied furiously to block, citing spurious safety concerns while refusing to reveal their client), building storage tanks across every major Asian port, and securing a nine-year exclusive supply contract with the Rothschilds' Russian oil operation. The entire operation had to be coordinated in secret across multiple continents. In August 1892 the tanker Murex passed through the Canal carrying Russian kerosene, cutting four thousand miles off the route to Asia and slashing costs. The coup nearly failed, though, because Samuel had assumed Asian customers would bring their own containers. They wouldn't—Standard Oil's blue tin cans had become essential household items, repurposed as roofing, birdcages, and tea strainers. "Despairing telegrams began to flow into Houndsditch" until Samuel improvised a solution by supplying his own tins. This episode exemplifies Yergin's method: a story about corporate strategy and logistics that is simultaneously about imperialism, technology, cultural adaptation, and the way consumer habits in distant markets could derail the most sophisticated plans.

**The Creation of OPEC (September 1960).** Yergin presents the founding of OPEC as a case study in how corporate arrogance creates the conditions for its own loss of control. Standard Oil of New Jersey's chairman, Jack Rathbone—an engineer whose entire career had been spent in domestic operations—overrode his own Middle East expert, Howard Page, and unilaterally cut posted prices by about 7 percent, slashing the national revenues of producing countries without consulting any of them. Page had tried to educate the board about the rising force of nationalism; journalist Wanda Jablonski had warned them directly that the existing concession structure might prove "short-lived." Rathbone dismissed both. Within hours of the announcement, Abdullah Tariki of Saudi Arabia telegrammed Juan Pablo Pérez Alfonzo of Venezuela. Iraq, seeing a chance to isolate oil policy from Nasser's influence, hosted the founding meeting in Baghdad. By September 14, 1960, OPEC existed. Yet neither the companies nor Western governments took it seriously at first: a secret CIA report two months later devoted just four lines to the new organization. Yergin uses this story to show how the shift in oil power from companies to countries was not inevitable—it was precipitated by specific decisions made by specific people who failed to understand the political context in which they operated.

**The Suez Crisis (1956) and the Geopolitics of Oil Transit.** Yergin devotes an entire chapter to the Suez Crisis, showing how the Canal's function shifted from being the "highway of empire" (connecting Britain to India) to being the "highway of oil" (carrying two-thirds of Europe's petroleum). When Nasser nationalized the Canal, the British and French response was shaped by traumatic historical memory: Eden, Macmillan, and Mollet all saw Nasser as a new Hitler and the seizure as a new Rhineland. Macmillan, who privately read through massive Victorian novels to keep from going "barmy," wrote in his diary that Britain faced a terrible dilemma: act and risk losing the oil supply through retaliation, or do nothing and watch Nasser "nationalize oil" across the region. The crisis revealed that oil had become the real strategic rationale for maintaining influence in the Middle East, replacing the older imperial logic of defending the route to India. It also demonstrated the decisive shift of geopolitical power from Britain and France to the United States, which refused to support military intervention. Yergin frames the entire episode as a hinge moment in which oil, decolonization, Cold War politics, and the memory of appeasement all collided.

**"Hydrocarbon Man" and the Suburbanization of America.** Yergin's chapter on postwar consumer society is the most directly relevant to urban history on your reading list. He shows how cheap oil produced a cascading transformation: coal gave way to oil, public transit gave way to automobiles, and cities gave way to suburbs. He traces the arc from Levittown (1946, houses priced at $7,990–$9,500, eventually 82,000 residents) through the Interstate Highway Act (1956, Eisenhower's comparison that the concrete would "build eighty Hoover Dams or six sidewalks to the moon"), to the proliferation of shopping centers (from 8 in 1946 to 20,000 by the early 1980s), motels, drive-in churches, and McDonald's. Between 1950 and 1976, 10 million Americans moved to central cities while 85 million moved to suburbs. Yergin frames all of this as dependent on the specific circumstance of cheap and abundant petroleum—not as an inevitable American destiny, but as the product of a particular energy regime that most people took entirely for granted. He closes the chapter with E.F. Schumacher, the economic adviser to Britain's National Coal Board, who warned that energy "is not 'just another commodity,' but the precondition of all commodities," and that the world's cheapest reserves were located in its most unstable countries.

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"To commit the Navy irrevocably to oil was indeed 'to take arms against a sea of troubles.' … Mastery itself was the prize of the venture." (Prologue, quoting Churchill)

"Oil is almost like money." (Prologue, quoting an unnamed tycoon)

"The abundance of oil begat the proliferation of the automobile, which begat a completely new way of life. This was indeed the era of Hydrocarbon Man." (Ch. 27\)

"There is no substitute for energy. The whole edifice of modern life is built upon it. Although energy can be bought and sold like any other commodity, it is not 'just another commodity,' but the precondition of all commodities, a basic factor equally with air, water and earth." (Ch. 27, quoting Schumacher)

"No man who owns his own house and lot can be a Communist. He has too much to do." (Ch. 27, quoting William Levitt)

"I must be absolutely blunt about the oil, because we would fight for it. We could not live without oil and we had no intention of being strangled to death." (Ch. 24, quoting Eden to Soviet leaders)

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

*The Prize* pairs naturally with William Cronon's *Nature's Metropolis* (both treat a commodity as the engine that reorganizes landscapes, politics, and daily life, though Cronon works at a regional scale and Yergin at a global one) and with Thomas Hughes's *Networks of Power* (both trace how large technical systems create path dependencies that reshape societies, though Hughes focuses on electrification and Yergin on petroleum). For the history of Cold War technoscience, it complements Paul Edwards's *The Closed World* by providing the resource-strategic backdrop against which Cold War computing and defense intellectualism unfolded. On suburbanization specifically, the "Hydrocarbon Man" chapter reads as an energy-systems companion to Kenneth Jackson's *Crabgrass Frontier*.

Two limitations stand out. First, for a book about a resource extracted from the earth, *The Prize* is strikingly uninterested in environmental consequences—pollution, climate change, and ecological damage appear only as afterthoughts in the epilogue, not as forces internal to the story. Second, Yergin's sympathies tilt toward the perspective of Western corporate and government elites; the voices and experiences of people in oil-producing regions (workers, communities displaced by extraction, populations affected by the "resource curse") are largely absent.

# 

# **Rosalind Williams, *Notes on the Underground: An Essay on Technology, Society, and the Imagination* (MIT Press, 1990; new edition 2008\)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Williams argues that since the nineteenth century, narratives about underground worlds—both real subterranean infrastructure and fictional stories of subterranean societies—have served as the most powerful prophetic model for understanding what happens when human beings live in a predominantly built rather than natural environment. The underground, as both fact and metaphor, reveals the psychological, social, and political consequences of enclosure in a "manufactured environment," consequences that range from degeneration of character and loss of community to the collapse of independent moral authority and the paradoxical creation of new forms of insecurity.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Williams is writing at the intersection of the history of technology, environmental history, and literary/cultural history at a moment (the late 1980s) when these fields were largely separate. Her primary interlocutor is Lewis Mumford, whose concept of the mine as a "manufactured environment" in *Technics and Civilization* (1934) provides the book's conceptual foundation—Williams literally traces Mumford's drafts to show him inventing the term. She builds on Mumford but pushes his framework in two directions he did not fully develop: first, toward sustained analysis of the literary imagination as a legitimate source of knowledge about technological life (not merely illustration of it), and second, toward what would later be called environmental consciousness—the awareness that humanity lives inside a finite, closed system rather than atop an infinite one. The book also engages Leo Marx's argument in *The Machine in the Garden* about the pastoral ideal in American literature, but Williams contends that British and French writers operate on a starker vertical axis (surface vs. subsurface) rather than Marx's horizontal one (city vs. garden vs. wilderness), because in the Old World there was no "middle landscape" to retreat to. She challenges scholars who equate science with Baconian exploitation, insisting (following Donald Worster's "arcadian" tradition) that science can expand rather than diminish the human imagination. The 2008 Afterword updates the argument for the age of climate change, contending that "subterranean consciousness"—the awareness of living inside a closed, finite system—is precisely what environmental consciousness has become.

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Cultural history of technology informed by literary analysis and environmental thought. Williams treats technologies as *environments* rather than discrete objects, and she uses what Fredric Jameson calls "world-reduction"—the systematic exclusion of empirical complexity to isolate a single variable—as her analytical principle. The underground functions as a thought experiment in which nature is surgically removed to reveal what remains. She also draws on the concept of the "technological sublime" (anticipating David Nye's later work) and introduces her own concept of the "social sublime," in which powerful actors orchestrate technological spectacles to instill feelings of helplessness in the relatively powerless.

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

Williams combines close reading of nineteenth- and early-twentieth-century fiction (Wells, Verne, Forster, Tarde, Bulwer-Lytton, Villiers de l'Isle-Adam, Kafka) with historical analysis of actual excavation projects (railway tunnels, the Paris sewer system, the London Underground, Alpine tunnels) and intellectual history (geology, archaeology, evolutionary biology, structuralism, psychoanalysis). She also draws on Mumford's manuscript drafts at the University of Pennsylvania. The geographic scope is primarily Britain and France, with some attention to broader European developments; the temporal range runs roughly from the Enlightenment through the early twentieth century, with the 2008 Afterword extending to the present.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**Mumford's drafts and the invention of "manufactured environment."** Williams traces three successive drafts of the mining passage in *Technics and Civilization* through Mumford's papers at the University of Pennsylvania. In his first draft (1933), Mumford described the mine as an "environment of work" and scrawled in the margin "Necessity to live by artificial means." In his second draft, he added language about artificial light and ventilation but concluded the passage with the phrase "a triumph, too, of the non-environment." Only in the final draft did he realize that "non-environment" was wrong—life, by definition, occurs in some environment—and settled on "manufactured environment," which he placed in quotation marks to signal that he was coining a term. This granular attention to Mumford's compositional process shows the difficulty of even *naming* the condition of living in a wholly built world. Williams uses this to establish her central claim: the underground is a more effective image of a manufactured environment than the city (Spengler's choice) or the spaceship (the twentieth century's favorite), because it combines total enclosure with the ancient power of vertical descent.

**The Paris sewer system and Haussmann's underground city.** Williams recounts how the Paris sewers expanded tenfold between 1800 and 1870, from roughly 40 miles to nearly 350 miles, and how Victor Hugo devoted long chapters of *Les Misérables* to the heroism and danger of this construction. Under Baron Haussmann and his chief of water services Eugène Belgrand during the Second Empire, the old labyrinth that Jean Valjean supposedly escaped through was transformed into a comprehensive system of multipurpose underground galleries—egg-shaped tunnels of hydraulic cement large enough to carry not just sewage but also water lines, gas lines, steam pipes, pneumatic tubes, and electrical cables suspended from their walls and roofs. By the 1867 international exposition, the sewers had become a tourist attraction recommended by Baedeker. Williams uses this case to illustrate her broader argument about the "networked city": that the modern metropolis is defined not by its surface architecture but by its buried subsystems, and that this underground infrastructure made the city of skyscrapers possible. Hugo's metaphor—comparing sewer construction to a military campaign and calling the engineers' deaths "more profitable, however, than the stupid slaughter of the battlefield"—captures the ambivalence of the era: underground labor was simultaneously glorified as civilization's highest expression and hidden from public view.

**H. G. Wells's *The Time Machine* and the Eloi/Morlock split.** Wells, deeply shaped by Thomas Huxley's lectures on evolution and the anxiety that biological fitness bore no relation to ethical principles, imagined the year 802701 as split between two degenerate species: the Eloi, beautiful but helpless surface-dwellers who represent the decay that follows "the too perfect triumph of man" over nature; and the Morlocks, brutalized underground laborers who operate subterranean machines and prey on the Eloi for meat. Williams reads this not just as biological speculation but as a political parable—"Darwinism and Marxism coincide"—in which the contemporary class struggle has evolved into a permanent species division. The two poles of degeneration (weakness from over-comfort and brutality from exploitation) structure her entire analysis of subterranean fiction. She shows how Wells's scientific training gave his fantasy a rigor that distinguished it from mere adventure: the Eloi's passive hedonism and the Morlocks' violent predation are both presented as evolutionary consequences of specific environmental conditions, not moral failings.

**Forster's "The Machine Stops" and the social sublime.** E. M. Forster's 1909 short story imagines a world in which all humanity lives in underground cells, every need supplied by the Machine, and all human contact mediated through a kind of proto-internet. Williams treats this as the most prescient text in her study—not because it predicted specific technologies (though the communication system is strikingly internet-like), but because it grasped that technological degeneration, not human degeneration, would be the real danger. Forster's character Vashti, "the quintessential consumer under late capitalism," enjoys comfort but suffers chronic irritation and helplessness: her dominant emotion is anger at petty malfunctions she cannot fix. Williams identifies a pivotal episode in which the Machine's "mending apparatus" destroys a gentle valley in Wessex—an act she reads as deliberate political destruction of nature, not accidental byproduct. She introduces the concept of the "social sublime" to describe how the Machine instills feelings of powerlessness: natural despoliation becomes "a requirement, rather than an accidental byproduct, of a social and political system that excludes from its moral frame of reference values that are not material, utilitarian, and presentist." This reading transforms Forster's story from a quaint anti-technology fable into an analysis of how power operates through infrastructure.

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"What are the consequences when human beings dwell in an environment that is predominantly built rather than given?" (p. 1\)

"The mine . . . is the first completely inorganic environment to be created and lived in by man: far more inorganic than the giant city that Spengler has used as a symbol of the last stages of mechanical desiccation." (p. 69—Mumford quoted by Williams from *Technics and Civilization*)

"What is commonly called environmental consciousness could be described as subterranean consciousness—the awareness that we are in a very real sense not on the earth but inside it." (p. 212\)

"Our environment will inevitably become less natural; the question is whether it will also become less human." (p. 213\)

"Natural despoliation is not just a result of economic pressures; it is also a political action aimed at removing a source of subversion." (pp. 146–148)

"The drift of modern history domesticates the fantastic and normalizes the unspeakable." (p. 209—Paul Fussell quoted by Williams)

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

Williams pairs most naturally with Leo Marx's *The Machine in the Garden*, which she is explicitly revising by proposing a vertical rather than horizontal axis for European responses to technological environments; with David Nye's *American Technological Sublime*, which develops the concept of sublimity she deploys throughout; and with Lewis Mumford's *Technics and Civilization*, which provides her point of departure. For exam purposes, the book also connects powerfully to the infrastructure and networks literature on the HoT list—particularly Hughes's *Networks of Power* (the "networked city" concept), Nye's *Electrifying America*, and Starosielski's *The Undersea Network* (buried/submerged infrastructure as both material and imaginative). In urban history, it speaks directly to the role of underground systems in shaping metropolitan life (compare Cronon's *Nature's Metropolis* on infrastructure and hinterland, or Needham's *Power Lines* on how infrastructure shaped political geography).

One limitation is the book's almost exclusive focus on Britain and France, which Williams acknowledges but does not fully resolve—the American underground tradition (Baum, Burroughs) is mentioned only to be set aside. A second is that Williams's argument works better as cultural history than as social history: the actual workers who built the tunnels, sewers, and subways appear mainly as victims or symbols rather than as agents with their own perspectives on the manufactured environment.

# **David Edgerton, *The Shock of the Old: Technology and Global History since 1900* (2006)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Edgerton argues that the history of technology has been distorted by an obsessive focus on invention and innovation, which blinds us to the technologies that actually shaped the twentieth century. By shifting attention from the moment of invention to the life of technology *in use* — maintenance, adaptation, disappearance, reappearance, and global circulation — a radically different picture emerges, one in which the horse, the condom, corrugated iron, the sewing machine, and the rickshaw matter as much as or more than the airplane, nuclear power, and the internet.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Edgerton is writing against the entire framework through which both historians and the public understand technological change: what he calls "innovation-centric" history. This framework privileges novelty, dates technology by invention, and assumes that the newest technologies are the most significant. It runs through popular futurism ("ever-increasing rates of change"), academic scholarship on "general-purpose technologies" (steam, electricity, ICT), policy discourse about "national systems of innovation," and standard museum curation that displays airplanes and atomic bombs but not rifles or corrugated iron roofs. Edgerton directly challenges the periodization schemes that organize most histories of technology — the idea that the late nineteenth century was "the age of electricity," the mid-twentieth "the atomic age," and the late twentieth "the information age." He argues these labels merely recycle the promotional claims made by boosters at the time of innovation, not the results of serious historical analysis. He engages critically with Schumpeterian economics and endogenous growth theory, with techno-nationalist frameworks that tie national prosperity to R\&D spending, and with the declinist narratives (especially about Britain) built on innovation-centric assumptions. He also takes aim at the idea of "resistance to technology," arguing it makes no sense in a world overflowing with alternatives — choosing one technology necessarily means rejecting many others, and that is rational, not backward. The book is in dialogue with Bruno Latour (whose observation that "we have never been modern" Edgerton draws on for his scrambled sense of technological time), with Robert Fogel's counterfactual economic history (particularly Fogel's argument that railroads added less than 5% to US GDP in 1890), and with the social construction of technology tradition, though Edgerton's focus is squarely on use rather than the design-stage negotiations that SCOT emphasizes.

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Edgerton works within a loosely materialist, globally comparative framework focused on technology-in-use rather than technology-in-design. His key conceptual moves are: distinguishing significance from novelty (using counterfactual reasoning about alternatives); insisting on maintenance and repair as central categories of technological life; and introducing the idea of "creole technologies" — local adaptations and hybrids that give imported or "old" technologies new lives in new contexts, particularly in the Global South.

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

Edgerton draws on an extraordinarily wide range of secondary literature spanning economic history, military history, development studies, and the history of technology, supplemented by production statistics, wartime assessment reports (especially the US Strategic Bombing Survey), national accounts data, and comparative global examples. The scope is global and covers the entire twentieth century, with particular attention to cases from the Global South, the Soviet bloc, and wartime economies that standard histories of technology ignore.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**The horse in twentieth-century warfare.** This is Edgerton's most vivid demonstration that innovation-centric history distorts our understanding. The German army, usually portrayed as the pinnacle of mechanized Blitzkrieg warfare, was in fact fundamentally dependent on horses. For the 1941 invasion of the Soviet Union, the Wehrmacht assembled 625,000 horses; by early 1945, it had 1.2 million. Each infantry division needed roughly 5,000 horses to move itself. The Wehrmacht marched to Moscow with many times more horses than Napoleon's Grand Armée — and took longer to get there. In the British case, the army went from 25,000 horses before WWI to 591,000 horses, 213,000 mules, 47,000 camels, and 11,000 oxen by mid-1917, with Britain buying 429,000 horses and 275,000 mules from the United States alone. The horse was not a quaint holdover; it was the "basic means of transport in the German Army" and a decisive factor in every major twentieth-century land war through Korea. The case demonstrates that what looks like a "pre-modern" technology was in fact at peak use during the most supposedly industrial wars in history.

**Corrugated iron and the "creole technologies" of the poor megacity.** Edgerton uses corrugated iron roofing to illustrate his argument about the "new technologies of poverty" — technologies that are simultaneously old (invented in the nineteenth century) and genuinely new in the sense that their massive global deployment is a twentieth-century phenomenon with no historical precedent. Corrugated iron became the defining building material of the Global South: cheap, light, long-lasting, easy to use. In Rwanda, it was the only part of the house villagers could not make themselves, and it became so prized that corrugated iron sheets were looted from Tutsi homes during the 1994 genocide; Hutu refugees later carried them on their backs or buried them in fields. Alongside corrugated iron, Edgerton discusses asbestos-cement ("Eternit," patented 1901), cement, and the oil-drum-derived materials of the *bidonville* (literally "tin-can town"). He frames these through the Martinican novelist Patrick Chamoiseau's periodization in *Texaco*, which divides Martinique's history not by inventions but by the materials of its shanty towns: the age of straw, the age of crate wood, the age of asbestos, the age of concrete. These are the technologies that actually built the cities where most of humanity now lives.

**The atomic bomb and V-2 as cost-ineffective technologies.** Edgerton deploys the US Strategic Bombing Survey's own data to make a startlingly deflationary argument about nuclear weapons. The USSBS concluded that the Hiroshima bomb did the same damage as roughly 220 B-29s on a conventional raid; the Nagasaki bomb equaled about 125 B-29s' worth. The effective destructive equivalence was 500–2,500 tons of TNT, not the usually cited 10,000–20,000, because most of the bomb's explosive power was not directed at the target. Even the Los Alamos scientists knew this: a key 1945 committee meeting was told that "one atomic bomb on an arsenal would not be much different from the effect caused by any Air Corps strike of present dimensions." Target selection required finding cities that had not yet been bombed conventionally — atomic targets had to be "reserved." The Manhattan Project cost roughly $2 billion ($20 billion in 1996 dollars) — one billion dollars per destroyed city, when a conventional raid could have done equivalent damage at minimal additional cost. The V-2, meanwhile, killed more people in its production (at least 10,000 slave laborers) than it killed on impact (about 5,000), costing $500 million to produce roughly the same destruction as a single conventional bombing raid. Edgerton's point is not that these weapons were unimportant, but that their significance was manufactured after the fact by myth-making (especially the "saved one million lives" narrative), and that they were spectacularly poor uses of resources compared to mundane alternatives like more tanks, artillery, or B-29s.

**The Ghanaian Suame Magazine and the "biography" of a Peugeot 504\.** To illustrate the centrality of maintenance and repair, Edgerton describes the Suame Magazine in Kumasi, Ghana — a vast open-air complex of car repairers that grew from 6,000 workers in 1971 to 40,000 by the mid-1980s. Working with hammers, incomplete spanner sets, files, and improvised anvils (the entire complex had single-digit numbers of machine tools), these "fitters" could not maintain vehicles to factory specifications. Instead, something remarkable happened: as imported vehicles degraded through accidents and lack of proper maintenance, they reached what one observer called "a state of apparent equilibrium in which it seems to be maintained indefinitely … a condition of maintenance by constant repair." Vehicles became essentially eternal in this state, in near-daily interaction with a workshop. Edgerton pairs this with the "biography" of a specific Peugeot 504 used as a long-distance taxi by a driver named Kwaku, who purchased a second 504 body and a second engine as sources of spare parts. Over years, the car was rebuilt, rewired, given a new carburettor; replacement gaskets were made from old tires, fuses replaced by copper wire, nails used as lock-pins. This was not dangerous incompetence — it was "a rather unique type of knowledge of how one can keep old ones working in a situation of limited goods," a form of what Edgerton calls the "creolisation of technology."

## **6\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"By thinking about the history of technology-in-use a radically different picture of technology, and indeed of invention and innovation, becomes possible. A whole invisible world of technologies appears." (p. xi)

"In the new picture, twentieth-century technology is not just a matter of electricity, mass production, aerospace, nuclear power, the internet and the contraceptive pill. It will involve the rickshaw, the condom, the horse, the sewing machine, the spinning wheel, the Haber-Bosch process … bicycles, corrugated iron, cement, asbestos, DDT, the chain saw and the refrigerator. The horse made a greater contribution to Nazi conquest than the V2." (p. xii)

"It is absurd to talk of resistance to technology or innovation in a world where individuals or societies simply could not accept every innovation, or indeed product, on offer. Resistance is required." (p. 9\)

"The largest private spenders on research and development at the end of the twentieth century were not computer giants, or even pharmaceutical firms, but motor-car producers — General Motors and the Ford Motor Company top the list, not Microsoft or Novartis." (p. 188\)

"Calling for innovation is, paradoxically, a common way of avoiding change when change is not wanted. The argument that future science and technology will deal with global warming is an instance. It is implicitly arguing that in today's world only what we have is possible. Yet we have the technological capacity to do things very differently: we are not technologically determined." (p. 210\)

"The history of invention is not the history of a necessary future to which we must adapt or die, but rather of failed futures, and of futures firmly fixed in the past." (p. 210\)

## **7\. CONNECTIONS**

Edgerton pairs naturally with Vinsel and Russell's *The Innovation Delusion*, which extends his critique of innovation-worship into a contemporary polemic about the neglect of maintenance; with Wisnioski's *Every American an Innovator*, which historicizes the ideology of innovation that Edgerton attacks; and with Cronon's *Nature's Metropolis*, which similarly reconstructs the material infrastructure (including animal labor and commodity flows) behind a supposedly modern industrial order. The book also works as a counterpoint to Hughes's *American Genesis*, which celebrates the inventive tradition Edgerton wants to decenter, and to Edwards's *The Closed World*, which takes the computer-centered periodization more seriously than Edgerton would.

Two limitations are worth noting. First, the book's relentless parade of examples can feel more like a cabinet of curiosities than a sustained analytical argument — Edgerton demonstrates that innovation-centric history is wrong far more convincingly than he builds a positive theoretical framework for use-centered history. Second, while the book is genuinely global in its examples, it does not engage deeply with the politics of why innovation-centric narratives have been so powerful and for whom — the political economy of who benefits from futurism gets gestured at but never fully developed.

# **Thomas P. Hughes, *Networks of Power: Electrification in Western Society, 1880–1930* (1983)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Hughes argues that electric power systems evolved through identifiable phases—invention, technology transfer, system growth, momentum, and regional consolidation—and that this evolution was shaped not by technology alone but by a seamless interweaving of technical, economic, political, and geographical factors. The systems that emerged in different places reflected the values, institutions, and power arrangements of the societies that built them, making electric power networks "cultural artifacts" as much as engineering achievements.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Before Hughes, the history of technology was largely written either as internalist accounts of inventions and machines divorced from social context, or as "impact" studies tracing how finished technologies affected society. Hughes rejected both approaches. He proposed instead that technology and society are mutually constitutive and that the appropriate unit of analysis is the *system*—not the individual device or even the individual inventor. This was a direct challenge to the prevailing tendency to write heroic narratives centered on lone geniuses (Edison, Tesla) or to treat technology as an autonomous force marching forward under its own logic. Hughes was also intervening against a simplistic form of technological determinism—the idea that machines "make history" on their own—while simultaneously refusing the equally simplistic claim that society fully controls technology. His model of "technological momentum" offered a middle path: young systems are open to social shaping, but as they mature and accumulate institutional, financial, educational, and physical mass, they develop an inertia that increasingly constrains the choices available to the societies that host them. The book appeared two years before the landmark *Social Construction of Technological Systems* (SCOT) volume that Hughes co-edited with Bijker and Pinch, and it provided the Large Technical Systems (LTS) framework that became one of the three foundational approaches in science and technology studies alongside SCOT and actor-network theory. Hughes was in dialogue with Alfred Chandler's work on managerial capitalism (*The Visible Hand*), with systems theorists like Ludwig von Bertalanffy, and with historians of industrialization like David Landes (*The Unbound Prometheus*).

## **3\. THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK**

Hughes develops what became known as the Large Technical Systems (LTS) approach. His key analytical concepts are: *system builders* (individuals who coordinate technical, economic, political, and organizational elements); *reverse salients* (components that fall behind the advancing front of a system, analogous to sections of a military line that lag); *critical problems* (the solvable formulations that engineers derive from reverse salients); *technological momentum* (the increasing inertia of maturing systems as they accumulate institutional, financial, and physical commitments); and *regional style* (the distinctive configurations that systems take on in different geographical and cultural settings).

## **4\. METHODOLOGY & SOURCES**

Hughes draws on corporate archives (Edison companies, AEG, Siemens & Halske, Commonwealth Edison), engineering trade journals, published speeches (especially Samuel Insull's), legislative records, patent documents, and utility company histories across the United States, Germany, and England from 1880 to 1930\. The study is explicitly comparative, examining how the same pool of available technology produced markedly different systems in different urban and regional settings.

## **5\. KEY CASE STUDIES**

**Edison's Pearl Street Station and the invention of a system (Ch. II).** Hughes shows that Edison did not simply invent the incandescent lightbulb—he invented an entire system of electric supply, including generators, distribution mains, meters, junction boxes, and the financial and organizational arrangements needed to make them work together. Edison worked backward from the economics of the existing gas lighting system to determine what his electrical system would need to look like to be competitive: he calculated the cost of copper wire, figured out that he needed high-resistance filaments to minimize conductor costs, and organized his Menlo Park laboratory as a mirror image of the system being designed. This case demonstrates Hughes's argument that invention is fundamentally a system-level activity. Edison's genius was not narrowly technical but lay in his ability to hold together technical, economic, and organizational variables simultaneously—what Hughes calls the work of the "inventor-entrepreneur."

**Berlin, Chicago, and London compared (Chs. VII–IX).** These three chapters form the comparative heart of the book and illustrate Hughes's argument that electric power systems are cultural artifacts shaped by their political environments. In Berlin, an efficient and progressive municipal government coordinated closely with the private utility (Berliner Elektrizitäts-Werke, an AEG subsidiary) and the major electrical manufacturers (AEG and Siemens & Halske), producing a system where technology and politics were well coordinated. The city's industrial character—over 50 percent of workers employed in industry, with Berlin serving as Germany's *Elektropolis*—created enormous demand for electric power. In Chicago, Samuel Insull built a monopolistic, technologically advanced system relatively free from political constraint, because the city's government was weak, fragmented, and pliable. Technology dominated politics. Insull ruthlessly pursued higher load factors by diversifying his customer base—adding streetcar loads to complement lighting loads, industrial night shifts to fill off-peak hours—and drove down unit costs through mass production of kilowatts. In London, by stark contrast, politics dominated technology. A byzantine administrative structure of sixty-five separate utilities, fragmented among dozens of boroughs with jealously guarded local autonomy, produced a system that contemporaries called "the maddest sort of performance"—seventy generating stations averaging only 5,285 kw, ten different frequencies, and twenty-four voltage levels for distribution. London's electric supply per capita was the lowest of any major Western city.
