First, he examines whether we even know nuclear missiles would work at all, recounting the remarkable 1962 Operation Frigate Bird—the only time a live nuclear warhead was fired on a ballistic missile (a Polaris, launched from the submarine USS Ethan Allen toward Christmas Island). Even after this test succeeded, skeptics like Air Force Chief of Staff Curtis LeMay and presidential candidate Barry Goldwater argued it proved nothing about ICBMs, since the Polaris trajectory was different and the missile had been modified. Second, MacKenzie examines the construction of missile accuracy figures themselves, showing that these depend on a chain of assumptions about testing conditions, atmospheric models, gravitational maps, and statistical methods—none of which is self-evident. Public controversy erupted in the early 1980s about whether Soviet missile accuracies were facts at all. Third, he goes inside the guidance system to show that even the apparently simple question "which is the most accurate gyroscope?" cannot be answered in a way that compels universal agreement, because testing procedures, environmental conditions, and criteria of evaluation are all contestable. This chapter provides the theoretical basis for the book's concluding argument: if accuracy is constructed, it can in principle be deconstructed.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"This book reveals just how wrong it is to assume that missile accuracy is a natural or inevitable consequence of technical change. Nor, however, has it come about simply because governments, for good or bad reasons, have desired it. Rather, it is the product of a complex process of conflict and collaboration between a range of social actors including ambitious, energetic technologists, laboratories and corporations, and political and military leaders and the organizations they head." (p. 3\)

"A technology is not just social up to the point of invention and self-sustaining thereafter. Its conditions of possibility are always social. As we shall see, it follows from this that there may be a very real, and politically important, sense in which accuracy can be uninvented." (p. 4\)

"People had to be engineered, too—persuaded to suspend their doubts, induced to provide resources, trained and motivated to play their parts in a production process unprecedented in its demands. Successfully inventing the technology turned out to be heterogeneous engineering, the engineering of the social as well as the physical world." (p. 28\)

"The single most important lesson of this book is the fallacy of this technological determinism. What we have found is that technological change is social through-and-through. Take away the institutional structures that support technological change of a particular sort, and it ceases to seem 'natural'—indeed it ceases altogether." (p. 384\)

"What is wrong is the fundamental idea that technological change can be self-sustaining, that it can take place independent of the conditions of its social environment." (p. 167, approximate)

"Who knows whether an intercontinental ballistic missile with a nuclear warhead will actually work? Each of the constituent elements has been tested, it is true. Each of them, however, has not been tested under circumstances which would be attendant upon the firing of such a missile in anger." (p. 342, quoting the House Armed Services Committee, 1961\)

## **5\. CONNECTIONS**

**To HoT list:** MacKenzie is in direct conversation with the SCOT framework (Bijker, Hughes, Pinch—\#9) and with Winner's critique of social constructivism (\#11), since Winner would ask whether MacKenzie's symmetrical analysis adequately addresses the *politics* embedded in accuracy as an artifact. The book is a landmark application of the large technical systems approach (Hughes, *Networks of Power*—\#39) to military technology. It speaks to Heilbroner (\#16) and the technological determinism debate (\#14) as perhaps the most sustained empirical refutation of determinism in the field. The concept of "heterogeneous engineering" connects to Latour, and the discussion of "technological trajectories" engages the economics-of-innovation literature (Rosenberg, \#62; Nelson and Winter). MacKenzie's own "Marx and the Machine" (\#17) is a companion piece.

**To HoS list:** The book's treatment of testing as a social process connects directly to Collins's *Changing Order* (\#28) and to Shapin and Schaffer (\#31) on the social construction of experimental knowledge. The "construction of technical facts" argument parallels Latour and Woolgar's *Laboratory Life* (\#21). The Cold War rationality theme connects to Erickson et al. (\#36), and the role of the Draper Laboratory as a site of knowledge production resonates with Warwick (\#15) on the institutional shaping of technical skill.

**To Urban History list:** O'Mara's *Cities of Knowledge* (\#17/56) and Light's *From Warfare to Welfare* (\#39/31) both examine the Cold War defense-intellectual complex that sustained the institutions MacKenzie studies, particularly the MIT-to-Silicon-Valley pipeline and the movement of systems analysis from military to civilian applications.

**Cross-list value:** This book appears on the HoT list (\#26) and is in dialogue with texts on both the HoS and HoT lists (MacKenzie's *Mechanizing Proof* \#24/\#43, Erickson et al. \#72/\#36, Edwards \#66/\#71). It is a high-value connective text: its arguments about the social construction of technical facts, the critique of technological determinism, and the institutional analysis of laboratories and military-industrial organizations are deployable across all three fields.

# **Marion Fourcade and Kieran Healy, *The Ordinal Society* (Harvard University Press, 2024\)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

We live in an "ordinal society"—a social order organized around the continuous, automated measurement, ranking, and matching of individuals through digital data. Fourcade and Healy argue that the massive expansion of personal data collection, combined with increasingly powerful analytical tools, has created a new form of stratification in which people are sorted into "classification situations" that shape their life chances across markets, institutions, and the state. This process is experienced by its beneficiaries as natural and deserved, producing a moralized hierarchy where systematic outcomes look like the fair consequences of individual choices.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Fourcade and Healy are intervening in several overlapping conversations. Against Shoshana Zuboff's *The Age of Surveillance Capitalism* (2019), which frames data extraction primarily as corporate theft and covert surveillance, they insist that much personal data is *freely given* through a kind of Maussian gift exchange—people hand over information in return for services, sociality, and convenience, making the process far more participatory and harder to resist than a surveillance framing suggests. Against purely technical or legal critiques of algorithmic bias (Eubanks, O'Neil, Benjamin), they offer a structural sociological account: the problem is not just that algorithms are poorly designed but that any system measuring individual behavior in a deeply unequal society will inevitably reconstitute group-level inequalities at the individual level. Drawing heavily on Pierre Bourdieu's theory of capital and distinction, and on Max Weber's concept of "life chances," they propose that digital data creates a new species of resource—"eigencapital"—that operates like cultural capital but is generated, accumulated, and expressed through digital traces rather than educational credentials.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

### **Chapter 3: Classification Situations**

This is the theoretical heart of the book. Fourcade and Healy introduce their central concept: **classification situations**, defined as positions in algorithmically generated systems of categories that carry real consequences for what people can access, what prices they pay, and how they are treated. They distinguish this from conventional demographic categories (race, gender, class) by emphasizing that classification situations are *independently generated*—they emerge inductively from behavioral data and may not map neatly onto familiar social groups, even though they often reproduce the same patterns of advantage and disadvantage. The chapter walks through Oscar Gandy Jr.'s concept of the "panoptic sort" (from the early 1990s) as a prescient description of automated, discriminatory classification, then updates it for the era of machine learning. A devastating case study concerns Virginia Eubanks's research on Allegheny County, Pennsylvania's predictive model for child abuse. Because the system optimized on available proxies—likelihood of another hotline call, likelihood of foster care placement—rather than the actual (extremely rare) outcome of child fatality, it effectively profiled poverty. Poor families were overrepresented in both the predictor variables and the outcome variables because they interacted with public services constantly, while suburban middle-class families were invisible to the system. The chapter then introduces **eigencapital**: the totality of one's digitally recorded interactions conceived as a form of resource, analogous to Bourdieu's cultural capital but expressed through data traces. Fourcade and Healy distinguish three forms—embodied (the "feel" of smooth digital transactions, shortened wait times, automatic trust), objectified (transmissible properties like a managed credit score or curated online presence), and institutionalized (widely circulated single scores like credit ratings or China's social credit experiments). The discussion of China's social credit system is notably restrained: they observe that despite alarming headlines, many municipal implementations still lack capacity and authority, and that the American credit score already functions as a de facto social credit system with enormous power over housing, employment, insurance, and dating.

### **Chapter 2: The Data Imperative**

This chapter historicizes the compulsion to collect data. Fourcade and Healy open with Georges Bataille's concept of "the accursed share"—the idea that surplus wealth *must* be expended—and apply it to data: once vast quantities of digital information have been accumulated, it is impossible to imagine leaving them untouched. The chapter then traces the deep roots of quantification as a technology of governance, from seventeenth-century British political arithmetic through nineteenth-century statistics to Herman Hollerith's electromagnetic tabulating machine for the 1890 US Census. They note that the Hollerith card's significance was not merely that it recorded what already existed but that it *expanded what was possible*—"it was an engine, not a camera," borrowing Donald MacKenzie's phrase about financial models. The chapter's key theoretical contribution is the concept of the **data imperative** itself, framed through John Meyer and Brian Rowan's institutional theory. Organizations collect data not only because it is profitable but because a diffuse cultural norm now dictates that rational organizations *must* collect data—it has become "second nature." This imperative operates through three channels: normative (professional exhortation—"we do these things because we must"), mimetic (imitation of industry leaders—"we do these things because that's what leaders do"), and voluntaristic (technological capability—"we do these things because we can"). The chapter closes with a remarkable passage likening the opacity of machine learning to Arthur C. Clarke's third law: "Instead of elevating us to a world of magic, the world of artificial intelligence and machine learning reduces us to it."

### **Chapter 7: Ordinal Citizenship**

This chapter examines what happens when the logic of ranking and scoring collides with the liberal democratic promise of equal citizenship. Fourcade and Healy structure the argument around three "problems." The **problem of equality**: drawing on T. H. Marshall's theory of social citizenship and Michael Young's 1958 coinage of "meritocracy" as a *satirical* term, they show that every historical expansion of rights has also created new axes of stratification. Educational expansion was supposed to dissolve inherited privilege but instead gave rise to credential inflation and the reproduction of class advantage through cultural capital—a point they trace through Bourdieu, James Coleman, and Randall Collins. The **problem of merit**: the shift from group-based to individual-level assessment was supposed to be liberating—antidiscrimination law required decisions "behind a veil of ignorance" that masked categorical differences. Credit scoring, for instance, was promoted as a tool against redlining and racial discrimination. But individual behavioral data inevitably reconstitutes group-level patterns because the behaviors being measured (payment history, credit utilization) are themselves shaped by structural inequality. Their most striking formulation: the unexplained statistical residual between racial groups in credit scores is not a measurement error but "a measure of advantages or disadvantages that have been produced and reproduced over the very longue durée—a kind of Solow residual for social inequalities." The **problem of value**: even if scoring were perfectly fair in some abstract sense, what is being measured is not moral worth but market value—what makes you profitable to an organization. They cite gig workers who experience algorithmic management as gambling ("The house always wins\!") and Hatim Rahman's ethnography describing freelance platforms as an "invisible cage."

### **Conclusion: The Unbearable Rightness of Being Ranked**

The conclusion develops what Fourcade and Healy call "ordinalization's double movement," again drawing on Bourdieu's concept of a "twofold truth." The first truth is the first-person experience: rankings feel useful, informative, even gratifying. The second truth is the third-person reality: ranking systems discipline, stratify, and moralize. They illustrate this with Wendy Espeland and Michael Sauder's study of U.S. News law school rankings, where faculty despised the rankings but found each attempt to resist them only entangled them further. The chapter extends this to Elo-style rating systems (originally designed for chess) now applied to dating apps and gig work, showing how competitive ranking methods designed for closed-world games are being transferred to the open social world where stakes are much higher. The final section turns the analysis on social scientists themselves, acknowledging that the same data hunger that drives corporate surveillance also animates quantitative research—"the little megalomaniac that lives inside anyone who collects data for a living thrills at the thought of what one could do at that scale."

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

* "An ordinal society creates order through automated ranking and matching. The apparent power of its methods justifies the ostensible rightness of its hierarchies and categories." (Introduction)

* "Companies breathed in the exhaust fumes of their own data and found that it smelled of money." (Introduction)

* "Classification situations are positions in a generated system of categories that are consequential for one's life chances. Classification situations are not merely approximations of commonly identified social groups and identities—though, of course, they may overlap substantially in specific cases. Rather, they are independently generated taxonomies that can come to have distinctive and consequential effects on the 'outcomes' people experience in life." (Ch. 3\)

* "Instead of elevating us to a world of magic, the world of artificial intelligence and machine learning reduces us to it." (Ch. 2\)

* "Ordinal systems cannot really 'adjust' or 'correct' for an entire society and its past. These systems begin with a highly structured social world, they measure and score it at the level of individual behavior with the intention of making society go away, and then they face awkward problems when the original social facts reappear in the individual-level results." (Ch. 7\)

* "The whole of social structure and circumstance is stuffed back into the channel labeled 'individual choice.'" (Ch. 7\)

# **Theodore M. Porter, *Trust in Numbers: The Pursuit of Objectivity in Science and Public Life* (1995)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Quantification is not primarily a mirror of nature but a "technology of distance"—a set of strategies for producing knowledge that can travel across social, geographic, and institutional boundaries without depending on personal trust. The drive toward rigid quantitative rules in science, government, and the professions reflects not the internal logic of better methods but the political vulnerability of decision-makers exposed to outside scrutiny. Objectivity, understood as the suppression of personal judgment in favor of mechanical rules, gains its authority precisely where trust is weakest and accountability is greatest.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Porter challenges two dominant narratives about quantification. The first is the triumphalist account in which numbers spread because they yield truer or more powerful knowledge; the second is the critical-theory account (Horkheimer, Adorno, Foucault) in which quantification is simply a tool of domination imposed by powerful elites. Against both, Porter argues that quantification is typically demanded not by the powerful but by the *weak*—bureaucrats, regulators, and professionals who lack the personal authority or elite social standing to make their judgments stick. He reverses the usual arrow of explanation: rather than the success of quantification in natural science inspiring its adoption in public life, he argues that the political and bureaucratic pressures driving quantification in government and administration illuminate its role in the sciences as well. The book is in direct dialogue with sociologists of scientific knowledge (Collins, Latour, Shapin) who emphasized the local, negotiated character of scientific facts, but Porter asks the complementary question they largely ignored: how does knowledge *become* universal? His answer—through disciplined impersonality, not through inherent truth—positions the book as a bridge between science studies and political history.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

### **Chapter 4: "The Political Philosophy of Quantification"**

This is Porter's most theoretically ambitious chapter, synthesizing arguments about the moral and political valences of numbers. He distinguishes between objectivity as truth-to-nature and objectivity as impersonality—the suppression of individual judgment in favor of rules—and argues that the latter is what gives quantification its public authority. He traces this through Karl Pearson's vision of science education as moral training for citizens: Pearson wanted to eliminate the subjective self entirely, replacing personal judgment with method. Porter then develops the crucial insight that quantification tends to break down elite cultures. Harold Lasswell observed in 1923 that America relied more on quantified expertise precisely because of its democratic character, while Britain could trust informal judgment because its administrators formed a cohesive social elite. Porter reads the left critique (Horkheimer and Adorno on the "calculative mentality") and the right critique (Oakeshott on rationalism as the destruction of deep cultural knowledge) as converging on the same point: quantification replaces rich, local, tacit understanding with something shallow but transportable. He borrows Peter Galison's metaphor of the "trading zone"—where experimentalists and theorists exchange predictions and measurements without sharing deeper commitments—to argue that this superficiality is not merely a deficiency but a functional virtue enabling communication across communities.

### **Chapter 6: "French State Engineers and the Ambiguities of Technocracy"**

Porter's account of the Corps des Ponts et Chaussées is the crucial counterexample that makes his argument about American quantification intelligible. French state engineers developed sophisticated forms of economic calculation—what would later be called cost-benefit analysis—decades before their American counterparts. Yet they never reduced these calculations to rigid, impersonal rules. The reason was institutional: the Corps des Ponts was an extraordinarily elite and autonomous body, recruited from the top graduates of the Ecole Polytechnique, operating within a centralized French administrative state. Its engineers could exercise discretion because they were trusted—trusted as members of a recognized social elite whose mathematical training was itself a credential of disinterestedness. The Conseil général, the Corps's top decision-making body, reached its decisions in closed session, and its recommendations were often approved automatically by higher authorities. Quantification served as one input among many in these deliberations, not as a mechanical decision procedure. Porter traces the internal debates between Navier (champion of abstract mathematical analysis) and Brisson (advocate of descriptive geometry and political economy) to show that even within the Corps, the meaning of mathematical expertise was contested. The key point is that the Corps's social prestige and bureaucratic autonomy *insulated* it from the kind of external pressure that would later force American engineers to adopt rigid quantitative standards.

### **Chapter 7: "U.S. Army Engineers and the Rise of Cost-Benefit Analysis"**

This is the book's longest and most empirically dense chapter, and the heart of Porter's argument. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, modeled on the French Corps des Ponts, occupied an entirely different political environment. American democratic culture was defined by systematic distrust of bureaucratic elites. The Corps's economic analyses of water projects evolved from rough-and-ready estimates in the 1920s—where a district engineer might simply list benefits until they exceeded costs—into increasingly rigid and standardized procedures by the 1950s. Porter shows this transformation was driven not by advances in economic theory but by bureaucratic warfare. After 1936, when the Flood Control Act required that benefits exceed costs for any federally funded project, the Corps's numbers were initially accepted on the strength of its institutional reputation alone. Congressional committees were "dazzlingly uninquisitive"—when told a benefit-cost ratio was 1.035, nobody questioned the margin of error. But when the Corps faced challenges from powerful opponents—utility companies, railroads, the Bureau of Reclamation, the Department of Agriculture—its informal calculations became objects of bitter controversy. The agencies tried to settle feuds by harmonizing their methods; when negotiation failed, they were "compelled to try to ground their makeshift techniques in economic rationality." Cost-benefit analysis was thus transformed from a collection of local bureaucratic practices into a set of supposedly universal economic principles—not because anyone proved it was scientifically sound, but because the American political system demanded impersonal rules where personal judgment could not be trusted.

### **Chapter 9: "Is Science Made by Communities?"**

The final chapter extends the bureaucratic argument to the natural sciences. Porter engages directly with Kuhn's concept of scientific community and with the microsociological tradition (Collins, Latour) that treats scientific knowledge as locally negotiated. He accepts the insight that knowledge is made locally, in laboratories and small groups, but argues that the interesting question is how it becomes universal. His answer: through the same mechanisms of standardization and impersonality that operate in bureaucracies. Scientific disciplines that are politically exposed—that must defend their findings to outsiders, secure government funding, or resolve disputes across institutional boundaries—adopt more rigid quantitative standards. Disciplines that are socially cohesive and insulated from outside pressure can afford to rely on personal judgment, tacit knowledge, and informal negotiation. The chapter draws an analogy between the French Corps des Ponts (elite, autonomous, judgment-based) and tight-knit scientific communities, and between the American Corps of Engineers (exposed, distrusted, rule-bound) and disciplines facing public scrutiny. Porter concludes that the pursuit of mechanical objectivity "cannot suffice to settle public issues in conditions of pervasive distrust," suggesting that the expansion of quantitative methods is both a symptom and an attempted cure for a deeper political problem.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"My summary answer to this crucial question is that quantification is a technology of distance." (p. ix)

"Quantification is a way of making decisions without seeming to decide. Objectivity lends authority to officials who have very little of their own." (p. 8\)

"The transition from expert judgment to explicit decision criteria did not grow out of the attempts of powerful insiders to make better decisions, but rather emerged as a strategy of impersonality in response to their exposure to pressures from outside." (p. xi)

"Objectivity means the rule of law, not of men. It implies the subordination of personal interests and prejudices to public standards." (p. 74\)

"Rigorous quantification is demanded in these contexts because subjective discretion has become suspect. Mechanical objectivity serves as an alternative to personal trust." (p. 90\)

"The United States relies on rules to control the exercise of official judgment to a greater extent than any other industrialized democracy." (p. 194, quoting James Q. Wilson)

# Week 8 — Cybernetics

# **Geof Bowker, "How to Be Universal: Some Cybernetic Strategies, 1943–70" (1993)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Cybernetics succeeded as a "universal discipline" not because it discovered some deep truth about the unity of nature, but because its practitioners deployed a specific set of rhetorical and institutional strategies: a new reading of human history that placed cybernetics at the apex of a new age, a universal language that facilitated "legitimacy exchange" across disciplines, and a reorganization of the economy of the sciences backed by Cold War military funding. Bowker argues that cybernetics operated through what he calls "distributed passage points"—a disciplinary strategy complementary to Latour's "obligatory passage point," in which the new science made itself inescapable not by funneling all problems through a single laboratory, but by claiming to be already present everywhere one looked.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Bowker is writing against a tendency in science studies to treat laboratory science and its strategies as the canonical form of scientific practice. The obligatory passage point—Latour's concept, developed through the Pasteur case—assumes that scientists gain power by creating a barricaded "inside" (the laboratory) whose contents only they can translate. Bowker argues this model cannot account for disciplines like cybernetics, which gained power by doing the opposite: dissolving the boundary between inside and outside, making the world itself into the laboratory. The article thus extends the actor-network theory toolkit by proposing a complementary concept (the distributed passage point) and calls attention to "universal disciplines" as an underexamined form of scientific practice distinct from circumscribed, tool-and-data-defined fields.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

**"An Anatomy of the Claim to Universality"**

Bowker traces cybernetics' origins to the 1943 paper by Wiener, Bigelow, and Rosenblueth on "Behavior, Purpose, and Teleology," which grew directly out of wartime gunnery control work commissioned by Vannevar Bush and Warren Weaver. The paper's key move was taxonomic: it classified behaviors (predictive/non-predictive, feedback/no feedback, purposeful/non-purposeful) and showed that both animals and machines appeared on both sides of every divide. But it also made a subtler historical argument: organisms achieved their effects through massive iteration in space (6.5 million cones in an eye), while machines achieved theirs through iteration in time (the speed with which a TV refreshes its single scanning point). Only now, with computing technology enabling extremely fast temporal operations, had machines and organisms become genuinely equivalent in informational terms. Cybernetics was therefore simultaneously eternal (always potentially true) and historically specific (only now realizable). Bowker shows cyberneticians exploiting this double register constantly—shifting between grand Hegelian narratives (Arbib listing Copernicus, Darwin, Freud, and now cybernetics as successive blows to the human self-image) and concrete conjunctural claims about the current state of military technology. Wiener's *God and Golem, inc.* is the clearest example: it directly paralleled the problems of cybernetics (can machines learn? can they reproduce?) with problems of Christian theology (the Book of Job, Paradise Lost), casting cyberneticians as stewards of godlike powers in an age of nuclear annihilation. The Soviet reaction—calling cybernetics "a reactionary pseudo-science" aimed at turning workers into extensions of machines—is quoted from the 1954 *Short Philosophical Dictionary* and shows how the same claim to universality could be read as a political threat.

**"A Universal Language for a New Age"**

The section traces how cybernetics' shared vocabulary functioned strategically even when it lacked clear content. Bowker identifies several mechanisms. First, "legitimacy exchange" (drawing on Susan Leigh Star's concept of "triangulation"): an isolated researcher making an outlandish claim in one field could point to cybernetic parallels in another field for support, which in turn referenced the first field. Uttley's "conditional probability machine" is the example—he used mathematics to support his physiology and physiology to support his mathematics, with cybernetic terminology doing the work of glossing the gap. Second, "discontinuous transmission": conceptual tools could be pulled out of one context (philosophy of mind) and plugged into another (automata theory) with the cybernetic language smoothing over the disjunction. Third, what Bowker calls the "Parsons effect" (after Talcott Parsons): because the technical language required heavy intellectual investment to learn, those who made the effort were psychologically inclined to use it regardless of payoff. Bowker frames the key insight sharply: "a cybernetics possibly without content could operate (via legitimacy exchange) together with a cybernetics possibly with content (via discontinuous transmission of ideas and the transgression of traditional ontological boundaries) to create a new universalism tied to a particular conjuncture in military and industrial development."

**"Arguing a New Economy of the Sciences"**

Here Bowker shows cybernetics reorganizing the traditional Comtean hierarchy of the sciences (biology reduces to chemistry, thence to physics, thence to mathematics). The key example is Nils Bohr's cybernetics-influenced argument that biological ideas could be imported *into* physics—a heretical reversal. Gordon Pask's electrochemical experiments are the most vivid case study: Pask grew conductive metal threads (dendrites) in an acid solution, using competition for energy as an evolutionary selection principle. The dendrites formed a kind of self-wiring computer—a chemical system that could develop memory and even learn to detect sound. This meant chemists and biologists could do what had been the exclusive province of electrical engineers. Pask even proposed an "animal computer" using pretrained pigeons as pattern-recognizing automata (citing Skinner) and unicellular organisms as basic computing elements. The section also tracks how military funding concretely backed this reorganization: the Air Force's Office of Scientific Research funded bionics research that applied biological principles to engineering, and the Office of Naval Research sponsored cybernetic systems conferences. Cybernetics could operate either as the primary discipline directing others, or as an infrastructural support discipline (like mathematics or statistics) providing analytic tools indispensable to all.

**"Cybernetic Strategies—Obligatory and Distributed Passage Points"**

The concluding section is where Bowker makes his main theoretical contribution. Latour's obligatory passage point depends on incommensurability: the state cannot speak the language of microbes, so it must go through Pasteur's lab. This requires a strict inside/outside boundary—Boyle's creation of the modern laboratory, which excluded all trace of social interest. Cybernetics employed a fundamentally different strategy. Instead of barricading the laboratory off from the world, cyberneticians made the world into a laboratory. They directly appropriated political and religious discourse, claimed their science produced the most faithful possible description of society, and argued that cybernetic truths could be found everywhere—in the frog's eye, in electrochemical dendrites, in missile guidance, in alcoholism, in insect vision, in nuclear war. This is the "distributed passage point": it is inescapable not because you need a translator to get where you want to go, but because wherever you go, you will find cybernetics already there.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"A uniform behavioristic analysis is applicable to both machines and living organisms, regardless of the complexity of the behavior." (p. 110, quoting Wiener, Bigelow & Rosenblueth 1943\)

"A cybernetics possibly without content could operate (via legitimacy exchange) together with a cybernetics possibly with content (via discontinuous transmission of ideas and the transgression of traditional ontological boundaries) to create a new universalism tied to a particular conjuncture in military and industrial development." (p. 117\)

"The obligatory passage point is inescapable because we need to draw on the services of a translator and his or her black-boxed set of tools in order to get where we want to go. The distributed passage point is inescapable because wherever we do go (the farthest reaches of the human mind, the depths of the jungle) we will find the new universal science." (p. 123\)

"Where traditional sciences operated behind the walls of the laboratory, cybernetics was everywhere you went. Where traditional sciences repudiated all possible mention of society, cybernetics proclaimed that it could produce the best possible description thereof, and that its universal truth was immediately tied to this historical conjuncture." (p. 123\)

"Instead of the laboratory being barricaded off from the world, the world will become a laboratory." (p. 123\)

# **Peter Galison, "The Ontology of the Enemy: Norbert Wiener and the Cybernetic Vision" (1994)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Galison argues that cybernetics originated not as an abstract intellectual program but as a specific wartime technology—Norbert Wiener's antiaircraft (AA) predictor—designed to model an enemy pilot as a servomechanism whose future behavior could be statistically forecast. From this narrow military instrument, Wiener progressively generalized outward: the enemy pilot became a model for the Allied gunner, then for the human nervous system, then for human intentionality as such, and finally for the universe itself as a system of input-output "black-box monads." The cultural meanings of cybernetics—its behaviorist reduction of mind to feedback, its agonistic framing of all relations as opposition between calculating agents—are inseparable from this genealogy in wartime combat.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Galison challenges accounts that treat cybernetics as a purely intellectual synthesis of preexisting ideas about feedback, communication, and control. While acknowledging earlier work by Maxwell, Kolmogorov, and others, he insists that what made cybernetics culturally powerful was its specific origin in mechanized aerial warfare, where human pilots really did behave like autocorrelated servomechanisms and where the stakes of reading another's intentions were lethal. This amounts to a strong claim about the relationship between cultural meaning and technological genealogy: "the cultural meaning of concepts or practices is indissolubly tied to their genealogy" (p. 264). The article also intervenes in postmodernist appropriations of cybernetics, arguing that Lyotard's game-theoretical, agonistic social theory is not a departure from cybernetics but a continuation of the "Manichean sciences," and that Haraway's attempt to detach the cyborg from its military origins is more difficult than she acknowledges. Galison's interlocutors include Steve Heims (whose dual biography of Wiener and von Neumann provides the richest prior account), Otto Mayr (whose work on early modern feedback devices and liberal political order Galison extends and critiques), and Edward Said (whose concept of the racialized Other Galison distinguishes from the cybernetic Other).

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

**Section 1: "The Enemy" — Three Wartime Ontologies of the Other**

Galison opens by distinguishing three Allied conceptions of the enemy during World War II. The first is the racialized enemy, exemplified by General Blamey's depiction of Japanese soldiers as "subhuman beasts" and "vermin"—the enemy as less than human. The second is the anonymous enemy of strategic bombing, where altitude and distance stripped the inhabitants of targeted cities of their individuality; British air censors noted an "absence of vindictiveness" in bomber crews' letters, a function of moral distance rather than dehumanization. The third—Galison's real subject—is the "mechanized Enemy Other" generated in wartime laboratories. This enemy was neither subhuman nor invisible but hyper-rational: a "cold-blooded, machinelike opponent" whose behavior could be modeled statistically. Galison groups the three war sciences that engaged this calculating enemy—operations research, game theory, and cybernetics—under the heading "Manichean sciences," borrowing Wiener's own distinction between the active, cunning "Manichean devil" and the passive, entropic "Augustinian devil."

**Section 2: "The AA Predictor" — Building the Machine**

Wiener wrote to Vannevar Bush in September 1940 offering his services, and by 1941 his attention had focused on predicting the future position of enemy bombers so antiaircraft shells could be directed to intercept them. With engineer Julian Bigelow, Wiener constructed a laboratory simulation: a colored light spot bouncing across a wall mimicked a bombing target, while an operator with a deliberately sluggish control stick tried to track it, reproducing the disassociation between kinesthetic sense and visual information that real pilots faced. The key statistical finding was that individual operators were highly self-consistent (autocorrelated) even though different operators' patterns were uncorrelated with each other—meaning a refined predictor could learn a specific pilot's behavioral signature and use it to kill him. On July 1, 1942, NDRC section chairman George Stibitz witnessed a demonstration and called the device's one-second predictions "positively uncanny"; Warren Weaver joked about sawing through the table legs to find hidden wires. Yet the predictor ultimately failed against real flight data (tracks 303 and 304 from Camp Davis), barely matching the performance of simpler geometrical methods designed by Hendrik Bode. Wiener attributed the failure to the enemy's capacity to change flight patterns during the twenty seconds of projectile flight, and recommended shelving the project until after the war.

**Section 3: "From AA Predictor to Human Nature" — The Generalization**

Even before the predictor proved inadequate as a weapon, Wiener began extracting broader implications. In a 1942 letter to J. B. S. Haldane, he described (without revealing military content) a "black-box" approach to the nervous system that surpassed anything behaviorists had achieved. In their 1943 article "Behavior, Purpose and Teleology," Wiener, Bigelow, and cardiologist Arturo Rosenblueth proposed a classification that rehabilitated the concept of "purpose" by placing it under a "uniform behavioristic analysis" applicable equally to living organisms and machines. Darwin had blurred the human-animal boundary; Wiener aimed to efface the human-machine boundary. The gremlin superstition among wartime aviators—attributing personality to the self-stabilizing feedback of airfoil design—exemplified how feedback systems naturally invited the ascription of intention. Harvard psychologist Edwin Boring embraced the program enthusiastically, proposing to translate all psychological functions into stimulus-response pairs that Wiener's circuits could replicate: "That's about what a person is, really." Philosopher Richard Taylor objected that the cybernetic definition of "purpose" was vacuous—a falling brick, a broken clock, a weighted roulette wheel could all qualify—but Wiener was unmoved: "as objects of scientific enquiry, humans do not differ from machines." Galison reads this globalized claim as a decontextualization of what had made perfect sense in the specific man-machine combat system of antiaircraft warfare.

**Sections 4–5: "Philosophy of Nature" and "Postmodern Continuities"**

Wiener periodized all of human history through its dominant machines: the clockwork age of mercantilism, the steam age of manufacturing, and the present cybernetic age of information and control, whose emblematic automaton "points guns to the place at which a radar beam picks up an airplane." After Hiroshima, his moral anguish intensified—he considered leaving science entirely, writing to MIT's president that he saw "no other course which accords with my conscience"—but the cultural meaning of cybernetics as simultaneously salvific and apocalyptic was already set. Through the Macy Conferences (1946–53), cybernetics reshaped the social sciences: Bateson's "schismogenesis" became "regenerative feedback," and anthropologists, sociologists, and psychologists adopted the vocabulary of systems, information, and homeostasis. Wiener raised the framework to metaphysics through Leibniz, recasting monads as communicating nodes in a "quasi-solipsistic" universe of input-output signals. Galison then turns to postmodernism: Lyotard's *Postmodern Condition* explicitly cites Wiener yet claims to depart from cybernetics, but Galison shows that Lyotard's agonistic theory of "moves and countermoves" is precisely what Wiener, von Neumann, and the operations analysts had theorized. Haraway's "Cyborg Manifesto" acknowledges the military roots of cybernetics but argues that "illegitimate offspring are often exceedingly unfaithful to their origins." Galison is skeptical: the associations of cybernetics with weapons, opposition, and black-box conceptions of human nature "do not so simply melt away."

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"It is necessary to assimilate the different parts of the system to a single basis, either human or mechanical. Since our understanding of the mechanical elements of gun pointing appeared to us to be far ahead of our psychological understanding, we chose to try to find a mechanical analogue of the gun pointer and the airplane pilot." (p. 240, quoting Wiener's *I Am a Mathematician*)

"As objects of scientific enquiry, humans do not differ from machines." (p. 251, quoting Rosenblueth and Wiener, 1950\)

"The cultural meaning of concepts or practices, I would argue, is indissolubly tied to their genealogy." (p. 264\)

"Cultural meaning is neither aleatory nor eternal. We are not free by fiat alone to dismiss the chain of associations that was forged over decades in the laboratory, on the battlefield, in the social sciences, and in the philosophy of cybernetics." (p. 265\)

"Perhaps disorganization, noise, and uncontrollability are not the greatest disasters to befall us. Perhaps our calamities are built largely from our efforts at superorganization, silence, and control." (p. 266\)

"Would cybernetics, information theory, and 'systems thinking' have proved such a central and enduring metaphor without combat? Would the pervasive postwar ontology of the enemy have had such a runaway success without the seduction of victorious military power? I doubt it." (p. 263\)

---

*Note: Page numbers correspond to the original Critical Inquiry pagination (vol. 21, no. 1, Autumn 1994, pp. 228–266). Some quotes drawn from OCR text should be verified against a clean copy.*

# **Donna Haraway, "A Manifesto for Cyborgs: Science, Technology, and Socialist Feminism in the 1980s" (1985)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Haraway proposes the figure of the cyborg—a hybrid of machine and organism—as a political myth for socialist feminism that rejects the search for original unity, natural identity, or organic wholeness. In a world where the boundaries between human and animal, organism and machine, and physical and non-physical have collapsed, the cyborg offers a model for political coalition built on affinity and partiality rather than on identity, essentialism, or claims to innocence. The essay simultaneously maps the "informatics of domination"—the new world order of communications technologies and biotechnologies—and argues that feminists must engage with, not retreat from, this technoscientific terrain.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

The Manifesto intervenes against two tendencies within 1980s feminism: the Marxist/socialist feminist reliance on "labor" as the ontological ground for women's unity, and radical feminism's (particularly Catharine MacKinnon's) totalization of women's experience through the lens of sexual objectification. Both, Haraway argues, reproduce the Western epistemological demand for a single, unified revolutionary subject, and both fail to account for differences of race, class, and sexuality among women. Against the broader progressive tradition running from Marcuse's *One-Dimensional Man* to Merchant's *Death of Nature*, which insisted on recovering an organic, pre-technological wholeness as the basis for resistance, Haraway argues that no return to nature is available or desirable. She draws on Chela Sandoval's concept of "oppositional consciousness" and Fredric Jameson's analysis of postmodernism as a cultural dominant to propose that feminist politics must work from within the blurred boundaries of high-tech culture, not against them.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

**Three Boundary Breakdowns (Opening Sections)**

The essay's theoretical architecture rests on three boundary collapses that Haraway identifies in late-twentieth-century scientific culture. First, the human/animal boundary has been "thoroughly breached" by evolutionary biology, animal behavior research, and the erosion of claims about human uniqueness through language, tool use, and social behavior. Second, the organism/machine boundary has dissolved: pre-cybernetic machines could be "haunted" by the ghost-in-the-machine dualism, but late-twentieth-century machines are "disturbingly lively, and we ourselves frighteningly inert." Modern machines are microelectronic, invisible, portable—"made of sunshine." Third, the physical/non-physical boundary has blurred through miniaturization: the silicon chip operates at molecular scales, and power now resides in signals and codes rather than in visible, material structures. These three breakdowns collectively render useless the old dualisms (mind/body, nature/culture, self/other) that had structured both Western domination and feminist resistance to it. Crucially, Haraway insists that each breakdown opens space for both domination and liberation—"single vision produces worse illusions than double vision or many-headed monsters."

**Fractured Identities**

This section mounts Haraway's most sustained critique of existing feminist theory. She works through Marxist/socialist feminism's attempt to ground women's unity in the category of labor (expanding Marx's concept by analogy to include reproduction, then sex, then race as additive categories) and MacKinnon's radical feminism, which grounds unity in sexual objectification. MacKinnon's theory, Haraway argues, is "totalizing in the extreme"—it produces "feminists' consciousness of the non-existence of women, except as products of men's desire," an even more authoritarian doctrine than the Marxist humanism it aimed to replace. Neither framework has "structural room for race." Haraway diagrams this failure schematically: socialist feminism proceeds from class/labor to reproduction to sex to race by analogy and addition; radical feminism proceeds from gender/sexual appropriation to labor to reproduction to race by the same logic. In both cases, race arrives last, as supplement. Against these totalizing moves, Haraway draws on Sandoval's theorization of "women of color" as a political identity forged through "conscious appropriation of negation"—not grounded in any natural or essential criterion, but in conscious coalition. Katie King's work on the taxonomizing impulse within feminism (how each "moment" in the movement rewrites feminist history to make its own position the telos) reinforces the point that the search for unity-through-identification always produces exclusions.

**The Informatics of Domination**

This is the essay's most concrete analytical section, where Haraway maps the transition from what she calls "the comfortable old hierarchical dominations" to "the scary new networks" of the informatics of domination. She presents this as a long chart of paired transitions: Representation → Simulation; Organism → Biotic component; Depth/integrity → Surface/boundary; Physiology → Communications engineering; Eugenics → Population control; Hygiene → Stress management; Organic sex role specialization → Optimal genetic strategies; Family/Market/Factory → Women in the Integrated Circuit; Nature/Culture → Fields of difference; Freud → Lacan; Sex → Genetic engineering; Mind → Artificial Intelligence; White Capitalist Patriarchy → Informatics of Domination. The key insight is that everything on the right-hand side of the chart "cannot be coded as 'natural,'" which retroactively destabilizes the naturalism of the left-hand column. Objects of knowledge are now best understood as "problems in communications engineering" or "theories of the text." Control operates through boundary conditions, interface management, rates of flow, and probabilistic modeling—not through the integrity of natural objects. The common move uniting communications sciences and modern biology is "the translation of the world into a problem of coding." In communications theory, the key operation is determining rates, directions, and probabilities of information flow; in molecular genetics, the organism has been translated into problems of genetic coding and read-out. "Biology is here a kind of cryptography. Research is necessarily a kind of intelligence activity." The stressed immune system—failing to distinguish self from other—becomes Haraway's emblematic figure for this new order, replacing the disciplined body of Foucault's analysis. "The cyborg is not subject to Foucault's biopolitics; the cyborg simulates politics, a much more potent field of operations."

**The Homework Economy / Women in the Integrated Circuit**

Drawing on Richard Gordon's concept, Haraway describes a restructuring of global labor in which work is "feminized"—meaning made extremely vulnerable, subject to disassembly and reassembly, reduced from worker to server—whether performed by men or women. She traces how new communications technologies enable capital to integrate and control dispersed labor forces, how the collapse of the welfare state intensifies demands on women, and how the "feminization of poverty" is structurally produced. The section is grounded in specific material conditions: women in Silicon Valley structured around electronics-dependent employment, serial monogamy, distance from extended kin; Korean women recruited from high schools into both the sex industry and electronics assembly; Black women in the U.S. moving from domestic service into clerical work without escaping poverty. Green Revolution technologies alter gender divisions of agricultural labor worldwide. Video games produce "gendered imaginations" oriented toward individual competition and extraterrestrial warfare. The section insists that "what is lost, perhaps especially from women's points of view, is often virulent forms of oppression, nostalgically naturalized in the face of current violation"—a refusal of any simple narrative of technological decline.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"A cyborg is a cybernetic organism, a hybrid of machine and organism, a creature of social reality as well as a creature of fiction." (p. 7\)

"By the late twentieth century, our time, a mythic time, we are all chimeras, theorized and fabricated hybrids of machine and organism; in short, we are cyborgs. The cyborg is our ontology; it gives us our politics." (p. 8\)

"The main trouble with cyborgs, of course, is that they are the illegitimate offspring of militarism and patriarchal capitalism, not to mention state socialism. But illegitimate offspring are often exceedingly unfaithful to their origins. Their fathers, after all, are inessential." (p. 10\)

"The boundary is permeable between tool and myth, instrument and concept, historical systems of social relations and historical anatomies of possible bodies, including objects of knowledge. Indeed, myth and tool mutually constitute each other." (p. 23\)

"Cyborg imagery can suggest a way out of the maze of dualisms in which we have explained our bodies and our tools to ourselves. This is a dream not of a common language, but of a powerful infidel heteroglossia." (p. 39\)

"Though both are bound in the spiral dance, I would rather be a cyborg than a goddess." (p. 39\)

---

**Exam Connections:**

This essay is a foundational text for multiple threads across your lists. On the HoT side, it sits in direct conversation with Winner's "Do Artifacts Have Politics?" (Haraway's footnote 4 wryly extends Winner's point: "Both chimpanzees and artifacts have politics, so why shouldn't we?"), with Cowan's *More Work for Mother* (cited in the notes as a starting point for feminist approaches to technology), and with the entire SCOT tradition—though Haraway pushes far beyond social construction toward a dissolution of the categories that constructivism still takes for granted. The "informatics of domination" framework anticipates Edwards's *The Closed World* and Kline's *The Cybernetics Moment* in mapping how Cold War information sciences restructured political and epistemological terrain, though Haraway foregrounds gender, race, and labor in ways those later histories largely don't. The essay's insistence that modern biology operates as "a kind of cryptography" connects to Rabinow's *Making PCR* and to the entire biology-as-technology cluster (Franklin, Creager, Rheinberger). On the HoS side, the critique of Foucault's biopolitics as a "flaccid premonition of cyborg politics" is a major historiographic claim—it argues that the disciplinary apparatus described in *Discipline and Punish* and *History of Sexuality* names "a form of power at its moment of implosion," already being replaced by networking, stress management, and communications redesign. This puts Haraway in productive tension with Foucault's *Order of Things* on your reading list. The essay's use of Sandoval and its attention to "women of color" as a political category constructed through coalition (not identity) anticipates later STS work on race and technology (Benjamin's *Race After Technology*, Nakamura) and connects to the urban history list's concerns with race and metropolitan development. For exam purposes, the "homework economy" section provides portable case-study material on the gendered geography of Silicon Valley that pairs directly with O'Mara's *Cities of Knowledge* and Harris's *Palo Alto*.

# **How Reason Almost Lost Its Mind: The Strange Career of Cold War Rationality**

## **Paul Erickson, Judy L. Klein, Lorraine Daston, Rebecca Lemov, Thomas Sturm, and Michael D. Gordin (2013)**

---

### **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

During the Cold War, a distinctive form of rationality—formal, algorithmic, context-independent, and potentially mechanizable—was developed across the American human sciences to address the unprecedented dangers of nuclear annihilation. This "Cold War rationality" expanded the domain of narrow instrumental calculation at the expense of older, richer traditions of reason that valued judgment, prudence, and deliberation. The book argues that this was not a unified program but a *field of contestation*: the formal ideal provoked a decades-long interdisciplinary debate in which critics tried to stretch, enrich, or replace it—a debate that fragmented into disciplinary specialties once the geopolitical pressures of the Cold War eased.

### **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

The book pushes back against two common approaches to Cold War intellectual history. The first treats game theory, operations research, and rational choice as a single triumphant paradigm imposed by military funding and RAND-style think tanks. The second dismisses these projects as naïve or sinister. Erickson et al. instead treat Cold War rationality as a *debate*—vigorous, cross-disciplinary, and internally contested from the start. Their key move is to distinguish *reason* (the broad philosophical faculty emphasizing judgment, deliberation, and wisdom) from *rationality* (formal, rule-bound, algorithmic optimization), and to show that the Cold War's novelty lay in the aggressive expansion of the latter into domains previously reserved for the former. They are in conversation with Hunter Heyck's work on the "high modern social sciences," Philip Mirowski's work on RAND and Cold War economics, and S.M. Amadae's account of rational choice theory, but they resist the "rise and fall" narrative, arguing instead for a story of "condensation under intense pressure"—elements that existed separately before and after the Cold War briefly fusing under extraordinary circumstances.

### **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

**Chapter 1: Enlightenment Reason, Cold War Rationality, and the Rule of Rules.** This chapter establishes the book's core conceptual distinction. It opens with RAND mathematician Merrill Flood testing game theory on his three teenage children by having them bid on a babysitting job—and finding their behavior "grossly irrational" by the payoff matrix. Flood drew a straight line from his kids' inability to split the difference to "mature nations at war." The chapter traces how, in the two decades after WWII, "human reason was reconceptualized as rationality"—a transformation in which philosophers, economists, strategists, and computer scientists converged on the idea that rational behavior could be captured in finite, well-defined, algorithmic rules applied without recourse to judgment. The authors track this from Leibniz's dream of a calculus of reason, through Babbage's analytical engine and nineteenth-century actuarial science, to the axiomatizations of von Neumann and Morgenstern. The crucial point is that algorithms—once the humble province of arithmetic—were elevated to a model for *all* rational thought. The chapter uses the contrast between Enlightenment ideals of *reasonable* judgment (which required experience, context-sensitivity, and wisdom) and Cold War ideals of *rational* rule-following (which aspired to eliminate precisely those things) to frame the entire book.

**Chapter 2: The Bounded Rationality of Cold War Operations Research.** This chapter is built around the 1948–49 Berlin Airlift. On the operational level, General William Tunner managed Operation Vittles as an assembly line in the sky: planes landing at Tempelhof every three minutes, pilots executing identical routines day after day—Lieutenant Fred McAfee's "very regular life" of rpm settings and compass headings repeated seventy times. Meanwhile, at the Pentagon, the Project for the Scientific Computation of Optimum Programs (Project SCOOP) tried to mathematize the airlift's logistics using linear programming algorithms that would find optimal resource allocations—essentially trying to achieve through centralized computation what a decentralized market achieves through prices. The punchline: their computing capacity was so limited that the only optimization they could actually run was for the cheapest diet meeting the nutritional needs of a seventy-kilogram urban economist. This gap between mathematical ambition and computational reality proved generative. Herbert Simon, working on production planning funded by the Office of Naval Research at Carnegie Tech, turned the practical limitation of scarce computing into a theory: "bounded rationality" and "satisficing" rather than maximizing. The chapter thus shows how Cold War rationality's own practical failures produced some of its most important theoretical innovations.

**Chapter 4: "The Situation" in the Cold War Behavioral Sciences.** The most arresting chapter, centered on the Micronesian islands as simultaneous nuclear and social-scientific test sites. Between 1946 and 1962, sixty-six atomic bombs were detonated in Micronesian territory—absorbing over 80 percent of the total yield of all US nuclear tests in that period. At the same time, led by Yale's George Peter Murdock, forty-one social scientists from twenty-plus institutions fanned out across the same atolls to administer Rorschach tests, thematic apperception tests, and moral-ideological batteries to nearly 100 percent of some island populations. The chapter's devastating observation: "neither enterprise to our knowledge ever referred to the other." Whole islands vaporized while personality inventories were being filed. The chapter uses Micronesia to introduce the concept of "the situation"—a flexible, portable unit of controlled behavioral observation that became central to postwar social science. This ranged from Micronesian atolls to Harvard's "strange situation" rooms rigged with one-way mirrors to Stanley Milgram's obedience experiments. The "situation" was both a method and a worldview: complex human behavior could be stripped down, contained, and made comparable through standardized experimental designs. The chapter shows how Cold War rationality's obsession with formal method over "gritty substance" produced a peculiar abstraction in which real places became partially unreal—laboratories for testing the limits of testing itself.

**Chapter 5: World in a Matrix.** The chapter opens with Nixon's "Madman Theory"—telling Haldeman he wanted the North Vietnamese to believe he might do anything, including going nuclear, while secretly sending nuclear-armed B-52s toward the Soviet Union in Operation Giant Lance (1969). This apparent lunacy, the authors argue, was rooted in game theory's logic of calculated irrationality. The chapter traces the prisoner's dilemma from its invention by Flood and Melvin Dresher at RAND in January 1950, through Albert Tucker's famous narrative framing for psychologists at Stanford that spring, to its extraordinary career as the master metaphor for Cold War conflict. The key analytical point is *why game theory persisted* even as its limitations became obvious. The answer lies not in its ability to solve strategic problems but in the game matrix's extraordinary flexibility as a tool for coding behavior across wildly different contexts: Nixon's Vietnam War, laboratory subjects pressing buttons, economic transactions, and even dung flies competing for mates all became prisoner's dilemmas. The payoff matrix allowed Cold War rationality to "vault between contexts and across disciplinary lines and spatial and temporal scales," making it the shared lingua franca of an otherwise fragmented set of human sciences.

### **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"In that implied gap between reason and rationality lay the novelty of Cold War rationality." (p. 2\)

"The aim of this book is to make the label 'Cold War rationality' stick." (p. 2\)

"This rationality should be formal, and therefore largely independent of personality or context. It frequently took the form of algorithms... And finally, at least ideally, advocates hoped that the rules could be applied mechanically: computers might reason better than human minds." (p. 3–4)

"Our story has not been an epic of rise and fall, life and death, but rather one of condensation under intense pressure: people, ideas, disciplines, methods, and institutions that dwelled apart both before and after the Cold War briefly came together." (p. 183\)

"This was rationality stripped for action in an age of high drama: for all anyone knew, the world might end tomorrow with a very big bang." (p. 188\)

"Neither enterprise to our knowledge ever referred to the other. A curious abstraction in attitude meant that the socio-psychological experts on the one hand and the nuclear on the other operated as if their 'experimental zones' did not share the same geography." (p. 110\)

# **Eden Medina, *Cybernetic Revolutionaries: Technology and Politics in Allende's Chile* (2011)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Medina argues that Project Cybersyn—Chile's attempt to build a real-time computer system for managing the nationalized economy under Salvador Allende (1971–1973)—reveals how technology and politics are mutually constitutive: political innovation spurred technological innovation, and the design of the system both reflected and exposed the contradictions within Chilean democratic socialism. The book demonstrates that embedding political values in technological systems through "sociotechnical engineering" is extraordinarily difficult, because changing the social and organizational relationships around a technology can cause it to support configurations of power entirely different from those its designers intended.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Medina engages the social construction of technology (SCOT) tradition but pushes beyond it: rather than uncovering hidden politics inside a black box, she takes the absence of a technology/politics dichotomy as her starting point, since politics were an explicit, visible feature of Chilean life during the Allende period. She builds on the work of Gabrielle Hecht (*The Radiance of France*), Paul Edwards (*The Closed World*), and Ken Alder on how nationalism and ideology shape technological development, but extends this approach outside the U.S. and Europe into Latin America. The book also contributes to the growing "disunity of cybernetics" thesis proposed by Ronald Kline, showing that Chilean cybernetics—government-centered, applied to economic management on a national scale—differed fundamentally from the academic U.S., Soviet, and British variants. Finally, Medina challenges unidirectional models of technology transfer (north-to-south), arguing that ideas and artifacts moved in multiple directions and that Chile's limited resources actually forced genuinely innovative technical solutions that wealthier nations had not attempted.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

### **Chapter 3: Designing a Network**

This chapter details how Beer and the Chilean team designed Project Cyberstride (the predecessor to Cybersyn) during Beer's initial eight-day visit in November 1971, under severe material constraints. The Chilean government could offer Beer processing time on only one mainframe computer—an IBM 360/50—so Beer proposed an unorthodox network architecture: a web of telex machines connected to that single mainframe. The team caught a lucky break when army major Gustavo Soto discovered that ENTEL, the national telecom enterprise, had four hundred unused telex machines purchased by the previous Frei government and left in storage—"our Aladdin's Cave," as engineer Raúl Espejo called it. The design explicitly rejected the Soviet approach to computerized economic planning, which collected massive quantities of data for centralized control. Instead, Beer proposed a "roll-up" method: policies traveled down from the government to factories, while factory needs traveled upward, with management sitting in the middle as a homeostatic regulator. When the system's statistical software detected a production anomaly, it would alert both CORFO (the state development agency) and the factory manager simultaneously, giving the manager a limited window to resolve the problem independently before higher-level intervention kicked in. The chapter's most vivid scene is Beer's meeting with Allende at La Moneda, where the president—trained as a pathologist—immediately grasped the biological metaphor of the Viable System Model, then insisted the system be "decentralizing, worker-participative, and anti-bureaucratic." When Beer reached the top tier of his model, reserved for the president, Allende leaned back and said: "At last, el pueblo." Beer returned triumphant to the project team waiting at the Hotel Carrera bar and announced, "We're on\!"

### **Chapter 4: Constructing the Liberty Machine**

This chapter covers April–September 1972, when the team built prototypes of all four Cybersyn components: the telex network (Cybernet), the statistical software (Cyberstride), the economic simulator (CHECO), and the operations room (Opsroom). Beer created a startup-like work culture emphasizing friendship, risk-taking, and independent learning—he gave team members copies of *Jonathan Livingston Seagull* and asked them to sign the cover. He and Flores developed a close working relationship, giving each other nicknames from García Márquez's *One Hundred Years of Solitude*: Flores was "Aureliano" (the revolutionary who survived fourteen assassination attempts), Beer was "Melquíades" (the gypsy who brought news of scientific innovations). The operations room is the chapter's centerpiece. Designed by Gui Bonsiepe, a graduate of the influential Ulm School of Design in Germany, the hexagonal room seated seven people in white fiberglass swivel chairs with orange cushions. Paper was banned; there was no table for writing. Each chair's armrest had ten buttons controlling which economic data appeared on back-projected screens, and the number seven was chosen based on George Miller's famous 1956 paper on human information-processing limits. The room's futuristic appearance, however, concealed enormous hidden labor: every graph and chart displayed on screen was drawn by hand by four female graphic design students from the Catholic University, and the slides had to be updated manually. Medina uses this to argue that the "clean" image of socialist modernity obscured the vast human network required to sustain it. She also notes that the chair lacked a keyboard—explicitly because typing was associated with female clerical labor—revealing how assumptions about gender and class constrained the revolutionary imagination even in the context of socialist transformation.

### **Chapter 5: The October Strike**

The October 1972 truckers' strike was the first major real-world test of Cybersyn's telex network and the book's most important chapter for Chilean political history. When truck owners, shopkeepers, and professionals launched a coordinated strike to destabilize the Allende government, the regime desperately needed a way to maintain distribution of essential goods across a country stretching over 5,000 kilometers. The government expanded the Cybersyn telex network beyond the industrial sector, using it to send and receive messages from Arica in the north to Punta Arenas in the south. Espejo and his team worked late into the night gathering and processing telexes to assemble a near-real-time picture of national production—something the government had never before achieved. The network allowed the presidential palace to connect with factories and distribution centers, coordinate emergency responses, and transmit directives quickly. Medina carefully adjudicates competing claims about the network's significance: Beer later said a senior minister told him "the government would have collapsed that night if it had not had the cybernetic tool," while Espejo took a more moderate view, crediting the network as one important factor alongside popular mobilization from below and the decision to bring the military into the cabinet. The strike also changed how Beer and Flores understood the relationship of technology and politics. Beer proposed shifting from a decentralized, consensus-driven approach to a top-down "method of decision," paralleling Allende's own insistence during the crisis that "it is the government that will give the directives for their actions." Decentralized control began to seem like a luxury rather than a viable strategy.

### **Chapter 6: Cybersyn Goes Public**

In January 1973, before the team's planned simultaneous announcement in Britain and Chile, the London *Observer* scooped them with the headline "Chile Run by Computer," claiming the system had been "secretly brought into operation" and was designed for "Big Brother" surveillance. This mischaracterization proved nearly impossible to correct. The story spread to Toronto, Johannesburg, and the U.S., where the *St. Petersburg Times* added a cartoon of a mainframe computer spying through binoculars on a man in a Mexican sombrero—a racialized caricature reflecting American cold war anxieties about Soviet expansion in Latin America. In Chile, the centrist magazine *Ercilla* broke the story under the headline "Mr. Beer's Big Brother," linking it to Flores's recent and disastrous announcement of a national distribution rationing program for thirty essential goods—a plan the government had no capacity to implement. Medina draws on Latour's insight that technologies need others to adopt and support them, but in that process inventors lose control of interpretation. The Cybersyn team needed public support but could not control how the project was presented once it became public, and their refusal to respond to the negative press allowed the totalitarian reading to gain momentum. The chapter shows that it was "remarkably easy for critics to separate the technology from the beliefs that had guided its creation"—the political values Beer had designed into the system were invisible to outside observers who saw only a centralized computer network in a socialist country.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"This book uses the confluence of these two utopian visions to address a central question in the history of technology: What is the relationship of technology and politics?" (p. 7\)

"THIS system destroys the dogmas of centralization and decentralization alike. This approach is organic." —Stafford Beer, from his first report to the Chilean government (p. 91\)

"At last, el pueblo." —Salvador Allende, upon hearing where he fit in Beer's Viable System Model (p. 101\)

"Although Allende believed that Chile would have a revolution with 'red wine and empanadas,' this assertion failed to account for the actual complexity that the process entailed. In the same way, the clean, futuristic appearance of the control room obscured the vast network of individuals, materials, expertise, and information required to make economic management appear simple." (p. 167\)

"It was remarkably easy for critics to separate the technology from the beliefs that had guided its creation." (p. 217\)

"Technologies are historical texts. When we read them, we are able to read history." (p. 15\)

# **Margaret O'Mara, *The Code: Silicon Valley and the Remaking of America* (2019)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Silicon Valley's rise was neither a pure free-market miracle nor a simple story of government intervention—it was both simultaneously. O'Mara argues that a distinctive ecosystem of massive (but indirect and largely invisible) federal spending, an entrepreneurial university, a new species of venture capitalist, a light regulatory touch, and a tightly networked community of migrants in a geographically isolated California suburb produced the most consequential concentration of technological innovation in modern history. The Valley remade America even as America's broader political, economic, and cultural currents—Cold War militarism, deindustrialization, deregulation, culture wars—remade the Valley.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

O'Mara writes against two dominant origin stories. The first is the libertarian myth, championed by figures from Reagan to the tech founders themselves, that Silicon Valley sprang from garage tinkerers and free markets alone. The second is a left-leaning counternarrative that reduces the Valley to a creature of the military-industrial complex. By insisting on the "both/and" rather than "either/or," O'Mara joins scholars like AnnaLee Saxenian (*Regional Advantage*, 1994\) in treating the Valley as a regional ecosystem, but pushes the analysis much further into national political history—connecting venture capital to LBJ's Small Business Investment Act, deregulation to Gingrich's Republican revolution, and Section 230 to the Communications Decency Act. She also updates the story from Lécuyer's *Making Silicon Valley* (which stops in the 1960s) and her own earlier *Cities of Knowledge* (which focused on the university-defense nexus) by carrying the narrative all the way through the rise of Facebook and the 2016 election. The book's biographical, narrative-driven structure—organized around "Arrivals" vignettes of immigrants and migrants—implicitly argues that the history of technology is best understood through the history of people and place, not through the history of ideas or artifacts.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

### **Chapter 1: "Endless Frontier"**

This chapter establishes the deep prehistory of Silicon Valley in Cold War defense spending and Stanford's institutional ambition. O'Mara traces how Vannevar Bush's wartime Office of Scientific Research and Development (OSRD) created the template for federal research outsourcing—competitive grants to university labs rather than government-run facilities—and how Bush's protégé Fred Terman brought that model back to Stanford with world-historical consequences. The chapter maps the extraordinary concentration of defense money: Massachusetts got a third of all OSRD funds, mostly to MIT's Rad Lab; California came second via Berkeley's Manhattan Project connections. Terman saw that the postwar surge would remapping high-tech geography and set about making Stanford the western anchor of the military-industrial complex. O'Mara also recovers less familiar figures: Ann Hardy, an IBM programmer who hit a glass ceiling (Harvard wouldn't take women for the MBA her bosses said she needed), left for the Bay Area and found that federal laboratories, with their wartime legacy of female technical workers, were less hostile to women than corporate America.
