The book draws on Edwards (*The Closed World*) for the Cold War computing context while pushing beyond his discourse-centered analysis toward concrete questions of engineering design and organizational culture.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

**Chapter 1: "White Heat and Cold War: The Origins and Meanings of Packet Switching"**

This is a masterful comparative case study of how the "same" technology—packet switching—was independently invented by Paul Baran at Rand and Donald Davies at Britain's National Physical Laboratory, yet meant entirely different things in each context. Baran, immersed in Rand's Cold War culture, designed packet switching as part of a distributed communications system meant to survive nuclear attack. His system featured extreme redundancy (hundreds of nodes, up to eight links each), cryptography, priority systems for military hierarchy, and nodes deliberately located away from population centers. Davies, working under Harold Wilson's "white heat" agenda to modernize the British economy, saw packet switching as a way to make commercial time-sharing affordable by reducing the cost of data communications. His proposed national network emphasized user-friendliness, fairness of access, and commercial viability—features meaningless in Baran's thermonuclear scenario. Neither man could build his national network: Baran's was killed when the Defense Communications Agency (which lacked digital expertise) was assigned to build it; Davies was hamstrung by the General Post Office's indifference and Mintech's industrial rationalization policy, which forced the cancellation of the Plessey minicomputer his team had chosen. Lawrence Roberts at ARPA learned of both men's work largely through Roger Scantlebury's presentation at a 1967 Gatlinburg symposium—the NPL group convinced Roberts to increase his planned bandwidth from 9.6 to 56 kilobits per second and adopt their packet format. The chapter's central analytical point is that packet switching achieved legitimacy not because of intrinsic technical superiority, but because the ARPANET provided a highly visible, well-funded demonstration. "The success of the ARPANET may have depended on packet switching, but it could equally well be argued that the success of packet switching depended on the ARPANET."

**Chapter 3: "The Most Neglected Element: Users Transform the ARPANET"**

This chapter is the book's most original contribution, documenting how ARPANET users actively reshaped a network whose original purpose—"resource sharing" (running programs on remote computers)—largely failed. The chapter catalogs the obstacles facing early users: setting up a new node cost $55,000–$107,000; implementing the host protocol could take a programmer a year; finding out what resources existed on the network was nearly impossible because host administrators rarely updated the Resource Notebook; and incompatibilities between computers made remote use frustrating. A 1972 consultant's report called the user "probably the most neglected element within the present development atmosphere." Against this backdrop, users improvised: MIT researchers turned their IMP into a local-area network hub (nearly 30% of ARPANET traffic by 1975 was intra-node); Illinois physicists created a "virtual internet" by using Rutherford as a file dropoff point to circumvent European carrier regulations banning ARPA-to-CERN connections; the ANTS and ELF terminal interfaces were built by users dissatisfied with BBN's TIP. Most consequentially, email—not part of Roberts's original blueprint, dismissed by him in 1967 as "not an important motivation"—was created informally by Ray Tomlinson at BBN and became the network's dominant application. ARPA director Stephen Lukasik's aggressive promotion of email illustrates a non-technical driver: other ARPA program managers adopted email not for its communications value but because Lukasik conducted business by email, and being offline meant being cut off from the budget process. The chapter also reveals the limits of user agency: when the Users Interest Working Group (USING) tried to organize collectively in 1973–74 to push for better applications, ARPA program manager Craig Fields shut them down. Individual innovation was tolerated; collective advocacy was not.

**Chapter 4: "From ARPANET to Internet"**

The transition from a single network to an interconnected system of networks was driven not by grand vision but by a practical problem: Robert Kahn needed to connect ARPA's new packet radio network (PRNET) to host computers, and those hosts were on the ARPANET. Since PRNET, ARPANET, and the planned satellite network (SATNET) used incompatible techniques—broadcast vs. point-to-point, different packet sizes, different reliability guarantees—Kahn and Vinton Cerf had to design an architecture that could accommodate heterogeneous networks. Their solution, TCP/IP, embodied two key design principles. First, a "gateway" (router) between networks would handle only basic packet forwarding, leaving complex functions like error recovery to the host computers—an "end-to-end" principle that kept the network simple and made it easy to add new types of networks. Second, TCP/IP was deliberately designed to accommodate any packet switching network, not just ARPA's own, a decision heavily influenced by Cerf's involvement in the International Network Working Group, which brought together researchers from the British NPL network, the French Cyclades project, and various national telecoms. The chapter traces how the military's operational needs shaped the architecture: the Department of Defense needed a system that could integrate Ethernet (for garrison or shipboard use), packet radio (for mobile field operations), ARPANET technology (for fixed installations), and satellite (for global coverage). The chapter also documents the military's growing operational use of the ARPANET in the 1970s and the eventual 1983 split into MILNET (military) and ARPANET (civilian research)—a pivotal moment that made the Internet's later opening to the public politically feasible.

**Chapter 6: "Popularizing the Internet"**

The final chapter traces the Internet's transformation from a research tool to a public medium through a series of institutional handoffs and decentralized growth. The NSF's role is central: CSNET (1981) extended network access beyond ARPA contractors to all computer science departments; the NSFNET backbone (1986–1995) replaced the aging ARPANET and linked regional networks and supercomputer centers across the country. The chapter documents how much of the Internet's explosive growth in the 1980s came not from central planning but from the bottom up, as universities built Ethernet LANs and connected them to the Internet using commercially available TCP/IP software and routers—products that ARPA had deliberately subsidized. The Domain Name System (designed by Paul Mockapetris at USC-ISI) decentralized the naming process, allowing each domain to manage its own names. The chapter culminates with Tim Berners-Lee's invention of the World Wide Web at CERN in 1990–91, which Abbate presents as continuous with the ARPANET tradition of user-driven innovation: Berners-Lee was not acting on any institutional plan but was improvising a tool to meet his own needs, much as Tomlinson had done with email two decades earlier. The ARPANET was formally decommissioned on February 28, 1990, its users transferred to the NSFNET backbone with almost no disruption—a testament to the flexibility of the TCP/IP architecture.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"The Internet is not, therefore, a story of a few heroic inventors; it is a tale of collaboration and conflict among a remarkable variety of players." (p. 3\)

"The network user, new and established, is probably the most neglected element within the present development atmosphere." (p. 83, quoting a 1972 RCA consultant report)

"The success of the ARPANET may have depended on packet switching, but it could equally well be argued that the success of packet switching depended on the ARPANET." (p. 66\)

"Originally, the network was supposed to provide resource sharing.... That's not really what happened." (p. 106, quoting Leonard Kleinrock)

"When the mail was being developed, nobody thought at the beginning it was going to be the smash hit that it was." (p. 109, quoting Frank Heart)

"It wasn't a technical issue; it was a management issue. The way to communicate with me was through electronic mail." (p. 111, quoting Stephen Lukasik)

# **Christopher M. Kelty, *Two Bits: The Cultural Significance of Free Software* (2008)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Free Software is not merely a technical practice but the formation of what Kelty calls a "recursive public"—a collective vitally concerned with maintaining and modifying the technical, legal, and organizational infrastructure through which it comes into being as a public in the first place. The cultural significance of Free Software lies not in any ideology (whether Stallman's freedom or Raymond's pragmatism) but in five concrete, historically specific practices—creating a movement, sharing source code, conceiving openness, writing copyright licenses, and coordinating collaborations—that together constitute a novel form of democratic political action in the context of a broad "reorientation of knowledge and power."

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Kelty writes against two prevailing framings of Free Software. The first treats it as an ideological phenomenon—either a libertarian business methodology or a quasi-socialist ethics of sharing—and the second treats it as a product of grand structural forces (the "network society," the "information economy," globalization). Against both, Kelty insists on the primacy of practice over ideology: Free Software and Open Source share identical practices despite holding diametrically opposed ideologies, which means the real politics operate at the level of what people actually do, not what they say they believe. He is also intervening in debates about the public sphere (drawing on and departing from Habermas and Michael Warner) by arguing that conventional theories of the public treat infrastructure as a settled given rather than as something that publics must actively build and maintain. The concept of "recursive public" is designed to fill that gap. Kelty positions his work as both anthropology (ethnographic fieldwork among geeks, lawyers, and entrepreneurs) and history (archival research into Usenet, Arpanet records, and industry standardization battles stretching back to the late 1950s), drawing on STS thinkers like Latour, Rheinberger, and Kim Fortun's notion of "figuring out."

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

### **Chapter 3: "The Movement" — Forking Free Software, 1997–2000**

This chapter tells the story of how Free Software became recognizable as a single thing in 1998–99, using the Netscape/Mozilla saga as its central case. When Netscape decided to release the source code for its Communicator browser in January 1998, the decision recapitulated all five of Kelty's core practices. Frank Hecker, a sales manager, wrote the internal document "Netscape Source Code as Netscape Product" that made the business case for what developer Jamie Zawinski had long advocated on engineering grounds. But the release went badly: Netscape wrote bespoke licenses (the NPL and MPL) rather than using the GPL, because existing contracts with third parties created legal tangles. The code was too "crufty" for outside contributors to work with, and crucially, Netscape never shipped a working browser from the freed source, killing the incentive loop that makes Free Software function. Zawinski resigned publicly in April 1999, calling the release a failure—though he insisted the failure was Netscape's, not Free Software's.

Simultaneously, Eric Raymond's "Cathedral and the Bazaar" paper and Tim O'Reilly's "Freeware Summit" triggered the renaming of Free Software as "Open Source"—a deliberate attempt to make the practice palatable to business during the dotcom boom. Raymond rejected Stallman's rhetoric of freedom in favor of a pragmatic, market-oriented framing. The two camps then proceeded to argue fiercely about ideology while doing exactly the same thing in practice. This is Kelty's key observation: "If two radically opposed ideologies can support people engaged in identical practices, then it seems obvious that the real space of politics and contestation is at the level of these practices and their emergence." The VA Linux IPO (700% first-day gains) and the Forbes cover story ("Peace, Love and Software") marked the peak of this moment, but none of the buzz changed how Free Software actually got made.

### **Chapter 5: "Conceiving Open Systems" — UNIX Wars and TCP/IP vs. OSI**

Kelty traces the 1980s "open systems" battles to show where the contemporary geek ideal of openness was forged. Everyone in the computer industry wanted "openness," but Kelty identifies a fatal "blind spot": intellectual property. Open systems advocates demanded interoperable, standardized computing, but the intellectual property regime required secrecy and product differentiation, making true standardization structurally impossible.

The UNIX story is the clearest illustration. By the mid-1980s there were over 100 versions of UNIX, all derived from AT\&T's original but deliberately made incompatible to differentiate products. A cascade of competing consortia—POSIX, X/Open, the Open Software Foundation, UNIX International—each tried to establish the standard. Companies joined multiple rival consortia simultaneously. Sun Microsystems branded itself the champion of openness while cutting a deal with AT\&T that terrified the rest of the industry. The "UNIX Wars" ended not with an open-systems victory but with the triumph of Microsoft Windows—a proprietary system that succeeded precisely because the legal structure of intellectual property favored a single strong monopoly over a fragile ecology of "open" competitors.

The parallel networking story provides the counterpoint. The OSI reference model, developed through legitimate international standards bodies (ISO, ITU), was the "official" standard—comprehensive, bureaucratic, and expensive to access. TCP/IP, developed through the military-funded Arpanet, used a radically different process: the Requests for Comments (RFC) system, where standards were freely available over the network itself, implementations served as the mode of achieving consensus, and anyone could participate. TCP/IP won because of availability (free standards), modifiability (working implementations you could copy and improve, especially via BSD 4.2), and serendipity (the World Wide Web was built on TCP/IP, becoming its "killer app"). The TCP/IP victory exemplified the moral-technical order that would become central to Free Software: the proof of a standard was whether it worked when you connected, not whether a bureaucracy approved it.

### **Chapter 6: "Writing Copyright Licenses" — EMACS and the Birth of the GPL**

This chapter reconstructs the controversy that produced the first Free Software license through painstaking archival research in Usenet and Arpanet records. Stallman created EMACS at MIT's AI Lab and distributed it through an informal "EMACS commune": users got the software free but had to send back any improvements. In 1983, James Gosling sold his UNIX port of EMACS (GOSMACS) to a commercial vendor, Unipress. Stallman, furious, began writing GNU EMACS and used a small piece of Gosling's display code, claiming third-hand permission through a contributor named Fen Labalme. This triggered an extended flame war on Usenet's net.emacs list, in which programmers debated copyright, infringement, the meaning of "public domain," and the mechanics of permission with surprising legal sophistication—and surprising ignorance.

Kelty situates this in the context of the 1976 Copyright Act and the 1980 software amendment, which had made all software automatically copyrighted upon creation. The participants were operating in a period when the shift from trade-secret to copyright protection was still poorly understood, when the copyrightability of source code versus object code was unresolved, and when the meaning of infringement in software was entirely untested. Stallman resolved the crisis by rewriting the disputed code in a single week, releasing "100% Gosling-free" EMACS version 16 on July 5, 1985\. But the experience taught him that informal communal norms were insufficient. He needed a legal instrument. Over the next several years, he iterated through several versions of distribution notices before arriving at GPL 1.0 in 1989—a document that "hacked" copyright law by using the strong rights it granted authors to immediately abrogate those rights, granting all subsequent users the freedom to copy, modify, and redistribute, with the restriction that they must offer the same terms downstream.

Kelty insists the GPL was not the "expression" of a preexisting hacker ethic, as if ethics were genotype and license phenotype. It was instead "figured out" through a messy, historically contingent controversy in a rapidly changing legal landscape. The hacker ethic did not produce the GPL; the GPL helped produce the hacker ethic.

### **Chapter 8: "If We Succeed, We Will Disappear" — Creative Commons and Connexions**

This chapter tracks what happens when Free Software's practices are "modulated" into domains beyond software. Kelty was himself a participant-observer in both projects. Connexions, started at Rice University by engineer Rich Baraniuk, attempted to create an open repository of educational textbook "modules" using Free Software principles. It emerged not from education or pedagogy but from Baraniuk's encounter with Linux and Open Source around 1998–99. Creative Commons, founded by James Boyle, Larry Lessig, Hal Abelson, and others, attempted to create Free Software-like licenses for cultural production generally, motivated by the legal scholar's recognition that the 1976 Copyright Act's elimination of the registration requirement had effectively made it impossible to intentionally place work in the public domain.

Kelty uses these two cases to ask: "Are these projects still Free Software?" and "Are these projects still recursive publics?" The distinction matters. Many "Web 2.0" projects (Flickr, YouTube, MySpace) used Free Software tools but were not themselves recursive publics, because they did not allow users to modify the infrastructure itself—they wanted information to be free but not the systems that made it available. By contrast, Connexions and Creative Commons maintained the "recursive" commitment: Baraniuk's mantra was "If we succeed, we will disappear," meaning the goal was not to become a famous publisher but a publishing infrastructure so open that any competitor could use it.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"If two radically opposed ideologies can support people engaged in identical practices, then it seems obvious that the real space of politics and contestation is at the level of these practices and their emergence." (Ch. 3, conclusion)

"A recursive public is a public that is vitally concerned with the material and practical maintenance and modification of the technical, legal, practical, and conceptual means of its own existence as a public." (Introduction)

"Source code and copyright licenses, revision control and mailing lists are the pamphlets, coffeehouses, and salons of the twenty-first century." (Introduction)

"The blind spot of open systems—intellectual property—at the heart of these failures also provided the impetus for some geeks, entrepreneurs, and lawyers to start figuring out the legal and economic aspects of Free Software." (Ch. 5, conclusion)

"The hacker ethic does not descend from the heights of philosophy like the categorical imperative—hackers have no Kant, nor do they want one. Rather... the philosophy of Free Software is the fact of Free Software itself, its practices and its things." (Ch. 6\)

"We are drowning in the why, both popular and scholarly, but starving for the how." (Preface)

# **Hafner & Lyon, *Where Wizards Stay Up Late: The Origins of the Internet* (1996)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

The ARPANET—the direct precursor to the Internet—was not built to survive nuclear war but emerged from a small community of computer scientists funded by the Pentagon's Advanced Research Projects Agency who wanted to share computing resources across distances. The network's creation was driven by the vision of human-computer symbiosis articulated by J.C.R. Licklider and operationalized by a handful of program managers (Bob Taylor, Larry Roberts) and engineers (Frank Heart's team at BBN), whose informal, collaborative working style and willingness to improvise proved as important as any single technical breakthrough.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Hafner and Lyon write explicitly against the popular myth—propagated by *Time* magazine and others—that the ARPANET was designed as a nuclear-survivable military communications system. This myth conflated Paul Baran's RAND work on survivable networks (which was never built) with the ARPA project, which had entirely civilian-academic motivations: resource-sharing and interactive computing. The book intervenes in the history of computing by foregrounding the social and institutional context of technical invention: ARPA's unusual bureaucratic structure (tiny staff, enormous autonomy, minimal oversight), the personal networks linking Lincoln Lab, MIT, BBN, and UCLA, and the culture of trust between government funders and academic researchers that made the project possible. It also challenges "great man" narratives by showing invention as deeply collaborative—Paul Baran, Donald Davies, and Larry Roberts arrived at packet-switching independently, and the ARPANET's design accumulated contributions from Clark, Kleinrock, Kahn, Cerf, Crocker, Tomlinson, and dozens of graduate students. The epilogue's account of the 1994 reunion, with its jockeying for credit, makes this point with understated irony.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

### **Chapter 1: "The Fastest Million Dollars"**

This chapter establishes the institutional conditions that made the ARPANET possible. Bob Taylor, IPTO director, sits in his Pentagon office in 1966 with three incompatible computer terminals connected to three different mainframes—MIT, Berkeley, and Santa Monica—each requiring different log-in procedures and commands. This annoyance crystallizes the resource-sharing problem. Taylor walks down the hall to ARPA director Charles Herzfeld and pitches a network to connect ARPA's scattered computing sites. Herzfeld approves roughly a million dollars in about twenty minutes—no formal review, no congressional approval needed. The chapter traces this extraordinary autonomy back to ARPA's founding in the Sputnik panic of 1957, when Eisenhower created the agency specifically to cut through interservice rivalry over R\&D. Hafner and Lyon detail how soap executive Neil McElroy, Eisenhower's new Secretary of Defense, modeled ARPA on Procter & Gamble's "blue-sky" research lab, where scientists pursued open-ended inquiry without pressure to justify each project. The chapter establishes the key figure of J.C.R. Licklider, whose 1960 paper on "man-computer symbiosis" and subsequent tenure as IPTO director (1962–64) seeded the entire community of interactive-computing researchers that Taylor would later try to connect. Licklider's genius, the authors argue, was less technical than evangelical—he traveled to each research site, planted the vision of interactive computing, and assembled a community of believers.

### **Chapter 2: "A Block Here, Some Stones There"**

The technical heart of the book. Hafner and Lyon narrate three independent inventions of packet-switching: Paul Baran at RAND (motivated by Cold War survivability concerns), Donald Davies at Britain's National Physical Laboratory (motivated by making interactive computing efficient over distances), and Larry Roberts at ARPA (who learned of both men's work only after beginning his own design). Baran's story is the most dramatic: working at RAND from 1960–65, he conceived of distributed networks with redundant connections (showing that a redundancy level of just 3 or 4 made a network extraordinarily survivable), and of breaking messages into "message blocks" routed independently through the network via "hot potato routing." His analogy was moving a house cross-country by disassembling it onto trucks that each take different routes. AT\&T officials dismissed the scheme as lunatic—they ran a ninety-four-speaker seminar to educate Baran on telephony, after which they asked if he now understood why it couldn't work. "No," he said. When the Air Force agreed to build Baran's network but handed it to the incompetent Defense Communications Agency, Baran himself told the Pentagon to kill the project rather than waste money on a botched implementation. Meanwhile Davies, independently arriving at virtually identical technical specifications (same packet size, same data rate), coined the word "packet" after consulting linguists about international cognates. The chapter culminates in the critical 1967 Ann Arbor meeting where ARPA's principal investigators resisted the network idea—until Wes Clark passed Roberts a note saying "You've got the network inside out" and proposed the subnet of small identical computers (IMPs) that would handle all routing, sparing the host machines. As Baran reflects: "The process of technological development is like building a cathedral... each lays down a block on top of the old foundations."

### **Chapter 3: "The Third University"**

An institutional biography of Bolt Beranek and Newman and the team Frank Heart assembled to build the IMPs. BBN began as an acoustics consultancy in 1948—its founders designed the UN's acoustics, analyzed the JFK assassination tapes, and later the Watergate tapes. Licklider's arrival in 1957 transformed the firm: he convinced Leo Beranek to spend $25,000 on a computer (an LGP-30) for an "unknown purpose," then attracted figures like Minsky and McCarthy. BBN became known as "the third university" in Cambridge—better than MIT or Harvard for some, because there was no teaching obligation and no tenure anxiety. Heart, a "self-described overprotected Jewish kid from Yonkers" who discovered computers in MIT's first-ever programming course in 1951, was recruited to BBN in 1966\. Heart's team-building philosophy is the chapter's analytical center: he believed individual productivity varied "not by factors of two or three, but by factors of ten or a hundred," kept teams small, avoided egocentric "head cases," and judged people purely by "how many neurons per cubic centimeter their brains contained." The chapter profiles each team member—Crowther (the rock-climber whose inner-loop code used 150 instructions where Roberts expected 1,500), Walden, Cosell (the ace debugger), Ornstein (the hardware perfectionist, son of a virtuoso pianist), Kahn (the theoretician). Their proposal cost BBN over $100,000 and filled 200 pages—more blueprint than outline. Senator Edward Kennedy sent a congratulatory telegram for BBN's "Interfaith Message Processor."

### **Chapter 7: "E-Mail"**

The ARPANET's most consequential unintended use. The chapter opens with Len Kleinrock in 1973 using the network's RSEXEC tool to locate Larry Roberts in Brighton, England, at 3 AM, then asking him via the TALK program to retrieve a forgotten razor—a stunt Kleinrock recognized as "slightly out of bounds" on an official federal research facility. Ray Tomlinson at BBN wrote the first inter-machine e-mail program in 1972 by grafting his file-transfer hack (CPYNET) onto the existing single-machine SNDMSG program. His choice of the @ sign—selected simply because it wouldn't appear in anyone's name—created "an icon for the wired world." The chapter traces how ARPA director Stephen Lukasik became e-mail's earliest power user, lugging a thirty-pound portable terminal onto airplanes, and how Larry Roberts wrote the first mail-manager software (RD) overnight after Lukasik complained about unmanageable volumes. A 1973 study found three-quarters of all ARPANET traffic was e-mail—the network built for resource-sharing had been repurposed for human communication. The chapter's second half chronicles the "header wars"—protracted battles over e-mail standards between Tenex and non-Tenex machines—and the MsgGroup, one of the first electronic mailing lists, which debated these standards for ten years. The Christmas 1973 lockup provides a vivid failure case study: Harvard's IMP developed a fault that broadcast itself as zero hops from every destination, creating a "black hole" that swallowed all network traffic.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"It became obvious that we ought to find a way to connect all these different machines." (Taylor, recalling his three-terminal Pentagon office, p. approx. 12\)

"The process of technological development is like building a cathedral. Over the course of several hundred years new people come along and each lays down a block on top of the old foundations, each saying, 'I built a cathedral.'" (Baran, p. approx. 79\)

"We take the position that it will be difficult to make the system work." (BBN proposal to ARPA, p. approx. 107\)

"Well, you may have got there first, but I got the name." (Davies to Baran on packet-switching, p. approx. 68\)

"After I heard the melodic refrain of 'bullshit' often enough, I was motivated to go away and write a series of detailed memoranda papers." (Baran on AT\&T's resistance, p. approx. 58\)

"E-mail was to the ARPANET what the Louisiana Purchase was to the young United States." (Hafner and Lyon, Ch. 7\)

# **Michael S. Mahoney, "The History of Computing in the History of Technology" (1988)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Mahoney argues that the history of computing has developed largely in isolation from the history of technology, dominated by "insider" accounts focused on hardware origins, pioneers, and firsts. To mature as a field, it must engage the analytical questions that historians of technology have been asking—about systems, labor, demand creation, professionalization, and the science-technology relationship—while computing's unique features can in turn test and refine those very frameworks.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Writing in 1988, Mahoney is responding to a history of computing that was almost entirely produced by participants and journalists rather than professional historians. He finds this literature fixated on invention narratives and heroic biography, with almost no engagement with the "big questions" posed by scholars like George Daniels, Nathan Rosenberg, Thomas Hughes, Edwin Layton, and Eugene Ferguson. Mahoney proposes that computing be understood through the lens of systems theory (following Hughes), incremental innovation (following Rosenberg), the social organization of work (following Daniels and Drucker), and professionalization—while insisting that computing's peculiar tripartite structure (hardware, theory, software) poses these standard questions in genuinely new forms. He is also implicitly arguing against treating the computer as historically unprecedented, insisting instead that its revolutionary character can only be understood by locating it within longer technological trajectories.

## **3\. SECTION-BY-SECTION ANALYSIS**

### **"Computing's Present History"**

Mahoney catalogs four kinds of existing literature and finds each wanting. "Insider" accounts by practitioners (conference proceedings, seminal paper compilations, corporate histories like IBM's *Early Computers*) are expert but presentist—their authors treat what were once genuine choices as inevitable technical givens. Journalistic accounts are vivid but lack perspective, gravitating toward the spectacular (microcomputers, AI). "Social impact statements" use history instrumentally to support polemical theses about computers and society. Professional historical work (Stern, Ceruzzi, Williams) is rigorous but stops precisely where computing becomes socially significant—at the boundary of the postwar period. Mahoney's image is striking: historians "pace on the edge" of a "trackless jungle," unable to find a way in.

### **"The Tripartite Nature of Computing"**

This is the article's most original conceptual contribution. Mahoney argues that the computer is not one thing but an amalgam of three historically distinct strands: hardware (the line from Pascal's calculator to ENIAC), mathematical theory (from Leibniz's combinatorics to Turing's abstract machines), and software (the craft of programming that emerged only when computers left research labs for business use in the 1950s). These strands belong to different histories—technology, mathematics, and something closer to industrial production—and they came together only in the 1940s with von Neumann's collaboration with the ENIAC team. Crucially, they never fully merged. This tripartite structure maps onto three distinct disciplines (electrical engineering, computer science, software engineering), each with different intellectual cultures, institutional homes, and relationships to theory and practice. The middle strand—software—is the most analytically interesting because it remains a craft, "technical rather than technological, mathematical only in appearance."

### **"History of Computing as History of Technology"**

Mahoney works through several of the "big questions" from history of technology and shows how each could be productively asked of computing. On innovation patterns, he invokes Rosenberg's argument that technological improvement enters the economy through "numerous and less visible side and rear entrances" and asks whether computing advanced more by radical breakthroughs or incremental refinement—a question that requires looking beyond "firsts." On demand creation, he notes that IBM and its competitors taught businesses how to use computers much as Ford taught the nation to use automobiles, and he highlights the alliance between computing and operations research in the early 1950s, when the *Harvard Business Review* featured both as untested but symbolically powerful management tools. The business community's adoption of these tools, he argues, preceded any rational cost-benefit justification. On government's role, he identifies four areas where state funding was decisive: microelectronics, interactive real-time systems, AI, and software engineering—noting that DARPA funding bypassed peer review and allowed AI to develop independently from the discipline's own internal agenda.

### **"Conclusion: The Real Computer Revolution"**

Mahoney closes with the analogy between the computer and the automobile, noting that different actors within the computing world invoke this comparison for different purposes—Apple and Atari to sell mass consumption, software engineers to justify industrial production methods. He argues that the meaningful comparison is not between the machines themselves but between the societies they transformed: the relevant historical benchmark is not the Model T but *Middletown*, the Lynds' 1929 study of how cars reshaped an entire community's work, leisure, and social relations. Where, Mahoney asks, is the Middletown of the computer age? The article's final move insists that "revolution" is an inherently historical concept, and that computing's truly revolutionary dimensions will only become visible when it acquires a proper history embedded in the history of technology.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"The computer is not one thing, but many different things, and the same holds true of computing. There is about both terms a deceptive singularity to which we fall victim when, as is now common, we prematurely unite its multiple historical sources into a single stream." (p. 116\)

"Between the mathematics that makes the device theoretically possible and the electronics that makes it practically feasible lies the programming that makes it intellectually, economically, and socially useful. Unlike the extremes, the middle remains a craft, technical rather than technological, mathematical only in appearance." (p. 117\)

"By and large, historians of computing are addressing few of the questions that historians of technology are now asking." (p. 114\)

"It seems crucial for the development of the computer industry that the business community accepted the joint claims of OR and the computer long before either could validate them by, say, cost-benefit analysis." (p. 119\)

"What is truly revolutionary about the computer will become clear only when computing acquires a proper history, one that ties it to other technologies and thus uncovers the precedents that make its innovations significant." (p. 123\)

"An activity's choice of historical models is itself part of the history of the activity." (p. 122\)

# **Jennifer S. Light, "When Computers Were Women" (1999)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Light argues that the nearly two hundred women who worked as human "computers" and the six women who became ENIAC's first programmers have been systematically rendered invisible in the history of computing—not by accident, but through an "idiom of sex-typing" that classified their sophisticated mathematical and technical work as merely clerical. The omission of these women from historical accounts perpetuates misconceptions about women's capabilities in computing and distorts the history of technological development itself.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Light intervenes directly against the hardware-centric, male-centered narrative of computing history exemplified by accounts focused on Eckert and Mauchly. She draws on Ruth Milkman's concept of an "idiom of sex-typing"—the idea that wartime rationales for why women performed certain jobs contradicted the actual sexual division of labor—and extends it into computing history. She also builds on Margaret Rossiter's work on "women's work" in the sciences and the "Matilda effect" (whereby credit accrues to those atop professional hierarchies while subordinates vanish from the record). Against historians like Herman Goldstine, whose own book mentioned only three married women instructors and framed computing as "subhuman" labor ripe for mechanization, Light uses archival records from the University of Pennsylvania and the Goldstine Papers to recover what the women actually did and how their contributions were actively obscured by War Department press releases, photograph cropping, and occupational classification systems.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

### **Women's Ambiguous Entry into Computing**

Light traces how ballistics computing shifted from a male occupation in World War I to a feminized one by World War II. A 1942 NACA memorandum captures the logic: engineers felt their training was "wasted and thwarted by mere repetitive calculation," so women were hired to free them. Yet these women held college degrees in mathematics—the same qualification as the engineers. The key mechanism was not substitution (women doing men's jobs) but redefinition: new job categories like "engineering computer" were created specifically for the female workforce, classified as subprofessional regardless of the actual skill involved. Light shows how this pattern extended to the new occupation of "operator" (programmer): computing was already a female job, women already operated business machines, so when six women were selected in July 1945 to program ENIAC, the gendering seemed natural. The fundamental hardware/software distinction mapped onto a gender hierarchy from the start.

### **Female Computers and ENIAC Girls**

This is the empirical core of the article. BRL recruited approximately two hundred women as human computers, hand-calculating firing tables at the Moore School and Aberdeen Proving Ground. Light reconstructs the experience of Kathleen McNulty, who graduated from Chestnut Hill College in 1942 with a math degree and answered a newspaper ad for mathematicians. McNulty's own testimony reveals the complexity of the work: tracking a bullet's path every tenth of a second, accounting for gravity, air pressure, temperature, and the wobble near the speed of sound, checking computations via Simpson's rule. The six women chosen to program ENIAC—McNulty, Frances Bilas, Betty Jean Jennings, Ruth Lichterman, Elizabeth Snyder, and Marlyn Wescoff—were handed a stack of blueprints and told to figure out how the machine worked. They learned ENIAC's circuitry by crawling inside its massive frame, locating burnt-out vacuum tubes and shorted connections. Jennings later recalled that because they knew both the application and the machine, they could diagnose hardware troubles "as well as, if not better than, the engineer." When Los Alamos physicists Frankel and Metropolis arrived to run calculations for the Super (thermonuclear weapon), the women programmed their problem into an untested machine using one million IBM punch cards, working around ENIAC's limited memory by printing intermediate results and repunching new cards.

### **Contemporary Accounts of ENIAC**

Light performs a close reading of War Department press releases, newspaper coverage, and archival photographs to show how women were actively erased from the public story. Press releases named only Eckert, Mauchly, and Goldstine as "primarily responsible." The New York Times described ENIAC solving in fifteen seconds what "would have kept busy 100 trained men for a whole year"—using "men" even though the computers were almost exclusively women. The iconic NYT photograph foregrounds a man in uniform plugging wires; two women are barely visible in the background. When this same image was repurposed as an army recruitment ad in Popular Science Monthly, the women were cropped out entirely. Light also recovers the unpublished photo captions from Goldstine's papers, which do name the women but describe their work in diminished terms—"setting switches," "plugging cables," "standing at function tables"—while the men alongside them are identified as "maintenance engineers." Adele Goldstine, who wrote the only complete ENIAC operating manual and trained both the operators and visiting Los Alamos physicists, received no media coverage despite her leadership role.

### **Postwar Displacement**

Light traces the rapid reversal of wartime gains. By 1946, the National Bureau of Standards had filled most computing vacancies with male veterans. The FBI dropped its wartime female cryptographers. A 1948 Women's Bureau Bulletin acknowledged that "as women leave, men will be hired to replace them." Yet Light complicates the standard narrative of total retreat: Fran Bilas, Kay McNulty, and Betty Snyder continued with ENIAC at BRL; Betty Snyder Holberton went on to program UNIVAC and write the first major automatic programming routine; the Electronic Computer Project at Princeton's IAS employed mostly female programmers through the early 1950s. The postwar story was not simply one of women disappearing but of their contributions being forgotten even as some women stayed.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"The omission of women from the history of computer science perpetuates misconceptions of women as uninterested or incapable in the field." (p. 455\)

"It is felt that enough greater return is obtained by freeing the engineers from calculating detail to overcome any increased expenses in the computers' salaries. The engineers admit themselves that the girl computers do the work more rapidly and accurately than they would." (p. 460, quoting 1942 NACA memorandum)

"Since we knew both the application and the machine, we learned to diagnose troubles as well as, if not better than, the engineer." (p. 471, quoting Betty Jean Jennings)

"Gender plays a role in defining which activities can readily be labeled 'technological.'" (p. 471, quoting Nina Lerman)

"The unmentioned computer technicians are reminiscent of Robert Boyle's 'host of laborants, operators, assistants and chemical servants' whom Steven Shapin described as 'invisible actors.' Working three centuries earlier, their fate was the same: they 'made the machines work, but they could not make knowledge.'" (p. 474\)

"Women can be trained to do any job you've got—but remember 'a woman is not a man'; A woman is a substitute—like plastic instead of metal." (p. 482, quoting 1943 War Department guide)

# Week 10 — AI

# **Jonnie Penn, *Inventing Intelligence: On the History of Complex Information Processing and Artificial Intelligence in the United States in the Mid-Twentieth Century* (PhD dissertation, University of Cambridge, 2020\)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Penn argues that artificial intelligence was a "science of industry" from its inception, not the purely academic venture depicted in canonical origin stories. The four men most credited with founding AI—Herbert Simon, Frank Rosenblatt, John McCarthy, and Marvin Minsky—each borrowed from existing social science traditions (administrative theory, Hayekian economics, programming pedagogy, cybernetics) to model cognition, and their shared project amounted to a singular effort to "reduce epistemology to code." The standard historiographic dichotomy between symbolic AI and neural networks obscures this deeper continuity, as well as the Cold War military and industrial patronage that made the field's rapid professionalization possible.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Penn directly challenges the "discontinuity narrative" that structures most AI history around a sequence of ruptures: the 1956 Dartmouth workshop as genesis moment, the rivalry between symbolic and sub-symbolic camps, and periodic funding "winters." He argues these episodes have been treated as if they occurred in a vacuum, detached from the structural conditions—military funding, industrial computing infrastructure, the maturation of programming languages—that actually enabled AI's ascent. Against scholars like Howard Gardner (*The Mind's New Science*) who accept the symbolic/sub-symbolic split as paradigmatic, Penn groups Rosenblatt with Simon, McCarthy, and Minsky rather than opposing him to them. He positions his work within Joel Isaac and David Engerman's framework for Cold War social science historiography, arguing that AI and machine learning should be understood as offshoots of postwar American social science—a connection that existing compendiums have largely ignored. Penn draws on Stephanie Dick's work on the materiality of computing and Hunter Heyck's studies of Simon to build his account, while extending both by foregrounding how automatic programming techniques (developed largely by industrial actors like IBM) lowered barriers to entry and lent an "aura of possibility" to claims about machine thought.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

**Chapter Two: On the Logic Behind the Logic Theory Machine (Simon, Newell, Shaw)**

This chapter traces the Logic Theory Machine—routinely called "The First AI Program"—back to its actual intellectual sources. Penn shows that Simon's training at the University of Chicago in the 1930s–40s under Rudolf Carnap shaped his positivist conviction that all organizational behavior could be reduced to analyzable decision-making processes. His 1947 book *Administrative Behavior* replaced the idealized "Economic Man" of classical economics with "Administrative Man," a bounded rationalist whose thinking could be modeled through concepts like "decision premises" and "means-ends analysis." When Simon joined the RAND Corporation as a consultant in 1952, he and Allen Newell struggled for two years to build a chess-playing program because they lacked a coherent language to translate between human thought and machine operations. The breakthrough came in 1955 when they turned to Russell and Whitehead's *Principia Mathematica* for formal axioms, layering Simon's administrative heuristics on top. The resulting Logic Theory Machine proved 38 of 52 theorems from *Principia*'s second chapter. Crucially, Penn shows this trio described their work as a contribution to *operations research*, not artificial intelligence. The RAND Corporation's Cold War culture of conformity—mandatory security clearances, Primary Mental Abilities testing, Cartesian architecture—provided the institutional conditions under which reducing human problem-solving to rule-following seemed both natural and desirable.

**Chapter Three: On Frank Rosenblatt's Perceptron Theory**

Penn rescues Rosenblatt from the standard narrative that casts him as the anti-AI dissident championing biological fidelity against symbolic abstraction. Drawing on unexamined archival material, Penn traces Rosenblatt's trajectory from his PhD on the limits of statistics in personality science through to his perceptron work at the Cornell Aeronautical Laboratory, funded by the Office of Naval Research and U.S. Air Force. The key revelation is Rosenblatt's debt to Friedrich Hayek. Rosenblatt used Hayek's theory of decentralized market behavior as an analogy for neural processing: brains, like economies, were too complex for centralized management, and their patterns could only be captured statistically rather than deterministically. This was itself an "abstract contrivance"—precisely the sin Rosenblatt accused symbolic AI researchers of committing. Penn argues Rosenblatt's self-positioning as a "loyal opposition" to the AI in-group has been uncritically absorbed by historians. In reality, Rosenblatt shared the same fundamental commitments: faith in digital computing as the right tool, belief that neural dynamics could be made mathematically legible, reliance on military patronage, and the conviction that a code was "latent" in otherwise irreducibly complex neural stimuli, awaiting discovery. His perceptron schematics abstracted the role of the human operator entirely out of existence, much as Simon's framework obscured the labor of the participants who conformed to his organizational logic.

**Chapter Four: Applied Epistemology (John McCarthy)**

Penn reframes McCarthy's coining of "artificial intelligence" less as a disciplinary founding act than as a "wishful mnemonic"—a rhetorical strategy to stake out territory. Shannon refused to include "intelligence" in the title of *Automata Studies* (1956), and von Neumann considered the term premature. Penn's most original contribution here is connecting AI's emergence to the industrial development of automatic coding techniques between 1952–57. IBM invested heavily in FORTRAN and other systems that reduced programming labor by an estimated 80–90%, enrolling new users in computational habits. McCarthy himself did not program a computer until 1955, the year he coined "AI," and he colloquially used the terms "artificial intelligence" and "automatic coding" interchangeably. The chapter tracks McCarthy through the Dartmouth workshop (which Penn treats as far less important than standard accounts suggest), the founding of the MIT AI Group (1958), and the MIT AI Laboratory (1959). McCarthy's grand claim—that "the artificial intelligence problem will settle the main problems of epistemology in a scientific way"—rested on an infrastructure of programming languages, IBM hardware, and industrial pedagogy that he left implicit, as if the viability of programming techniques were self-evident.

**Chapter Five: Contextualizing Minsky's Agenda for AI**

Penn uses previously unexamined material from Minsky's personal archives to trace how Minsky's two 1961 papers—a research agenda and a bibliography, both republished in the bestselling 1963 textbook *Computers and Thought*—consolidated the field. Minsky began his career building SNARC, the first mechanical neural network, at Princeton in 1950, and was initially cautious about neural metaphors. By 1954–56, frustrated by the slow pace of biological neural-network research, he pivoted to "universal" decision procedures and heuristic programming. Penn reads Minsky's landmark "Steps Toward Artificial Intelligence" through what he calls an "archive-as-intelligence lens": Minsky moved freely between archival and cognitive understandings of search and storage, treating the mind as something like an information service or research library. The chapter's most striking finding concerns reception: the U.S. Air Force treated Minsky's and Rosenblatt's competing approaches as modular and combinable rather than mutually exclusive, undermining the rivalry narrative entirely. By the early 1960s, AI appeared in New York Times job advertisements under "Programmers/Analysts," and the MIT AI Lab operated on a budget reportedly in the millions—a transformation from provisional to professional science in under a decade that Penn attributes to alignment with military priorities, the liminal status of brain-model metaphors, and the maturation of programming itself.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"I argue that AI has been a science of industry since its beginnings, in contrast to historical treatments that depict a purely academic venture concerned with the demonstration of intelligence by machines." (p. 11\)

"Once one system of epistemology is programmed and works no other will be taken seriously unless it also leads to intelligent programs. The artificial intelligence problem will settle the main problems of epistemology in a scientific way." — John McCarthy, quoted by Penn (p. 13\)

"In the early 1950s, professional etiquette compelled Minsky to use scare quotes around his early mathematical notions of machine 'learning.' By the late 1950s, these quotes had disappeared." (p. 14\)

"Simon elevated rule following to the status of thinking, claiming that cognition existed outside of its material instantiation. In fact, I argue, he had made a virtue out of his participants' conformity to his creative organizational framework." (p. 204\)

"The men I examine were active agents in this enrolment, proselytizing to other academics about the inevitable need to revisit one's expertise through the lens of computer programming." (p. 14\)

"Cold War intellectual life was no meritocracy. Winners and losers over the long term were not always determined according to intellectual talent." — George Reisch, quoted by Penn (p. 206\)

# **Matthew L. Jones, *Reckoning with Matter: Calculating Machines, Innovation, and Thinking about Thinking from Pascal to Babbage* (2016)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

Jones argues that the two-century history of failed calculating machines—from Pascal in the 1640s through Babbage in the 1840s—reveals the impossibility of cleanly separating mental design from material production, idea from implementation, inventor from artisan. The persistent failure of philosopher-engineers to build working machines stemmed not from insufficient theoretical insight but from their inability to coordinate with skilled craftspeople and to reckon with the stubborn properties of materials. These failures, paradoxically, generated the very philosophical and legal frameworks—intellectual property, Romantic genius, the hylomorphic split between design and execution—that still govern how we think about invention today.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Jones positions the book against two dominant modes of writing the history of technology: the heroic, individualist narrative of inspired genius (the "linear model" where science drives technology) and the deterministic, collectivist account of gradual evolutionary change. Both, he argues, are products of the period he studies rather than adequate descriptions of how things were actually made. The book recovers a third, older understanding—rooted in the classical concept of *techne* and the early modern culture of "emulation"—in which imitation and originality, design and production, individual vision and collective skill were braided together, not opposed. Jones is in direct conversation with scholars of intellectual property (Pamela Long, Carlo Marco Belfanti), with the sociology of technology (Bijker, Pinch, and the SCOT tradition), with histories of artisanal knowledge (Pamela Smith), and with philosophers of skill and tacit knowledge (Harry Collins, Michael Polanyi). He also challenges histories of computing that center logical abstraction (the Turing-Von Neumann lineage) by insisting that the material problem of mechanizing carry—not formal logic—has been the persistent, unglamorous challenge at the heart of digital computation from the seventeenth century to the present.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

**Chapter 1: "Carrying Tens: Pascal, Morland, and the Challenge of Machine Calculation"**

This chapter establishes the book's central technical problem—the mechanization of "carry" in addition—and its central social problem: the relationship between philosophical inventors and skilled artisans. Jones contrasts two seventeenth-century approaches. Samuel Morland, in building his multiplication machine for the Duke of Lennox, hired three of England's finest craftsmen—Pierre Blondeau (a French coin-maker who revolutionized English minting), Henry Sutton (the premier engraver of mathematical instruments), and John Fromantle (a Dutch clockmaker famous for pendulum clocks after Huygens's design). Morland openly credited these artisans by name, itemized their contributions in a remarkable cost breakdown (the machine cost £67 7s. 6d., at a time when a duke's entire household staff earned £889 yearly), and directed buyers to the artisan who could build the machine. Pascal, by contrast, left his artisans nameless and depicted their labor as merely imitative, directing prospective buyers to a professor of mathematics rather than to a craftsman. This contrast reveals two fundamentally different accounts of what invention is and who deserves credit for it. Jones also lays out a careful taxonomy of artisanal knowledge: propositional knowledge of materials gained through experience, sensory discernment, manual dexterity, and knowledge of the social world where other expertise can be found. The chapter demonstrates that Pascal's machines failed to reach the market not because of inadequate theory but because he could not organize a workforce capable of producing standardized machines at affordable prices—a failure of social coordination, not intellect.

**Chapter 3: "Improvement for Profit: Calculating Machines and the Prehistory of Intellectual Property"**

Jones traces how the modern concept of intellectual property emerged, contingently and unevenly, from the early modern system of royal privileges, using the calculating machines of Pascal, Morland, and Leibniz as case studies. Pascal's 1649 privilege was profoundly unusual: it covered not any particular mechanism but the *goal* of automatically performing carries—all possible machines, in any form, in any material, for an unlimited duration. Jones shows this was not a proto-patent revealing the inevitable unfolding of modern IP law. Rather, it was a royal gift to a favored client, made possible by the patron-client debts between Pascal's father and Chancellor Séguier, and shaped by the political context of French absolutism—specifically, the aftermath of the Norman tax revolt (the Nu-Pieds rebellion) that Pascal's father had been sent to suppress. The privilege accepted Pascal's polemical claim that artisans could only produce "monsters" without the guiding "total comprehension" of the philosophical inventor—a normative hierarchy of mind over hand that did not reflect how things were actually made. Jones argues that the "intellectualization" of patents emerged from the clash of jurisdictions within absolutist states: between economies of glory (international philosophical reputation) and economies of money (local manufacture); between royal prerogative and the resistance of parliaments, guilds, and local judicial bodies. When artisans defended their own claims to ownership using the language of "authorship," they inadvertently contributed to a framework that would ultimately exclude the full scope of their craft contributions.

**Chapter 5: "Teething Problems: Charles Stanhope and the Coordination of Technical Knowledge from Geneva to Kent"**

This chapter is Jones's most detailed account of the actual process of designing and building a calculating machine, using Stanhope's extraordinary surviving workshop documents—sketches, notes to workmen, annotated drawings, patterns for iron casting. Stanhope, educated in Geneva in the 1760s-70s, was formed within a distinctive culture that sought to bridge propositional knowledge (mathematical theory of gear shapes) and artisanal know-how (actually cutting gears). His teacher, the natural philosopher Georges Le Sage, lectured on mechanics to clockmakers; the Genevan Society of Arts (founded shortly after Stanhope left) institutionalized collaboration between savants and skilled makers, promoting "emulation"—improvement through competitive imitation—as the engine of progress. Jones shows how Stanhope's workshop notes reveal a design process that was irreducibly iterative: form in the designer's mind did not simply get impressed on matter. Instead, design emerged through "a nonlinear process of sketching, mocking up, tearing apart, reusing, redrawing, and modifying." The chapter's centerpiece is Stanhope's "horny piece"—a helical gear that solved the problem of secondary carries—whose flat teeth could not be fully specified on paper but required collaborative refinement in the workshop. Jones argues this chapter chronicles "the abandonment of a vision of how philosophers and elite artisans might better coordinate and collaborate to make new things" in favor of a more familiar segregation of mind from hand, design from execution.

**Chapter 6: "Calculating Machines, Creativity, and Humility from Leibniz to Turing"**

The final substantive chapter traces debates about whether machines can think from the seventeenth century to Turing's 1950 paper. Jones's key finding is surprising: despite the proliferation of materialist philosophy in the eighteenth century, calculating machines were almost never invoked in debates about thinking matter. This was not because the connection was unthinkable but because (a) formal logic had few advocates in the Enlightenment—reasoning was understood as the grasping of ideas, not the manipulation of symbols; (b) eighteenth-century materialisms invoked vibrating fibers and sensitive matter, not clockwork; and (c) calculation was considered a low, mechanical activity, so mechanizing it proved little about higher thought. The great exception was Stanhope himself, who around 1800 claimed his logic machine *actually reasoned* and that "Invention, upon whatever subject, is a mere Mechanical Operation." Jones connects this to the broader crisis of creativity in the transition from the rhetorical tradition (where imitation was integral to all making) to Romantic genius (where originality required the ex nihilo production of organic wholes). The chapter links Ada Lovelace's famous objection—that the Analytical Engine "has no pretensions whatever to originate anything"—to Turing's rejoinder that "original work" might be "simply the growth of the seed planted in him by teaching." Jones frames this as the persistence of the book's central tension: whether creativity is combinatorial (drawing on prior work) or generative (producing truly new wholes).

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"Materiality was social: to be undersocialized often led to designs not being materialized." (Introduction)

"Ultimately, *Reckoning with Matter* uses these machines, with their dense accumulations of documentation, as recording instruments that track major contingencies of European early modernity, from its economic history to its visions of creative activity itself." (Introduction)

"In such a conception of making, which Tim Ingold calls 'hylomorphic,' ideas precede materialization, as form precedes matter. Such a division of design from production had little reality in the world of actually making artifacts." (Introduction)

"These conceptions of creativity and making are often more incisive—and more honest—than those still dominating our own legal, political, and aesthetic culture." (Introduction)

"Realizing a more just sociotechnical order means reckoning with matter." (Epilogue)

"Purely 'rational' design remains a goal, not a reality." (Epilogue)

# **Diana E. Forsythe, *Studying Those Who Study Us: An Anthropologist in the World of Artificial Intelligence* (2001)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

AI researchers and knowledge engineers operate within a culture they do not recognize as such—a set of taken-for-granted assumptions about knowledge, work, and expertise that systematically shapes the systems they build. Their restricted, formalized understanding of knowledge leads them to "delete the social" (a phrase Forsythe borrows from Leigh Star), producing systems that are narrow, brittle, and prone to "falling off the knowledge cliff." The very failures of expert systems—and the profession's tendency to blame users rather than design processes—are cultural artifacts of the AI community's unexamined commitments.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Forsythe positions herself within a "second wave" of ethnographic science studies, distinct from the earlier laboratory studies of Latour and Woolgar, Knorr-Cetina, and Lynch. Where those works focused on the philosophical problem of how scientific facts are socially constructed, Forsythe is interested in *culture and power*: what assumptions do scientists carry invisibly into their practice, and what are the consequences for the technologies they build and the people who use them? She draws on Laura Nader's concept of "studying up" and extends Sharon Traweek's community-centered (rather than single-lab) approach to the ethnography of science. She is also intervening in the AI community itself, arguing against its self-understanding as a purely technical, culture-free enterprise. More broadly, the book is an early and influential contribution to the anthropology of computing—a field Forsythe helped found—and anticipates later work on algorithmic bias, the politics of classification, and feminist critiques of tech culture.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

### **Chapter 1: "Blaming the User in Medical Informatics"**

This chapter examines why medical expert systems, after two decades of development, had almost universally failed to be adopted. The American College of Medical Informatics in 1991 identified "the problem of user acceptance" as the field's top challenge; one insider estimated fewer than ten such systems were in routine use anywhere. Forsythe argues that the very name—"user acceptance," or worse, "end-user failure"—reveals the profession's instinct to locate the problem in users (computer-phobia, ignorance) rather than in the design process. She traces this to standard operating procedure in medical informatics: it was "almost unheard of" for a design team to observe real-life problem-solving in the workplace before building a system. Instead, developers formulated problems based on their own assumptions about what physicians needed, built systems accordingly, and then blamed doctors for not using them. The chapter establishes the book's core move: treating what AI professionals regard as technical failures as cultural ones.

### **Chapter 3: "Engineering Knowledge"**

The longest and most theoretically dense essay in the collection, this chapter dissects how knowledge engineers understand the concept of "knowledge" itself. Forsythe finds they divide the world into "expertise" (formal, textbook-style rules that can be encoded) and "common sense" (everything else). Their knowledge elicitation methods—formal interviews with a single expert, structured around extracting explicit rules—reflect and reinforce this division. When asked how they go about eliciting knowledge, her informants looked surprised and said, "We just do it" or "Oh, that's just a matter of common sense." They treated face-to-face knowledge gathering as requiring no special theory, methodology, or training. The result was that tacit knowledge, contextual judgment, and social dimensions of expertise were systematically excluded from the systems they built. Forsythe uses the concept of the "knowledge cliff" to describe what happens when these stripped-down systems encounter real-world complexity: they give absurd answers (the famous case of a diagnostic system suggesting amniocentesis for a male patient). The chapter also raises questions about power—knowledge engineers decide whose knowledge counts, whose interpretation of reality gets encoded, and whose gets excluded—all while believing they are merely building neutral technical tools.

### **Chapter 7: "New Bottles, Old Wine"**

This chapter offers Forsythe's most detailed case study of a single system: a computerized patient education tool for migraine sufferers built in the early 1990s. She traces how the designers' own assumptions about the doctor-patient relationship were silently embedded in the software. The system was supposed to answer patients' questions about migraine, but the questions it could handle, the information it provided, and the way it framed the disease all reflected the physicians' perspective rather than patients' actual concerns. Designers never observed real interactions between migraine patients and their doctors, nor did they ask patients what they wanted to know. The chapter demonstrates Forsythe's argument that intelligent systems are "cultural objects as well as technical ones"—they encode particular beliefs about authority, expertise, and the proper distribution of knowledge between doctor and patient, then render those beliefs invisible by wrapping them in the apparent neutrality of software.

### **Chapter 9: "Studying Those Who Study Us"**

The title essay describes an ironic reversal: having introduced AI researchers to ethnographic methods, anthropologists found their own methods appropriated, stripped down, and used to replace them. By the mid-1990s, medical informaticians with no social science training were conducting and publishing their own "ethnographic" studies—which typically amounted to structured observations or surveys repackaged under the ethnography label. Meanwhile, the NIH institute funding medical informatics had stopped supporting anthropologists and was instead funding physicians and computer scientists to do these "more scientific" versions of social science. Forsythe documents specific misconceptions driving this appropriation: the belief that ethnography is "just common sense," that insiders are automatically qualified to study their own workplace, and that fieldwork means simply "chatting with people and reporting what they say." The chapter is a sharp analysis of how disciplinary power operates—the informaticians could redefine ethnography because they controlled the funding, the publication venues, and the institutional prestige.

## **4\. ESSENTIAL QUOTES**

"The problem of user acceptance is to a significant extent the outcome of values and assumptions that the scientists bring to their own research and development process." (p. 3\)

"Knowledge elicitation is quite a standard science.... Oh, that's just a matter of common sense." (p. 47\)

"Such implicit consequences of taken-for-granted meanings illustrate the cultural nature of scientific practice." (p. 57\)

"When an expert system is built, whose interpretation of reality should it encode? Whose 'knowledge' should constitute the 'knowledge base'; whose practice should be enshrined as 'expertise'?" (p. 57\)

"Science is supposed to be about ideas.... The study of gender issues is for social scientists. The study of computer science issues is for computer scientists." (p. 164, quoting a lab head)

"Rather, my point is that the epistemological stance taken by members of two particular disciplinary communities is not the same, and that such epistemological commitments have consequences for the way the members of these communities go about their work." (p. 115, from her reply to Fleck)

# **Stephanie Dick, *After Math: (Re)configuring Minds, Proof, and Computing in the Postwar United States* (PhD Dissertation, Harvard University, 2015\)**

## **1\. CENTRAL ARGUMENT**

What were, for the architects of early theorem-proving programs, mere "implementation concerns" are in fact significant epistemological issues for the history of mathematics. Dick argues that when practitioners translated elements of mathematical proof — symbol systems, inference rules, intuition — into running computer programs between 1955 and 1975, they did not simply reproduce existing mathematics in a new medium. They invented new formal and material tools (programming languages, data structures, punched card encodings, user interfaces) that transformed what proof was, how it worked, and what could be known through it. She calls this process "reformalism": the simultaneous transformation of abstraction and materiality when mathematical objects are translated into computational form.

## **2\. HISTORIOGRAPHIC INTERVENTION**

Dick writes against Jon Agar's influential argument (in "What Difference Did Computers Make?") that early computing was not epistemologically significant because it merely replaced pre-existing paper-based calculation practices. She agrees with Agar's caution against "revolution" talk but insists that even automating existing techniques required the creation of genuinely novel tools and perspectives — visible only if you look at implementation rather than high-level program descriptions. She also pushes back against Latour's claim that a "properly functioning computer" is merely a "complicated intermediary" that transports meaning without transformation, and against Rheinberger's distinction between epistemically productive "experimental systems" and stable, non-surprising "technical things." Dick shows that all three of her properly functioning, fully formalized programs produced unexpected results — a new proof, a new structural discovery, solutions to open problems in unfamiliar domains. The dissertation builds on Donald MacKenzie's sociology of computer proof (*Mechanizing Proof*) but asks a prior question: not how communities negotiated the credibility of computer proofs after they existed, but what happened to the constituent elements of proof — inference, intuition, form — during the implementation process that brought those programs into being. She follows Michael Mahoney's call for histories of computing communities and their software, while arguing that Mahoney underestimates the material constraints that computers imposed on their users.

## **3\. CHAPTER-BY-CHAPTER ANALYSIS**

### **Chapter 1: "Rewriting Principia: Implementing Intelligence" — The Logic Theory Machine (RAND Corporation, mid-1950s)**

Herbert Simon, Allen Newell, and J. Clifford Shaw at the RAND Corporation built the Logic Theory Machine to prove theorems from Whitehead and Russell's *Principia Mathematica* by simulating a human mathematician. Dick traces how their program required a wholesale transformation of the material world of *Principia*. She begins with what she calls "A Mathematical World on Paper" — the early twentieth-century culture in which logical proofs were written in elaborate symbol systems designed for the human eye, collected in bound volumes, and circulated among reading mathematicians. Whitehead and Russell's notational system was built explicitly to capitalize on human visual pattern recognition.

To put this world into the RAND Johnniac mainframe, Newell and Shaw had to invent an entirely new representational system. Logical propositions were translated into "linked list information structures" — a data structure in which elements of a logical expression were stored as nodes in the computer's magnetic drum memory, connected by pointers rather than by spatial proximity on a page. The four canonical inference rules from *Principia* became forty-two variations of eight computer operations for manipulating these linked lists. Simon drew proof trees by hand on paper, working out how a branching "heuristic search" through subproblems could model the way humans select promising paths of deduction rather than exhaustively pursuing all possible inferences. To make these trees executable, Newell and Shaw created IPL (Information Processing Language), one of the first list-processing programming languages — a tool that shaped the future of symbolic computing and artificial intelligence. The Logic Theory Machine proved about fifty theorems from *Principia*'s early chapters and, most celebrated, produced a previously unknown proof of one proposition that both Newell and Simon and Bertrand Russell himself considered "more elegant" than the original.

### **Chapter 2: "Mathematical Objects in Action: Implementing Herbrand's Theorem" — The Program P (IBM and Bell Labs, late 1950s)**

Hao Wang, a Chinese-American logician trained under Willard Quine at Harvard, rejected the Logic Theory Machine's approach wholesale. His famous dismissal — "There is no need to kill a chicken with a butcher's knife. Yet the net impression is that Newell-Shaw-Simon failed even to kill the chicken with their butcher's knife" — opened a fundamental dispute about what automated theorem-proving should be. Wang had no interest in simulating human reasoning. He believed computers and humans were capable of qualitatively different kinds of mathematical work, and that the computer's real contribution lay in executing procedures beyond any person's reach.

Wang turned to Herbrand's Theorem (1930), a foundational result in proof theory establishing that theorems in predicate logic could in principle be proved using tools from the simpler propositional logic. The catch: actually using this procedure would require analyzing astronomical numbers of cases — trillions even for simple problems — making it purely theoretical, an "in principle" insight about the relationship between two branches of logic rather than an actionable method. Wang saw in computers the capacity to make this abstract theorem into a working tool. He developed a "method of sequential tables" or "pattern recognition" technique that exploited structural properties of logical formulas to cut through the combinatorial explosion. These "patterns" existed at scales invisible to human perception and were processed by methods that no person could execute. The Program P proved all 350+ theorems in the first nine chapters of *Principia* in under ten minutes — where the Logic Theory Machine had managed thirty-eight of the first fifty-two. Dick argues that this constituted an epistemological transformation of Herbrand's Theorem itself: it went from being a "meta" insight about the structure of logic to an actionable computational toolkit. Moreover, the program yielded a genuinely surprising discovery — every predicate calculus theorem in *Principia* turned out to belong to the decidable AE (∀∃) subdomain, a previously unknown structural result that Wang called evidence of "our general ignorance of the extensive range of decidable subdomains."

### **Chapter 3: "A New Collaborator: Implementing Intuition and Inference" — AURA (Argonne National Laboratory, 1970s)**

Larry Wos, Steven Winker, Ross Overbeek, and their colleagues at the Argonne National Laboratory rejected both previous approaches.
